              "args": {
                "command": "str_replace",
                "new_str": "## Stage 7 — Crysknife Orchestrator\n\nCrysknife automates the pain points from Stage 6 and scales from 6 to ~15 agents with progressive upgrades. Full spec: `crysknife-design.md`.\n\n### What Crysknife Automates\n\n```\nSTAGE 6 PAIN POINT                    CRYSKNIFE SOLUTION\n────────────────────────────────────   ────────────────────────────────\nYou dispatch work manually             crys sling + @crysknife/sling MCP\nYou don't notice idle agents           crys watch (heartbeat + pane diffing)\nYou merge branches in lazygit          Merger agent with staging branch\nAgents share one working directory     Git worktrees per worker\nYou track status in your head          .crysknife/state.json + crys status\nYou are mayor + monitor + merger       Three roles: mayor, workers, merger\nAgents ignore their assignment         Agent configs with hooks + MCP\nAgents write outside their area        preToolUse hook blocks writes\n```\n\n### How It Works (Summary)\n\nThree roles: mayor (plans/dispatches), workers (code on isolated branches), merger (merges to main via staging). Communication flows through shared files and MCP tool calls, not direct messaging.\n\nkiro-cli native integration: agent configs define prompt, tools, context, hooks, and MCP server per role. `agentSpawn` hook loads task on startup. `preToolUse` hook enforces area boundaries. `stop` hook updates heartbeat. `crys mcp-serve` exposes role-filtered `@crysknife/*` tools.\n\nAdaptive planning: the mayor maintains a living plan.md with tasks grouped by readiness (ready/blocked/discovered/done). No rigid phases.\n\nSee `crysknife-design.md` for: agent config examples, hook scripts, MCP tool definitions, CLI command details, templates, state file format, architecture diagrams, and implementation phases.\n\n### Scaling Limits and Breaking Points\n\nDesigned for 6 agents, stretches to ~15 with upgrades:\n\n```\nAGENTS   WHAT BREAKS                         FIX\n──────   ─────────────────────────────────   ──────────────────────────────\n6        Nothing. Design target.              --\n8-10     You are the bottleneck.              Mayor autonomy, dashboard-first.\n10-12    tmux layout.                         Session groups.\n12-15    JSON state contention.               SQLite upgrade.\n15-20    Area boundaries + single merger.     Soft ownership, second merger.\n20-25    Mayor context + manual oversight.    Split mayor, autonomous patrols.\n25-30    Shared files across worktrees.       Dolt upgrade.\n30+      You've rebuilt Gas Town.             Use Gas Town.\n```\n\nPast 15, the fundamental assumptions (single mayor, single merger, hard area boundaries, you at the keyboard) break. That's by design — by the time you hit 15 agents, you'll know exactly *why* Gas Town needs 7 roles and a database.\n\n### What to Skip at This Stage\n\nGas Town roles not needed with you at the keyboard: Deacon, Dogs, Boot, Wisps, Polecats. Add these only when you need unattended operation (20+ agents).",
                "old_str": "## Stage 7 — Crysknife Orchestrator\n\nCrysknife automates the pain points from Stage 6 and scales from 6 to ~15 agents with progressive upgrades. See `crysknife-design.md` for the full spec.\n\n### What Crysknife Automates\n\n```\nSTAGE 6 PAIN POINT                    CRYSKNIFE SOLUTION\n────────────────────────────────────   ────────────────────────────────\nYou dispatch work manually             crys sling + @crysknife/sling MCP\n                                        tool. Template-based task\n                                        assignment with tiers.\n\nYou don't notice idle agents           crys watch — heartbeat via stop\n                                        hooks (primary), pane diffing\n                                        (fallback). Auto-nudge, auto-restart.\n\nYou merge branches in lazygit          Merger agent with standing orders,\n                                        staging branch (merge/staging),\n                                        tests before fast-forward to main.\n\nAgents share one working directory     Git worktrees per worker, symlinked\n                                        .crysknife/ and .kiro/specs/.\n\nYou track status in your head          .crysknife/state.json — single\n                                        source of truth, crys status.\n\nYou are mayor + monitor + merger       Three roles: mayor agent (plans),\n                                        workers (code), merger (merges).\n\nAgents ignore their assignment         kiro-cli agent configs with hooks.\n                                        agentSpawn loads task on startup.\n                                        preToolUse enforces area boundaries.\n                                        MCP tools for structured interaction.\n\nAgents write outside their area        preToolUse hook blocks fs_write\n                                        outside assigned area. Hard\n                                        enforcement, not just prompting.\n```\n\n### Three Agent Roles\n\n```\n  MAYOR (1 agent)     — plans, dispatches, adapts. You talk to this one.\n  WORKERS (3-4+)      — code on isolated branches in their own worktrees.\n  MERGER (1 agent)    — merges completed branches to main via staging.\n```\n\nCommunication flows through shared files (task files, plan.md, state.json) and MCP tool calls, not direct messaging. Workers write feedback in their task files. The merger writes merge reports. The mayor reads both and adapts the plan.\n\n### kiro-cli Native Integration\n\nCrysknife works *with* kiro-cli instead of fighting it:\n\n```\nKIRO-CLI FEATURE     CRYSKNIFE USE\n──────────────────   ──────────────────────────────────────────────\nAgent configs        Each role defined as .kiro/agents/<role>.json.\n(.kiro/agents/)      Per-worker configs generated by crys start.\n                     Prompt, tools, context, hooks, MCP — all in one file.\n\nHooks                agentSpawn = GUPP (auto-load task on startup)\n                     preToolUse = area enforcement (block writes)\n                     stop = heartbeat (update last_activity)\n\nMCP server           crys mcp-serve exposes @crysknife/* tools.\n                     Mayor calls @crysknife/sling. Workers call\n                     @crysknife/done. Role-based tool filtering.\n\nContext resources    Agent configs auto-load design.md, principles.md,\n                     and the agent's own task file into context.\n\nAuto-compaction      Built into kiro-cli. Workers auto-compact when\n                     context fills. Task file on disk is continuity.\n```\n\n### Adaptive Planning\n\nNo rigid phases. The mayor maintains a living plan.md with tasks grouped by readiness:\n\n```\nReady      — can be assigned now\nBlocked    — waiting on a dependency or product decision\nDiscovered — found during execution, needs prioritization\nDone       — completed and merged\n```\n\n### Key Crysknife Commands\n\n```\ncrys init              — create .crysknife/, templates, hooks, agent configs\ncrys start             — generate worker configs, worktrees, tmux, launch agents\ncrys stop              — shut down agents\ncrys status            — dashboard of all agents, queue, convoys\ncrys sling <agent>     — assign work (picks template by tier, regenerates config)\ncrys queue             — manage work queue\ncrys watch             — monitoring loop (heartbeat + pane diffing, auto-nudge)\ncrys nudge <agent>     — manual poke\ncrys done <worker>     — mark done, notify mayor + merger\ncrys convoy            — feature-level tracking\ncrys heartbeat         — update last_activity (called by stop hooks)\ncrys my-task           — output task summary (called by agentSpawn hooks)\ncrys mcp-serve         — MCP stdio server (configured in agent JSON)\n```\n\n### Implementation Phases\n\n```\nPhase 1: Foundation      — init, start, stop, status, state, tmux, worktrees,\n                           agent config generation, heartbeat, my-task\nPhase 2: Work + MCP      — sling, queue, mcp-serve, MCP tools, templates,\n                           merger standing orders, enforce-area hook\nPhase 3: Monitoring      — watch (heartbeat + pane diffing), nudge,\n                           auto-nudge, auto-restart\nPhase 4: Planning        — convoy, plan file support, dashboard improvements\n```\n\n### Scaling Limits and Breaking Points\n\nCrysknife is designed for 6 agents but can stretch to ~15 with progressive upgrades. Here's what breaks first and what to do about it:\n\n```\nAGENTS   WHAT BREAKS                         FIX\n──────   ─────────────────────────────────   ──────────────────────────────\n6        Nothing. Design target.              --\n\n8-10     YOU are the bottleneck.              Give the mayor more autonomy.\n         Can't cycle through 8+ agents.       Let it sling without asking.\n         Assign-ignore-harvest loop too        crys watch dashboard becomes\n         slow. You lose track.                 your primary interface.\n\n10-12    TMUX LAYOUT.                         Switch from windows to tmux\n         Can't fit 10+ workers in one          session groups. Workers get\n         tmux session. Navigation painful.     own sessions. C-b s to browse.\n\n12-15    JSON STATE.                           Upgrade to SQLite.\n         12+ heartbeats hitting state.json     Atomic writes, concurrent\n         = file contention. crys status        reads, history + audit logs.\n         parsing big JSON gets slow.           crys CLI still sole writer.\n\n15-20    AREA BOUNDARIES.                     Move from hard boundaries to\n         Can't slice codebase into 15          soft ownership + merge\n         non-overlapping areas. Workers        conflict resolution. Merger\n         need shared files (types, configs).   becomes critical. Add second\n         preToolUse blocks legitimate work.    merger.\n\n15-20    SINGLE MERGER.                       Second merger, or smarter\n         Merge queue backs up. Branches        merge strategy (parallel\n         drift from main. Conflicts worsen     merges on independent areas).\n         the longer they wait.                 Workers rebase periodically.\n\n20-25    MAYOR CONTEXT.                       Split the mayor. One mayor\n         Can't hold plan + state for 20        per feature/convoy. Each\n         workers in one context window.        owns a subset of workers.\n         Bad assignments, lost feedback.       You talk to the relevant one.\n\n20-25    MANUAL OVERSIGHT.                    Autonomous patrols. Witness\n         Can't babysit 20 agents even          agent monitors workers.\n         with dashboard. Need unattended       Deacon monitors Witness.\n         operation for hours.                  You're rebuilding Gas Town.\n\n25-30    SHARED FILES.                        Dolt upgrade. Each agent\n         Symlinks across 25+ worktrees =       gets own Dolt branch for\n         filesystem pressure. Concurrent       state. Cell-level merge.\n         writes to plan.md if split mayors.    No file contention.\n\n30+      You've rebuilt Gas Town.             Use Gas Town, or accept\n                                               Crysknife evolved into\n                                               your own version of it.\n```\n\nThe realistic ceiling for Crysknife's current architecture is ~12-15 agents with the SQLite upgrade. Past 15, the fundamental assumptions (single mayor, single merger, hard area boundaries, you at the keyboard) start breaking. Past 20, you're fighting the architecture.\n\nThat's by design. Crysknife teaches you multi-agent orchestration by building it. By the time you hit 15 agents, you'll know exactly *why* Gas Town needs 7 roles and a database.\n\n### What to Skip at This Stage\n\nThese Gas Town roles exist for 30+ autonomous agents running for hours unattended. Not needed with you at the keyboard:\n\n- **Deacon** (heartbeat daemon) — you are the heartbeat\n- **Dogs** (deacon's helpers) — no deacon, no dogs\n- **Boot** (deacon health check) — same reason\n- **Wisps** (ephemeral orchestration beads) — not enough overhead to worry about\n- **Polecats** (ephemeral swarming workers) — workers are persistent, not disposable\n\nAdd these only when you need unattended operation (20+ agents).",

        "content": {

      },
      "assistant": {
        "ToolUse": {
          "message_id": "9a1b025b-d4b8-40bf-925c-7e43236a56e7",
          "content": "Now trim the Context Preservation section — the Crysknife block repeats design doc details:",
          "tool_uses": [
            {
              "id": "tooluse_bDXV50MFZFfamIUN3FVnLd",
              "name": "fs_write",
              "orig_name": "fs_write",
              "args": {
                "command": "str_replace",
                "new_str": "## Context Preservation — How Each Approach Works\n\n```\nKIRO (Stage 5-6):\n  .kiro/specs/ files on disk ARE the memory.\n  Session dies = start fresh, same files.\n\nCRYSKNIFE (Stage 7):\n  Agent configs + task files on disk = continuity.\n  Session dies = crys watch detects, restarts with config.\n  Hooks rebuild full context automatically.\n\nGAS TOWN (Stage 8):\n  Dolt database = persistent agent identity.\n  Session dies = agent resumes via GUPP.\n  Agent identity survives across sessions.\n```",
                "old_str": "## Context Preservation — How Each Approach Works\n\n```\nKIRO (Stage 5-6):\n  .kiro/specs/ files ──> Agent reads on startup\n  Session dies = agent dies\n  New session = new agent, same files\n  No identity persists. Files ARE the memory.\n\nCRYSKNIFE (Stage 7):\n  .kiro/agents/<role>.json ──> kiro-cli loads config on startup\n  Agent config defines: prompt, context files, hooks, MCP server\n  agentSpawn hook loads task assignment into context automatically\n  Session dies = crys watch detects (heartbeat stops), restarts with\n    kiro-cli chat --agent <id>. Config + hooks rebuild full context.\n  preToolUse hooks enforce area boundaries at the tool level.\n  MCP tools (@crysknife/*) for structured state interaction.\n  Git worktrees isolate code. Symlinks share state.\n\nGAS TOWN (Stage 8):\n  .beads/ (Dolt database) ──> Agent reads on startup\n  Session dies = agent LIVES (identity in Beads/Dolt)\n  New session = same agent, resumes work automatically via GUPP\n  Dolt branches per agent. Cell-level merge for state.\n  Files ARE the memory + agent identity persists in database.\n```",
                "path": "/Users/lemi/Documents/code/projects/chip8-emulator/docs/multi-agent-progression.md",
                "summary": "Trim Context Preservation to essentials - remove design doc duplication"
              },
              "orig_args": {
                "command": "str_replace",
                "new_str": "## Context Preservation — How Each Approach Works\n\n```\nKIRO (Stage 5-6):\n  .kiro/specs/ files on disk ARE the memory.\n  Session dies = start fresh, same files.\n\nCRYSKNIFE (Stage 7):\n  Agent configs + task files on disk = continuity.\n  Session dies = crys watch detects, restarts with config.\n  Hooks rebuild full context automatically.\n\nGAS TOWN (Stage 8):\n  Dolt database = persistent agent identity.\n  Session dies = agent resumes via GUPP.\n  Agent identity survives across sessions.\n```",
                "old_str": "## Context Preservation — How Each Approach Works\n\n```\nKIRO (Stage 5-6):\n  .kiro/specs/ files ──> Agent reads on startup\n  Session dies = agent dies\n  New session = new agent, same files\n  No identity persists. Files ARE the memory.\n\nCRYSKNIFE (Stage 7):\n  .kiro/agents/<role>.json ──> kiro-cli loads config on startup\n  Agent config defines: prompt, context files, hooks, MCP server\n  agentSpawn hook loads task assignment into context automatically\n  Session dies = crys watch detects (heartbeat stops), restarts with\n    kiro-cli chat --agent <id>. Config + hooks rebuild full context.\n  preToolUse hooks enforce area boundaries at the tool level.\n  MCP tools (@crysknife/*) for structured state interaction.\n  Git worktrees isolate code. Symlinks share state.\n\nGAS TOWN (Stage 8):\n  .beads/ (Dolt database) ──> Agent reads on startup\n  Session dies = agent LIVES (identity in Beads/Dolt)\n  New session = same agent, resumes work automatically via GUPP\n  Dolt branches per agent. Cell-level merge for state.\n  Files ARE the memory + agent identity persists in database.\n```",
                "path": "/Users/lemi/Documents/code/projects/chip8-emulator/docs/multi-agent-progression.md",
                "summary": "Trim Context Preservation to essentials - remove design doc duplication"
              }
            }
          ]
        }
      },

                "content": [
                  {
                    "Json": {
                      "exit_status": "0",
                      "stdout": "7:## Stage Overview\n19:## Concepts: Gas Town vs Our Workflow\n23:### What We Already Have\n61:### What Gas Town Has That We Don't (Yet)\n77:### The MEOW Stack — Gas Town's Work Layers\n91:### Workflow Tiers — Matching Effort to Task Size\n109:### Patrols — Automated Monitoring Loops (crys watch)\n125:### What We Adopted from Gas Town\n146:## Stage 5 — Where We Started\n150:### tmux Layout\n159:### Context Files\n168:### How It Works\n178:### Limitations\n187:## Stage 6 — Parallel Agents, Manual Coordination\n191:### What Changes from Stage 5\n199:### tmux Layout\n210:### tmux Session Script\n245:### Context Files — Split Tasks Per Agent\n259:### Per-Agent Task File Format\n264:## Context\n270:## Tasks\n275:## Rules\n281:### Workflow Tiers in Practice\n299:### How It Works\n325:### The Crew Cycling Cadence\n351:### Session Hygiene\n357:### What You Learn at This Stage\n371:## Stage 6.5 — Conventions and Tracking Solidified\n375:### Add an Agents Definition File\n380:## agent-1: Frontend\n384:## agent-2: Backend API\n388:## agent-3: Tests and Docs\n393:### Add Feature-Level Tracking (Convoys)\n400:## Active Features (Convoys)\n409:## Done\n412:## Up Next\n417:### Context File Structure at Stage 6.5\n432:### Prevent Heresies with a Principles File\n441:## Core Principles\n447:## Anti-Patterns (DO NOT do these)\n456:### Standing Orders (PR Sheriff Pattern)\n463:## Standing Orders (run on every session startup)\n469:## Latest Report\n472:## Regular Tasks\n478:### Review Sweep / Fix Sweep Workflow\n498:## Stage 7 — Crysknife Orchestrator\n502:### What Crysknife Automates\n517:### How It Works (Summary)\n527:### Scaling Limits and Breaking Points\n546:### What to Skip at This Stage\n552:## Gas Town Role Mapping Across Stages\n571:## Context Preservation — How Each Approach Works\n591:## Timeline\n615:## Key Principles\n",
                      "stderr": ""
                    }
                  }
                ],
                "status": "Success"
              }
            ]
          }
        },

                    "Json": {
                      "exit_status": "0",
                      "stdout": "63:These concepts from Gas Town were adopted into Crysknife (see \"What We Adopted\" below and `crysknife-design.md` for full details):\n",

      "assistant": {
        "Response": {
          "message_id": "bf4ec21c-77c5-409e-8512-1717d4e7b3b2",
          "content": "That reference is valid — \"What We Adopted\" is at line 125, below line 63. All good.\n\nHere's the summary of what changed:\n\n**844 → 635 lines** (trimmed ~25%)\n\nChanges made:\n\n1. **\"What Gas Town Has That We Don't\" (saved ~40 lines)** — Replaced the verbose 3-column table with a concise bullet list. The old table described each concept in detail as if we hadn't adopted them yet. Now it's a quick list pointing to the adoption section and design doc.\n\n2. **MEOW Stack (saved ~15 lines)** — Cut the 5-layer ASCII diagram. Kept just the mapping table. The layer hierarchy is Gas Town internals — you don't need to re-read it every time you open this doc.\n\n3. **Stage 7 (saved ~130 lines)** — This was the biggest win. The old version duplicated the design doc: full kiro-cli integration table, all CLI commands, all implementation phases, the full scaling chart with multi-line descriptions per tier. Now it has:\n   - The pain-point-to-solution table (unique to this doc — maps Stage 6 problems to Crysknife answers)\n   - A 2-paragraph \"How It Works\" summary\n   - A compressed scaling chart (one line per tier instead of 3-4)\n   - A pointer to `crysknife-design.md` for everything else\n\n4. **Context Preservation (saved ~10 lines)** — Compressed from detailed implementation notes to one-line-per-stage essence. The Crysknife details are in the design doc.\n\nWhat stayed untouched: Stages 5, 6, 6.5 (your progression story with practical patterns), the \"What We Already Have\" table, workflow tiers, patrols, role mapping, timeline, key principles. All still valuable and not duplicated elsewhere."
        }
      },

        "content": {
          "ToolUseResults": {
            "tool_use_results": [
              {
                "tool_use_id": "tooluse_klIYbQgM2eXDTPOX8n8k5I",
                "content": [
                  {
                    "Text": "# Multi-Agent Development Progression\n\nA personal roadmap for evolving from single-agent development to multi-agent orchestration, inspired by Steve Yegge's Gas Town article and adapted to a tmux + nvim + kiro-cli + lazygit workflow.\n\n---\n\n## Stage Overview\n\n```\nStage 5    Single agent, sequential work\nStage 6    3-5 agents in parallel, manual coordination\nStage 6.5  Conventions, area ownership, workflow tiers\nStage 7    6-15 agents, Crysknife orchestrator (with upgrades)\nStage 8    20+ agents, Gas Town or equivalent\n```\n\n---\n\n## Concepts: Gas Town vs Our Workflow\n\nBefore diving into stages, here's how Gas Town's concepts map to what we already have and what's new to learn from.\n\n### What We Already Have\n\n```\nOUR WORKFLOW                               GAS TOWN EQUIVALENT\n────────────────────────────────────────   ────────────────────────────────\n.kiro/specs/requirements.md                Role Bead (shared context that\n.kiro/specs/design.md                      defines how agents should behave\n                                           and what standards to follow)\n\n.kiro/specs/tasks.md                       Molecule (ordered workflow chain\n  - [ ] Task 1                             with checkboxes = bead status)\n  - [x] Task 2\n\nSPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md                        Formula / Protomolecule\n  (every feature follows the same          (template that generates\n   requirements -> design -> tasks          consistent workflows)\n   structure)\n\nAcceptance criteria traceability           NO EQUIVALENT in Gas Town.\n  AC 1.2 -> Property 3 -> Task 4.1        We're ahead here.\n\ntmux sessions + window switching           tmux sessions (same)\n\nlazygit                                    Merger agent (we do it manually\n                                           now, merger agent at Stage 7)\n\nYou deciding what to work on next          Mayor agent (you are the mayor\n                                           now, mayor agent at Stage 7)\n\nYou checking if things are done            crys watch (you are the monitor\n                                           now, automated at Stage 7)\n\n/compact or new session                    Task files + state.json\n                                           (Gas Town: GUPP + Hooks.\n                                           Ours is file-based, not\n                                           automatic yet)\n```\n\n### What Gas Town Has That We Don't (Yet)\n\nThese concepts from Gas Town were adopted into Crysknife (see \"What We Adopted\" below and `crysknife-design.md` for full details):\n\n- **Workflow tiers** (full/standard/quick) — match effort to task size\n- **Per-agent task files** — split tasks.md so agents don't collide\n- **Convoys** — feature-level tracking across multiple agents\n- **Task files as hooks** — persistent assignment that survives session death\n- **Automated monitoring** (crys watch) — detect idle/dead agents, auto-nudge/restart\n- **Shared files** — agents communicate through task files, plan.md, state.json (not direct messaging)\n- **crys sling / crys nudge** — dispatch and poke commands\n\nWhat we skipped (not needed at our scale):\n- **Wisps** — ephemeral beads, only useful at 20+ agents\n- **gt seance** — session recovery, `/chat save` covers most of this\n\n### The MEOW Stack — Gas Town's Work Layers\n\nGas Town organizes work in 5 layers (Beads → Epics → Molecules → Protomolecules → Formulas). Our equivalents:\n\n```\nMEOW LAYER          OUR EQUIVALENT\n────────────────    ──────────────────────────────────────────\nBeads               Individual acceptance criteria + subtasks\nEpics               A feature's full spec folder\nMolecules           tasks.md (ordered checklist)\nProtomolecules      SPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md templates\nFormulas            The requirements -> design -> tasks flow\n```\n\n### Workflow Tiers — Matching Effort to Task Size\n\nGas Town uses named workflow variants (called \"shiny/chrome\" for full, \"quick\" for minimal). We use three tiers:\n\n```\n\"Full\" tier (our current spec guide):\n  requirements.md -> design.md -> tasks.md (with PBT checkpoints)\n  For: new features, complex changes, architectural work\n\n\"Standard\" tier:\n  brief requirements -> tasks.md (with unit tests, no PBT)\n  For: medium features, refactors, multi-file changes\n\n\"Quick\" tier:\n  just a task description -> implement -> basic test\n  For: bug fixes, small tweaks, docs, one-file changes\n```\n\n### Patrols — Automated Monitoring Loops (crys watch)\n\n`crys watch` is Crysknife's patrol loop. It uses two detection methods:\n\n```\nPRIMARY: Heartbeat timestamps (from stop hooks)\n  - Every agent has a stop hook that runs crys heartbeat after each response\n  - crys watch reads last_activity timestamps from state.json\n  - No heartbeat for 2 min → IDLE (auto-nudge)\n  - No heartbeat for 5 min → DEAD (auto-restart)\n\nFALLBACK: tmux pane content diffing\n  - Capture pane content, hash it, compare to previous\n  - Used when heartbeat hooks fail or aren't configured\n```\n\n### What We Adopted from Gas Town\n\nAll of these are now part of the Crysknife design (see `crysknife-design.md`):\n\n1. **Workflow tiers** (full/standard/quick) — template-based task assignment\n2. **Per-agent task files** — split tasks.md, one per worker\n3. **Convoys** — feature-level tracking via `crys convoy`\n4. **State file** — .crysknife/state.json as single source of truth\n5. **Task files as hooks** — persistent assignment that survives session death\n6. **Automated monitoring** — `crys watch` with heartbeat + pane diffing\n7. **Git worktrees** — per-worker code isolation with symlinked shared files\n8. **Three roles** — mayor (plans), workers (code), merger (merges)\n9. **Staging branch** — merge/staging for safe merges before main\n\nWhat we added that Gas Town doesn't have:\n- **preToolUse area enforcement** — hard block on writes outside assigned area\n- **kiro-cli native integration** — agent configs, hooks, MCP instead of fighting the tool\n- **Traceability** — acceptance criteria chain that Gas Town explicitly skips\n\n---\n\n## Stage 5 — Where We Started\n\nOne kiro-cli session, one task at a time. Work is sequential.\n\n### tmux Layout\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim        (editor)\nWindow 2: kiro-cli    (single agent)\nWindow 3: lazygit     (git management)\nWindow 4: terminal    (builds, tests, misc)\n```\n\n### Context Files\n\n```\n.kiro/specs/\n├── requirements.md    <- what to build\n├── design.md          <- how to build it\n└── tasks.md           <- sequential checklist, one agent works top to bottom\n```\n\n### How It Works\n\n- You switch between tmux windows with `C-b <number>`\n- One agent reads the spec files, works through tasks.md\n- When context fills up, `/compact` summarizes or you start a new session\n- Each new session starts fresh — reloads spec files, no conversational memory\n- State lives in spec files on disk\n- You are the agent's manager, memory, and merge tool\n- All features use the \"full\" workflow tier (SPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md)\n\n### Limitations\n\n- Only one thing happens at a time\n- Agent finishes a task, waits for you to assign the next\n- Big features take many sequential sessions\n- No workflow tiers — a one-line fix gets the same process as a new feature\n\n---\n\n## Stage 6 — Parallel Agents, Manual Coordination\n\nRun 3-5 kiro-cli agents simultaneously, each on its own branch and task list.\n\n### What Changes from Stage 5\n\n- Multiple agents run in PARALLEL (not sequential)\n- tasks.md splits into per-agent task files\n- Each agent on its own branch in its own area\n- Introduce workflow tiers (full/standard/quick)\n- You manually dispatch, monitor, and merge\n\n### tmux Layout\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim         (editor / overview)\nWindow 2: agent-1      (kiro-cli — feature work)\nWindow 3: agent-2      (kiro-cli — second feature or bug fixes)\nWindow 4: agent-3      (kiro-cli — tests / docs / small tasks)\nWindow 5: lazygit      (merge branches, resolve conflicts)\nWindow 6: terminal     (builds, tests, misc)\n```\n\n### tmux Session Script\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\nSESSION=\"chip8\"\nPROJECT=~/Documents/code/projects/chip8-emulator\n\ntmux has-session -t $SESSION 2>/dev/null\n\nif [ $? != 0 ]; then\n  tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION -c $PROJECT\n\n  tmux rename-window -t $SESSION:1 'nvim'\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:1 'nvim .' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:2 -n 'agent-1' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:2 'kiro-cli chat' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:3 -n 'agent-2' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:3 'kiro-cli chat' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:4 -n 'agent-3' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:4 'kiro-cli chat' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:5 -n 'lazygit' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:5 'lazygit' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:6 -n 'terminal' -c $PROJECT\n\n  tmux select-window -t $SESSION:1\nfi\n\ntmux attach -t $SESSION\n```\n\n### Context Files — Split Tasks Per Agent\n\n```\n.kiro/specs/\n├── requirements.md        <- shared, read-only for agents\n├── design.md              <- shared, read-only for agents\n└── tasks/                 <- one file PER agent\n    ├── agent-1.md\n    ├── agent-2.md\n    └── agent-3.md\n```\n\nShared spec files guarantee consistent code style across all agents. Per-agent task files prevent agents from stepping on each other.\n\n### Per-Agent Task File Format\n\n```markdown\n# Agent 1 — Auth Module\n\n## Context\nRead: requirements.md, design.md\nWork in: src/auth/, src/middleware/\nBranch: feat/a1-auth\nWorkflow tier: full\n\n## Tasks\n- [ ] Implement JWT token generation\n- [ ] Add refresh token endpoint\n- [ ] Wire up auth middleware\n\n## Rules\n- Follow patterns in design.md\n- Don't touch files outside your area\n- When done, update status to DONE at the top of this file\n```\n\n### Workflow Tiers in Practice\n\nAt Stage 6, you start matching effort to task size:\n\n```\n\"Full\" tier agent (agent-1):\n  Has: requirements.md, design.md, tasks/agent-1.md with PBT checkpoints\n  For: the main feature being built\n\n\"Standard\" tier agent (agent-2):\n  Has: brief requirements in task file, tasks with unit tests\n  For: medium refactors, secondary features\n\n\"Quick\" tier agent (agent-3):\n  Has: just task descriptions in task file\n  For: bug fixes, docs, small tweaks\n```\n\n### How It Works\n\n```\n  requirements.md ──────┐\n  design.md ─────────┐  │\n                     │  │  (shared context = consistent code)\n          ┌──────────┤  ├──────────┐\n          v          v  v          v\n    ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐\n    │ agent-1  │ │ agent-2  │ │ agent-3  │\n    │ \"full\"   │ │\"standard\"│ │ \"quick\"  │\n    │ tier     │ │ tier     │ │ tier     │\n    │ reads:   │ │ reads:   │ │ reads:   │\n    │ design + │ │ design + │ │ design + │\n    │ its own  │ │ its own  │ │ its own  │\n    │ tasks    │ │ tasks    │ │ tasks    │\n    └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘\n         │            │            │\n    feat/a1-auth feat/a2-api  feat/a3-fixes\n         │            │            │\n         └────────────┼────────────┘\n                      v\n                  you merge\n                 (lazygit)\n```\n\n### The Crew Cycling Cadence\n\nThe most important workflow at Stage 6 is the assign-ignore-harvest loop:\n\n```\n1. ASSIGN — Cycle through each agent (C-b <number>),\n   give each one a task, then leave it alone.\n\n2. IGNORE — Don't watch them work. Go do something else.\n   Read docs, plan the next batch, review design.\n\n3. HARVEST — Cycle back through agents. Some are still\n   working (skip them). Some are done. For each finished\n   agent:\n   - Read what it did (scroll back, understand the output)\n   - Accept the work, or send it back with corrections\n   - Assign the next task\n   - Move to the next agent\n\n4. REPEAT\n```\n\nThis cadence is more important than the number of agents. Three agents with a good rhythm beats ten agents you're watching type.\n\nKey discipline: when you see an agent is finished, stop everything and read its output before moving on. Act on each agent's response or risk losing work.\n\n### Session Hygiene\n\nKeep sessions short. Hand off (start a new session) after every completed task rather than letting sessions run until context fills up. Long sessions accumulate stale context that leads to mistakes. Short sessions with fresh context and a clear task file are more reliable.\n\nException: let sessions run long only when accumulating important context for a big design discussion or complex decision.\n\n### What You Learn at This Stage\n\n- How to prompt agents to stay in their lane\n- Branch-per-agent discipline\n- Which tasks deserve \"full\" vs \"quick\" treatment\n- The assign-ignore-harvest rhythm\n- When agents finish and sit idle (you won't always notice)\n- Merge conflicts multiply with more agents\n- You start losing track of who's doing what\n\nThese pain points tell you what to automate in Stage 7.\n\n---\n\n## Stage 6.5 — Conventions and Tracking Solidified\n\nSame setup as Stage 6, but with explicit ownership rules and feature-level tracking (convoys).\n\n### Add an Agents Definition File\n\n```markdown\n# .kiro/specs/agents.md\n\n## agent-1: Frontend\n- Works in: src/components/, src/pages/\n- Branch prefix: feat/a1-\n\n## agent-2: Backend API\n- Works in: src/api/, src/services/\n- Branch prefix: feat/a2-\n\n## agent-3: Tests and Docs\n- Works in: tests/, docs/\n- Branch prefix: feat/a3-\n```\n\n### Add Feature-Level Tracking (Convoys)\n\nInstead of tracking individual tasks, track features as units:\n\n```markdown\n# .kiro/specs/work-queue.md\n\n## Active Features (Convoys)\n- **User Auth** [agent-1: implementing, agent-3: writing tests]\n  - feat/a1-auth: JWT endpoints (in progress)\n  - feat/a3-auth-tests: integration tests (in progress)\n  - feat/a1-auth-middleware: middleware (queued)\n\n- **API Refactor** [agent-2: implementing]\n  - feat/a2-refactor: connection pooling (in progress)\n\n## Done\n- **Navbar Fix** — merged 2026-02-25\n\n## Up Next\n- WebSocket support\n- API docs update\n```\n\n### Context File Structure at Stage 6.5\n\n```\n.kiro/specs/\n├── requirements.md        <- what to build (shared)\n├── design.md              <- how to build it (shared)\n├── principles.md          <- guiding principles + anti-patterns (shared)\n├── agents.md              <- who owns what area\n├── work-queue.md          <- feature-level tracking (convoys)\n└── tasks/\n    ├── agent-1.md         <- current assignment + checklist\n    ├── agent-2.md\n    └── agent-3.md\n```\n\n### Prevent Heresies with a Principles File\n\nWhen multiple agents work in parallel, wrong assumptions spread. One agent guesses how something works, gets it wrong, and the mistake gets into the code. Other agents see it and copy it. Yegge calls these \"heresies.\"\n\nThe fix: a shared principles file that every agent reads on startup.\n\n```markdown\n# .kiro/specs/principles.md\n\n## Core Principles\n- All API responses use camelCase, never snake_case\n- Error handling uses Result types, not try/catch\n- State lives in stores, components are stateless\n- No direct database access from route handlers\n\n## Anti-Patterns (DO NOT do these)\n- Don't create global singletons for services\n- Don't put business logic in UI components\n- Don't use string concatenation for SQL queries\n- Don't import from internal packages across module boundaries\n```\n\nThis is more targeted than design.md. Design describes *how* to build things. Principles describe *what to never do* — the guardrails that prevent agents from drifting.\n\n### Standing Orders (PR Sheriff Pattern)\n\nGive one agent a permanent task that runs on every session startup. This is a lightweight patrol that doesn't need an orchestrator.\n\n```markdown\n# tasks/agent-3.md (permanent standing orders)\n\n## Standing Orders (run on every session startup)\n1. Check for any TODO/FIXME comments added in the last 5 commits\n2. Run the test suite — flag any failures\n3. Check for files that have grown over 300 lines — flag for refactor\n4. Report findings at the top of this file under \"## Latest Report\"\n\n## Latest Report\n(agent updates this section each session)\n\n## Regular Tasks\n- [ ] Current assigned work goes here\n```\n\nThis agent becomes your quality sentinel. It runs its standing orders first, then moves on to regular tasks. You get a health check on every startup for free.\n\n### Review Sweep / Fix Sweep Workflow\n\nA two-phase pattern for maintaining code quality across multi-agent work:\n\n```\nPhase 1 — REVIEW SWEEP\n  Agent reviews code (or a specific area)\n  Files issues in work-queue.md for every problem found\n  Does NOT fix anything — only documents\n\nPhase 2 — FIX SWEEP\n  Agents pick up issues from work-queue.md\n  Each agent fixes issues in their area\n  Normal per-agent branch + merge workflow\n```\n\nThis separates finding problems from fixing them. The review agent can be thorough without context-switching into implementation. The fix agents get well-defined, isolated tasks — exactly what they're best at.\n\n---\n\n## Stage 7 — Crysknife Orchestrator\n\nCrysknife automates the pain points from Stage 6 and scales from 6 to ~15 agents with progressive upgrades. Full spec: `crysknife-design.md`.\n\n### What Crysknife Automates\n\n```\nSTAGE 6 PAIN POINT                    CRYSKNIFE SOLUTION\n────────────────────────────────────   ────────────────────────────────\nYou dispatch work manually             crys sling + @crysknife/sling MCP\nYou don't notice idle agents           crys watch (heartbeat + pane diffing)\nYou merge branches in lazygit          Merger agent with staging branch\nAgents share one working directory     Git worktrees per worker\nYou track status in your head          .crysknife/state.json + crys status\nYou are mayor + monitor + merger       Three roles: mayor, workers, merger\nAgents ignore their assignment         Agent configs with hooks + MCP\nAgents write outside their area        preToolUse hook blocks writes\n```\n\n### How It Works (Summary)\n\nThree roles: mayor (plans/dispatches), workers (code on isolated branches), merger (merges to main via staging). Communication flows through shared files and MCP tool calls, not direct messaging.\n\nkiro-cli native integration: agent configs define prompt, tools, context, hooks, and MCP server per role. `agentSpawn` hook loads task on startup. `preToolUse` hook enforces area boundaries. `stop` hook updates heartbeat. `crys mcp-serve` exposes role-filtered `@crysknife/*` tools.\n\nAdaptive planning: the mayor maintains a living plan.md with tasks grouped by readiness (ready/blocked/discovered/done). No rigid phases.\n\nSee `crysknife-design.md` for: agent config examples, hook scripts, MCP tool definitions, CLI command details, templates, state file format, architecture diagrams, and implementation phases.\n\n### Scaling Limits and Breaking Points\n\nDesigned for 6 agents, stretches to ~15 with upgrades:\n\n```\nAGENTS   WHAT BREAKS                         FIX\n──────   ─────────────────────────────────   ──────────────────────────────\n6        Nothing. Design target.              --\n8-10     You are the bottleneck.              Mayor autonomy, dashboard-first.\n10-12    tmux layout.                         Session groups.\n12-15    JSON state contention.               SQLite upgrade.\n15-20    Area boundaries + single merger.     Soft ownership, second merger.\n20-25    Mayor context + manual oversight.    Split mayor, autonomous patrols.\n25-30    Shared files across worktrees.       Dolt upgrade.\n30+      You've rebuilt Gas Town.             Use Gas Town.\n```\n\nPast 15, the fundamental assumptions (single mayor, single merger, hard area boundaries, you at the keyboard) break. That's by design — by the time you hit 15 agents, you'll know exactly *why* Gas Town needs 7 roles and a database.\n\n### What to Skip at This Stage\n\nGas Town roles not needed with you at the keyboard: Deacon, Dogs, Boot, Wisps, Polecats. Add these only when you need unattended operation (20+ agents).\n\n---\n\n## Gas Town Role Mapping Across Stages\n\n```\nGas Town Role        Stage 5    Stage 6    Stage 6.5  Stage 7         Stage 8\n                                                      (Crysknife)\n────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\nOverseer (you)       you        you        you        you             you\nMayor (planner)      you        you        you        mayor agent     automated\nCrew (workers)       1 agent    3-5 agents 3-5 agents workers (4-12)  agents\nPolecats (ephemeral) --         --         --         --              automated\nRefinery (merger)    you+git    you+git    you+git    merger agent    automated\nWitness (monitor)    you        you        you        crys watch      automated\nDeacon (heartbeat)   --         --         --         --              automated\nDogs (helpers)       --         --         --         --              automated\nBoot (health check)  --         --         --         --              automated\n```\n\n---\n\n## Context Preservation — How Each Approach Works\n\n```\nKIRO (Stage 5-6):\n  .kiro/specs/ files on disk ARE the memory.\n  Session dies = start fresh, same files.\n\nCRYSKNIFE (Stage 7):\n  Agent configs + task files on disk = continuity.\n  Session dies = crys watch detects, restarts with config.\n  Hooks rebuild full context automatically.\n\nGAS TOWN (Stage 8):\n  Dolt database = persistent agent identity.\n  Session dies = agent resumes via GUPP.\n  Agent identity survives across sessions.\n```\n\n---\n\n## Timeline\n\n```\nNow              Stage 5. Single agent, tmux, spec-driven workflow.\nWeek 1-2         Stage 6: run 3-5 agents manually.\n                   Introduce per-agent task files and workflow tiers.\nWeek 2-3         Stage 6.5: solidify conventions.\n                   Add principles.md, agents.md, work-queue.md.\nMonth 1-2        Stage 7: Build Crysknife (6 agents).\n                   Phase 1: Foundation (init, start, stop, status, configs)\n                   Phase 2: Work + MCP (sling, queue, mcp-serve, hooks)\n                   Phase 3: Monitoring (watch, nudge, auto-restart)\n                   Phase 4: Planning (convoy, dashboard)\nMonth 2-3        Scale to 8-10 agents.\n                   Trust the mayor. Dashboard-driven workflow.\nMonth 3-4        Scale to 10-12 agents.\n                   tmux session groups. Consider SQLite upgrade.\nMonth 4+         Iterate based on real pain points.\n                   Each upgrade unlocks the next scaling tier.\nEventually       Try Gas Town, or keep evolving Crysknife.\n```\n\n---\n\n## Key Principles\n\n1. **Externalize all state to files.** Don't keep anything in your head or in a single agent's context. If it's not written down in a shared file, it doesn't exist. This scales from 1 agent to 30.\n\n2. **Keep traceability.** Gas Town optimizes for throughput. We optimize for correctness AND throughput. The acceptance criteria -> design properties -> task subtasks -> tests chain is our advantage. Don't drop it.\n\n3. **Match effort to task size.** Not everything needs a full spec. Use workflow tiers (full/standard/quick) so small fixes don't get buried in process.\n\n4. **Automate what hurts.** Don't build the orchestrator upfront. Use Stage 6 manually until specific pain points demand automation. Build exactly what you need.\n\n5. **Shared specs, split tasks.** Design docs and requirements stay shared — they're the quality guarantee. Only the work assignments (task files) get split per agent.\n\n6. **Short sessions, frequent handoffs.** Don't let sessions run until context fills up. Hand off after each completed task. Fresh context with a clear task file beats a long session with accumulated noise.\n\n7. **Assign, ignore, harvest.** The crew cycling cadence is the core rhythm. Give each agent work, leave it alone, come back to read results. Don't watch agents type.\n\n8. **Guard against heresies.** Multiple agents will propagate wrong assumptions through the codebase. A shared principles file with explicit anti-patterns is the cheapest defense. preToolUse hooks are the hard enforcement. Review sweeps are the third line.\n\n9. **Work with the tool, not against it.** Use kiro-cli's native features (agent configs, hooks, MCP, context) instead of fighting it with tmux send-keys. The less you have to work around the tool, the more reliable the system.\n\n10. **Scale by upgrading, not redesigning.** Crysknife's architecture handles 6 agents out of the box. Each scaling tier (8-10, 10-12, 12-15) requires a specific upgrade. Know what breaks next and fix it when it hurts, not before."
                  }
                ],
                "status": "Success"
              }
            ]
          }

Lessons learned are what *actually* mattered.\n\n2. **A \"Stage 8 — What It Would Take\" stub.** Right now Stage 8 is just \"20+ agents, Gas Town or equivalent\" in the overview and a line in the timeline saying \"Eventually.\" The scaling chart hints at what breaks past 15-20, but there's no explicit section saying \"here's the gap between Crysknife at 15 and Gas Town at 30.\" Even 3-4 lines would close the narrative arc — the doc tells a story from Stage 5 to Stage 7 in detail, then just... stops.\n\nWant me to add either or both?"

        "content": {
          "Prompt": {
            "prompt": "okey so what where the documents you recomend to keepr relaterd to cryknife"
          }
        },
        "timestamp": "2026-03-01T14:59:00.390909-07:00",
        "images": null
      },
      "assistant": {
        "Response": {
          "message_id": "cf0e82e6-b7b7-412f-b420-eb3f9c710e72",
          "content": "Three documents, same as we decided last session:\n\n```\ncrysknife/docs/\n├── crysknife-design.md          # the blueprint — how to build it\n├── multi-agent-progression.md   # personal roadmap — why and when\n└── gas-town-user-manual.md      # operational wisdom — reference material\n```\n\nWhat each one does:\n\n- `crysknife-design.md` (~1594 lines) — the build spec. Agent configs, hooks, MCP tools, CLI commands, templates, state file, architecture, implementation phases. This is what you code from.\n\n- `multi-agent-progression.md` (~635 lines) — your personal journey from Stage 5 to Stage 8. Gas Town concept mapping, the patterns you'll use at each stage (crew cycling, heresies, standing orders, review sweeps), scaling chart, timeline. This is the *why* behind the design decisions.\n\n- `gas-town-user-manual.md` — Steve Yegge's operational wisdom. Reference for when you need to understand a Gas Town concept in depth. You don't build from this — you consult it when something in the other two docs references a Gas Town idea and you want more context.\n\nWe left behind `gastown.md` (the first article) — everything useful from it was already distilled into the other three docs."
        }
      },

  "transcript": [
    "> I have the gastown.md article in the root can you see it ?",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Yes, I can see the gastown.md article in your project root! It's Steve Yegge's comprehensive article about Gas Town - his orchestration system for managing multiple Claude Code instances (and similar AI coding agents) simultaneously.\n\nThe article is quite extensive (34 min read) and covers:\n\n- **Gas Town** - An IDE/orchestrator for managing 20-30+ AI coding agents at once\n- **The MEOW Stack** - Molecular Expression of Work (Beads → Epics → Molecules → Protomolecules → Formulas)\n- **Seven worker roles** - Mayor, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs, and Crew\n- **GUPP** - Gastown Universal Propulsion Principle (keeping agents working continuously)\n- **Convoys** - Work-order system for tracking deliverables\n- Built on top of **Beads** (the Git-backed issue tracker) and uses **tmux** as the primary UI\n\nIt's positioned as a \"Stage 8\" tool for developers who already manage 10+ AI agents simultaneously and are comfortable with \"vibe coding\" - high-throughput, sometimes chaotic development where work flows continuously.\n\nIs there something specific about the article or Gas Town you'd like to discuss or explore?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yes can you help me formated better in the markdown fasshion and clean the emojis I don't like emojies",
    "I'll clean up the markdown formatting and remove the emojis for you.\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "> y",
    "Let me create a cleaned-up version of the entire file:\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",
    "> y",
    "> why did it fail the operation ?",
    "The operation failed because the text I tried to match didn't exist exactly as I specified it in the file. When using `str_replace`, the `old_str` parameter must match the file content EXACTLY - including all whitespace, line breaks, and characters.\n\nThe issue is likely that the emojis in the file are encoded differently than what I copied, or there are subtle whitespace differences I didn't account for.\n\nLet me try a different approach - I'll create a cleaned version from scratch by processing the entire file:\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",
    "> y",
    "\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",
    "> y",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Good! The emojis have been removed. Now let me add proper markdown formatting with headers and better structure:\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "> y",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "> y",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "> t",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",
    "> t",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Perfect! The document is now cleaned up with emojis removed and proper markdown formatting. Let me verify the rest of the document has been properly formatted:\n[Tool uses: grep]",
    "Excellent! The file has been successfully cleaned up. Here's what I did:\n\n1. **Removed all emojis** - Used `sed` to strip out all the emoji characters (🏙️, 🏗️, 👤, 🎩, 😺, 🏭, 🦉, 🐺, 🐶, 🐕, 👷, 📬, 🚚)\n\n2. **Added proper markdown formatting**:\n   - Main title as `#` heading\n   - Major sections as `##` headings  \n   - Subsections as `###` headings\n   - Formatted author/date info with bold text\n   - Cleaned up figure references with italic formatting\n   - Removed redundant \"Press enter or click to view image\" text\n\nThe document now has clean, professional markdown formatting without any emojis, making it much easier to read and more suitable for documentation purposes.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> /mode",
    "> /model",
    "> okey now that wehave the article there lets coment it out",
    "\n\nMarkdown doesn't have a native comment syntax, but the common convention is to use HTML comments (`<!-- ... -->`). Let me wrap the entire content:\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",
    "> no I mean letstalk about it you have. the markdown article in context right ?",
    "Yes, I have the full article in context! What would you like to discuss about it? \n\nSome angles we could go with:\n\n- The overall architecture and whether the MEOW stack / molecular workflow approach makes sense\n- The Kubernetes comparison and how apt it really is\n- Whether the \"8 stages of dev evolution\" framing holds up\n- The economics and sustainability of running 20-30 AI agents simultaneously\n- The GUPP principle and the challenges of keeping agents working autonomously\n- The bold claims vs. practical reality (17 days, 75k lines, 100% vibe coded)\n- How Gas Town compares to other orchestration approaches\n\nWhat's on your mind?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> /model",
    ">  have questions regarding the 7 types of agents and how they interact really can you make me a ascii diagram and explanation",
    "\n\nHere's how the 7 worker roles interact, plus you (the Overseer):\n\n```\n                          ┌─────────────┐\n                          │  OVERSEER   │ (You - the human)\n                          │  (Stage 8)  │\n                          └──────┬──────┘\n                                 │ talks to / directs\n                    ┌────────────┼────────────┐\n                    ▼            ▼             ▼\n             ┌───────────┐ ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐\n             │   MAYOR   │ │  CREW   │  │  DEACON  │\n             │ concierge │ │ per-rig │  │  daemon   │\n             │ chief of  │ │ coding  │  │  beacon   │\n             │  staff    │ │ agents  │  │ (patrol)  │\n             └─────┬─────┘ └────┬────┘  └────┬─────┘\n                   │            │             │\n          slings   │            │ direct      │ heartbeat / DYFJ\n          work     │            │ coding      │ signal downward\n                   │            │             │\n                   ▼            │        ┌────┴─────┐\n            ┌────────────┐     │        ▼          ▼\n            │  POLECATS  │     │   ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐\n            │ ephemeral  │     │   │  DOGS  │ │  BOOT  │\n            │  swarm     │     │   │ helper │ │ checks │\n            │  workers   │     │   │ agents │ │ on the │\n            │  per-rig   │     │   │        │ │ Deacon │\n            └─────┬──────┘     │   └────────┘ └────────┘\n                  │            │        every 5 min\n       produce    │            │\n       Merge      │            │\n       Requests   │            │\n                  ▼            │\n            ┌────────────┐     │\n            │  REFINERY  │     │\n            │  merge     │     │\n            │  queue     │◄────┘ (crew can also submit MRs)\n            │  manager   │\n            └────────────┘\n                  ▲\n                  │ watches over polecats + refinery\n            ┌────┴───────┐\n            │  WITNESS   │\n            │  per-rig   │\n            │  patrol    │\n            │  supervisor│\n            └────────────┘\n```\n\nHere's the breakdown of how they interact:\n\n**Overseer (you)** - You mainly talk to the Mayor and your Crew. You can also interact with any worker since they're all Claude Code instances. You have your own inbox and identity in the system.\n\n**Mayor** - Your main point of contact. Kicks off convoys, slings work to polecats via `gt sling`, and gets notified when convoys land. Town-level role (not tied to a single rig).\n\n**Crew** - Your personal per-rig coding agents with long-lived identities and names you choose. Great for back-and-forth design work. Not managed by the Witness - they report directly to you.\n\n**Polecats** - Ephemeral swarm workers that spin up on demand, do a task, produce a Merge Request, submit it to the Merge Queue, then get decommissioned. Names get recycled. This is where the bulk throughput comes from.\n\n**Refinery** - Sits at the end of the pipeline processing the Merge Queue. Merges changes one at a time to main, handling rebase conflicts intelligently. Can escalate if things get too messy. Runs its own patrol loop.\n\n**Witness** - The per-rig supervisor. Watches over polecats and the refinery, nudges stuck workers, hustles MRs through. Also peeks in on the Deacon. Runs rig-level plugins.\n\n**Deacon** - The town-level daemon beacon. Gets pinged every couple minutes and propagates \"do your job\" signals downward to all workers. Runs town-level plugins. Delegates heavy work to Dogs so it doesn't get bogged down.\n\n**Dogs** - The Deacon's personal helpers. Handle maintenance (stale branches, etc.), run plugins, and do investigations the Deacon doesn't have time for. Town-level workers.\n\n**Boot** - A special Dog that wakes up every 5 minutes just to check if the Deacon is alive and functioning. Acts as a buffer so the daemon's heartbeats don't interrupt the Deacon directly.\n\nThe key flow is: you tell the Mayor what you want → Mayor slings work to polecats → polecats swarm and produce MRs → Refinery merges them → Witness keeps everything unstuck → Deacon keeps the whole town's heartbeat going.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> good how do I save this conversation for later is there a kiro-cli command ?",
    "\n[Tool uses: introspect]",
    "You have a couple of options:\n\n**Save to a file:**\n```\n/chat save gastown-discussion.json\n```\nYou can reload it later with `/chat load gastown-discussion.json`.\n\n**Auto-save (already happening):**\nKiro CLI automatically saves your session to a database on every turn. To resume it later:\n```bash\nkiro-cli chat --resume          # resumes the most recent session\nkiro-cli chat --resume-picker   # lets you pick from a list\n```\n\nOr from within a chat session:\n```\n/chat resume\n```\n\nSo if you just `/quit` right now, you can pick up where we left off next time with `--resume`.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> but kiro-cli chae --resum it ofuter resomes a summary of the conversation not tho whole one right ?",
    "\n\nYou're right to be cautious about that. Kiro CLI has an auto-compaction feature that summarizes the conversation when the context window gets full. So if you had a long session, `--resume` might bring back a compacted/summarized version rather than the full verbatim conversation.\n\nIf you want to preserve the full conversation exactly as-is, your safest bet is:\n\n```\n/chat save gastown-discussion.json\n```\n\nThat exports the complete message history to a JSON file. Then `/chat load gastown-discussion.json` restores the full thing later.\n\nThere's also a setting to disable auto-compaction if you prefer:\n```bash\nkiro-cli settings chat.disableAutoCompaction true\n```\n\nBut saving to a file is the most reliable way to keep the whole conversation intact.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey create achats forder and save the gastown discussion there ",
    "\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",
    "Done! The `chats/` folder is created. Now run this command in the chat:\n\n```\n/chat save chats/gastown-discussion.json\n```\n\nI can't run that slash command for you — you'll need to type it yourself.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "We worked with Steve Yegge's \"Welcome to Gas Town\" article (`gastown.md`) in your chip8-emulator project:\n\n1. Confirmed the file existed and reviewed its contents\n2. Cleaned up the markdown formatting — removed all emojis and added proper headers\n3. Discussed the article's content, specifically the 7 worker roles (Mayor, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs, Crew) and created an ASCII diagram showing how they interact\n4. Covered how to save this conversation in Kiro CLI using `/chat save`, and created a `chats/` folder for it\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yeah con you showe me de diagram of the different agents and how they connect with each other (ascii)",
    "\n\nHere it is again:\n\n```\n                          ┌─────────────┐\n                          │  OVERSEER   │ (You - the human)\n                          │  (Stage 8)  │\n                          └──────┬──────┘\n                                 │ talks to / directs\n                    ┌────────────┼────────────┐\n                    ▼            ▼             ▼\n             ┌───────────┐ ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐\n             │   MAYOR   │ │  CREW   │  │  DEACON  │\n             │ concierge │ │ per-rig │  │  daemon   │\n             │ chief of  │ │ coding  │  │  beacon   │\n             │  staff    │ │ agents  │  │ (patrol)  │\n             └─────┬─────┘ └────┬────┘  └────┬─────┘\n                   │            │             │\n          slings   │            │ direct      │ heartbeat / DYFJ\n          work     │            │ coding      │ signal downward\n                   │            │             │\n                   ▼            │        ┌────┴─────┐\n            ┌────────────┐     │        ▼          ▼\n            │  POLECATS  │     │   ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐\n            │ ephemeral  │     │   │  DOGS  │ │  BOOT  │\n            │  swarm     │     │   │ helper │ │ checks │\n            │  workers   │     │   │ agents │ │ on the │\n            │  per-rig   │     │   │        │ │ Deacon │\n            └─────┬──────┘     │   └────────┘ └────────┘\n                  │            │        every 5 min\n       produce    │            │\n       Merge      │            │\n       Requests   │            │\n                  ▼            │\n            ┌────────────┐     │\n            │  REFINERY  │     │\n            │  merge     │     │\n            │  queue     │◄────┘ (crew can also submit MRs)\n            │  manager   │\n            └────────────┘\n                  ▲\n                  │ watches over polecats + refinery\n            ┌────┴───────┐\n            │  WITNESS   │\n            │  per-rig   │\n            │  patrol    │\n            │  supervisor│\n            └────────────┘\n```\n\nQuick recap of the flow:\n\n- **You (Overseer)** direct the Mayor, Crew, and sometimes the Deacon\n- **Mayor** slings work to Polecats via `gt sling`, kicks off convoys\n- **Polecats** swarm tasks, produce Merge Requests, then get decommissioned\n- **Refinery** processes the Merge Queue, merging changes one at a time to main\n- **Witness** supervises polecats and refinery, nudges stuck workers\n- **Deacon** sends heartbeat signals downward to keep the whole town alive\n- **Dogs** handle grunt work for the Deacon (maintenance, plugins)\n- **Boot** is a special Dog that checks on the Deacon every 5 minutes\n- **Crew** are your personal long-lived coding agents for hands-on work\n[Tool uses: none]",

Coding   │  │  Daemon &   │\n             │  of Staff) │ │  Agents)  │  │  Scheduler) │\n             └─────┬──────┘ └─────┬─────┘  └──────┬──────┘\n                   │              │ (Ephemeral  │      │   │   DOGS    │ │   BOOT    │\n            │  Task       │      │   │ (Maint.   │ │ (Daemon   │\n            │  Workers)   │      │   │  Helpers) │ │  Health   │\n            │ (Merge      │      │\n            │  Queue      │◄─────┘ (crew can also submit MRs)\n            │  Manager)   │\n            └─────────────┘\n                   ▲\n                   │ watches over polecats + refinery\n            ┌──────┴──────┐\n            │   WITNESS   │\n            │ (Supervisor │\n            │  & Monitor) │\n            └─────────────┘\n```\n\nThe parenthetical names should make it clearer what each role actually does without needing to remember the Gas Town lore.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> I understand the crew. and the refinary is what desides the order and how to merge differen changes. that is the extend of it what is the deacon why is deere how does it interecatw with the crow that actualy does the thing not sure what the dogs and boot are. olso the polecats what is ephimeral tasks why does the mayor needs to cordinate those",
    "\n\nLet me break these down one at a time.\n\n**Polecats (Ephemeral Task Workers)**\n\n\"Ephemeral\" just means they're disposable — they spin up, do one job, and die. Think of them as temp workers. Your Crew are permanent employees with names and ongoing relationships. Polecats are anonymous contractors: the Mayor says \"go fix this bug,\" a polecat spawns, fixes it, submits a Merge Request, and gets destroyed. Their names get recycled for the next task. The Mayor coordinates them because someone has to decide *what* work gets done and *who* gets assigned. The Mayor is the dispatcher — you tell it what you want, and it slings tasks to polecats so you don't have to manage each one individually. When you want 10 things done at once (a \"swarm\"), the Mayor spins up 10 polecats in parallel.\n\n**Deacon (Heartbeat Daemon)**\n\nThe core problem: Claude Code sessions die. Context fills up, things crash, agents get stuck. The Deacon exists to keep the whole system alive. Every couple minutes, a background daemon pings the Deacon and says \"do your job.\" The Deacon then checks on all the other workers — are they running? Are they stuck? Do they need a restart? It propagates this \"are you alive?\" signal downward through the system. Without the Deacon, you'd have to manually babysit every agent and notice when one silently dies. The Deacon doesn't do project work — it's pure infrastructure, like a process supervisor (think systemd or supervisord).\n\n**Dogs (Deacon's Helpers)**\n\nThe Deacon's patrol loop has a lot of responsibilities — checking workers, running plugins, cleaning up stale branches, handling session recycling. If the Deacon gets bogged down doing a slow task (like running a plugin that takes 20 minutes), it stops sending heartbeats and the whole town stalls. So Dogs exist to offload work from the Deacon. The Deacon says \"hey, go clean up those stale branches\" and a Dog handles it while the Deacon continues its patrol. They're the Deacon's assistants so it can stay focused on keeping the heartbeat going.\n\n**Boot (Deacon's Watchdog)**\n\nWho watches the watchman? Boot watches the Deacon. Every 5 minutes, Boot wakes up and checks: is the Deacon still running? Is it stuck? Does it need a restart? That's literally Boot's only job. It exists because the daemon's heartbeat pings were interrupting the Deacon's actual work, so Boot acts as a buffer — it receives the \"are you alive?\" check and decides whether the Deacon actually needs intervention or should be left alone.\n\n**How they connect to the Crew:**\n\nYour Crew doesn't interact much with the Deacon/Dogs/Boot directly. Those three are background infrastructure keeping Gas Town's engine running. Your Crew just codes. The Deacon's heartbeat system ensures that if a Crew member's session dies, it gets noticed and restarted. But day-to-day, you talk to your Crew, they code, they submit MRs, the Refinery merges them. The Deacon/Dogs/Boot are invisible plumbing you rarely think about — like how you don't think about your OS's process scheduler while writing code.\n\nIn short:\n- **Crew** = your hands (you direct them)\n- **Polecats** = disposable hands (Mayor directs them)\n- **Refinery** = the merge gatekeeper\n- **Witness** = makes sure workers aren't stuck\n- **Deacon/Dogs/Boot** = keeps the lights on\n[Tool uses: none]",
