    "Glad it clicked! Let me know if you want to dig into any other part of the article — the MEOW stack, GUPP, convoys, or anything else.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> oke more informatino on decon Dogs Boot. how they interact with the actual agents do that do the wor ccew plocasts. also why de we have a crew why cant all be plocasts ?",
    "\n\n**How Deacon/Dogs/Boot interact with the workers**\n\nThink of it as a chain of health checks flowing downward:\n\n```\nDaemon (background timer, every ~2 min)\n    │\n    ▼\n  Boot (every 5 min: \"is the Deacon alive?\")\n    │\n    ▼\n  Deacon (propagates \"do your job\" signal down)\n    │\n    ├──▶ Witness: \"check on your polecats and refinery\"\n    │        │\n    │        ├──▶ Polecat 1: stuck? nudge it\n    │        ├──▶ Polecat 2: dead? restart it\n    │        └──▶ Refinery: MQ backed up? hustle it\n    │\n    ├──▶ Dogs: \"go clean stale branches\" / \"run this plugin\"\n    │\n    └──▶ Mayor: \"anything need attention?\"\n```\n\nThe workers (Crew, Polecats) don't *talk to* the Deacon. The Deacon talks *about* them, through the Witness. The Witness is the one who actually pokes a stuck polecat or nudges the Refinery. The Deacon just makes sure the Witness is doing its job. It's a supervision hierarchy — Deacon supervises Witness, Witness supervises Polecats and Refinery.\n\nYour Crew is mostly outside this chain. The Witness doesn't manage Crew — you do. But the Deacon's heartbeat system still ensures Crew sessions get restarted if they crash.\n\n**Why not make everything Polecats?**\n\nPolecats are stateless and disposable. They spin up, do one task, submit an MR, and die. Every time a new polecat starts, it has zero memory of what came before. That's great for parallelism but terrible for:\n\n- **Back-and-forth design work** — You say \"try this approach,\" the agent does it, you say \"no, tweak this part,\" it adjusts. A polecat would die after step one. A Crew member remembers the whole conversation.\n\n- **Context and relationships** — Your Crew member \"Alice\" knows your codebase patterns, your preferences, the decisions you made yesterday. A polecat knows nothing — it reads its task description and goes.\n\n- **Complex multi-step work** — If you're iterating on an architecture, reviewing designs, or doing exploratory work, you need a persistent agent you can have a dialogue with. Polecats are fire-and-forget.\n\n- **Trust and familiarity** — You build a working relationship with Crew members. You learn their quirks, they accumulate context about your project through their persistent identity in Beads.\n\nThe analogy: Polecats are Uber drivers — they show up, take you somewhere, disappear. Crew are your coworkers — they sit next to you, know the project, and you can tap them on the shoulder anytime.\n\nYou use Polecats when you have 15 well-defined tasks and want them all done in parallel right now. You use Crew when you need to think through something together.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey so currently kiro uses some sort of agent arquitecture with the spec dreven design. every session has the file contexts design spec and tasks + all the different files in teh codebase that deems approparet after the sessino is done is we start a new session where we loed it again if we are in the middel of a big task and the context gets filled up we make a summary. you probably know how kiro works. now how does dies compare so we acheve contexts thrgouht this files or summaries but we create  new session every task. so how does it compare ?",
    "\n\nGood comparison to draw. Let me lay it out side by side.\n\n**Kiro's approach (what you're using now)**\n\n```\nYou ──▶ Single Agent Session\n            │\n            ├── Reads: specs, design docs, tasks.md\n            ├── Reads: relevant codebase files\n            ├── Works on task\n            ├── Context fills up ──▶ /compact (summary)\n            └── New session ──▶ Reloads files + summary\n```\n\nContext is preserved through *files on disk* — your `.kiro/specs/` folder with requirements, design, and tasks. When a session ends or compacts, the next session re-reads those files and picks up from there. It's one agent, one task at a time, sequential. The \"memory\" lives in the spec files and summaries, not in the agent itself.\n\n**Gas Town's approach**\n\n```\nYou ──▶ Mayor ──▶ slings 10 tasks to 10 Polecats (parallel)\n            │         each polecat: one task, one MR, dies\n            │\n            ├──▶ Crew member \"Alice\" (persistent, back-and-forth)\n            ├──▶ Crew member \"Bob\" (persistent, different area)\n            │\n            └── Context preserved via:\n                  ├── Beads (issues in Git = persistent memory)\n                  ├── Hooks (what each agent is working on)\n                  ├── Molecules (workflow chains that survive crashes)\n                  └── Agent Beads (persistent identity across sessions)\n```\n\n**Key differences:**\n\n| | Kiro CLI | Gas Town |\n|---|---|---|\n| Agents | 1 at a time | 10-30 simultaneous |\n| Context survival | Spec files + summaries | Beads (Git-backed issues) + molecules |\n| When session dies | You restart, reload files | GUPP auto-restarts, agent finds its place in the molecule |\n| Task management | You pick the next task from tasks.md | Mayor/Witness dispatch and track via convoys |\n| Parallelism | None — sequential | Core feature — swarms |\n| Memory | Files on disk (.kiro/specs/) | Beads database (also files in Git) |\n| Merge conflicts | N/A — one agent | Refinery handles the MQ |\n\n**Where they're actually similar:**\n\nThe core idea is the same — context doesn't live in the agent's head, it lives in *files*. Kiro uses spec files. Gas Town uses Beads (which are also files in Git). Both solve the \"session dies, context is lost\" problem by externalizing state to disk.\n\nThe big leap Gas Town makes is: if your context is already externalized to files, then *any* agent can pick up *any* task — not just the one that started it. That's what enables parallelism. Kiro's spec-driven approach is actually the same principle, just applied to one agent at a time.\n\nIn a sense, Kiro is like having one really good Crew member. Gas Town is like having a whole factory of them, plus infrastructure to keep the factory running (Deacon, Witness, etc.). The tradeoff is complexity — Gas Town needs all that extra machinery (7 roles, GUPP, patrols) just to manage the chaos of 30 agents stepping on each other.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey so who has access the the beads ? the crow or the policats ? kiro being like o good crew member. the problem is that in partially remombers what he did. he does remember because of the tasks files and the checkboxes and becous it toks a look sometmise at the files that where coded but I guess polecats do that as well right ? I guess after that question we need to get more detalis bout context presevred primitives beads hooks molucuels agent beeds. what they are exactly how do they make the agenst what they are. correctly if im wroing so a agent is a agent type becouse of the initiar promp and teh beads it has access to  right ? so a third qustion",
    "\n\nLet me tackle these one at a time.\n\n**Who has access to Beads?**\n\nEveryone. All workers — Crew, Polecats, Mayor, Witness, Refinery, Deacon, Dogs — can read and write Beads. Beads is the shared database. It's just JSON files in Git, so any agent with access to the repo can see everything. The difference isn't *access*, it's *what they care about*:\n\n- A Polecat looks at its one assigned task bead, does the work, closes it, moves on, dies\n- A Crew member browses beads, picks up work, discusses it with you, files new ones\n- The Witness reads beads to check if polecats are stuck\n- The Refinery reads the Merge Queue beads\n\nSo yes, Polecats and Crew both look at code files and task descriptions, just like Kiro does with your tasks.md checkboxes. The difference is lifespan — a Polecat sees one task and forgets everything. Crew accumulates conversational context across a session (like Kiro does).\n\n**The context primitives — what they actually are:**\n\n**Beads** — The base unit. A bead is just an issue/ticket stored as one JSON line in a file tracked by Git. It has an ID, title, description, status, assignee. Think of it as a row in a todo list, but in Git. Everything in Gas Town is a bead — tasks, mail messages, agent identities, hooks, molecules. It's beads all the way down.\n\n**Agent Beads** — A special bead that *is* the agent's identity. It's a persistent record in Git that says \"this agent exists, its role is Mayor, its inbox is here, its hook is here.\" When a Claude Code session dies and a new one starts for the same role, it reads the same Agent Bead and becomes that agent again. The session is disposable; the identity persists.\n\n**Role Beads** — A template bead that defines what a role *is*. It contains the prompting, the instructions, the behavioral rules for that role. The Agent Bead points to its Role Bead. So:\n\n```\nRole Bead: \"Refinery\"\n  └── defines: what a Refinery does, how it behaves\n        │\nAgent Bead: \"refinery-1\"\n  └── points to Role Bead \"Refinery\"\n  └── has: inbox, hook, orchestration state\n        │\nSession: (ephemeral Claude Code instance)\n  └── reads Agent Bead, becomes \"refinery-1\"\n```\n\n**Hooks** — A special pinned bead attached to each agent. It's the agent's \"current assignment.\" When you `gt sling` work to an agent, it goes on their hook. When the agent starts up (or restarts after a crash), GUPP says \"check your hook — if there's work, do it.\" The hook is what makes agents self-starting.\n\n**Molecules** — A chain of beads linked together as a workflow. Bead 1 → Bead 2 → Bead 3. The agent works through them in order, checking each off. Because each step is a separate bead in Git, if the agent crashes between step 2 and 3, the next session sees \"step 2 is done, step 3 is not\" and picks up there. This is what makes workflows crash-proof.\n\n**So what makes an agent what it is?**\n\nYou're mostly right. An agent's identity comes from:\n\n1. **Role Bead** — the system prompt / behavioral instructions (what *kind* of agent)\n2. **Agent Bead** — the persistent identity (which *specific* agent)\n3. **Hook** — what it's currently working on\n4. **Inbox** — mail and messages from other agents\n5. **The Claude Code session** — the ephemeral brain that reads all of the above\n\nThe session is just a temporary brain plugged into a permanent identity. Like a shift worker clocking in — the role, the desk, the inbox, the current task were all there before they arrived and will be there after they leave.\n\n**Back to the Kiro comparison:**\n\nKiro is actually doing a simpler version of the same thing. Your `.kiro/specs/` files *are* the Role Bead (they tell the agent what the project is about and how to behave). Your `tasks.md` with checkboxes *is* a molecule (a sequential workflow the agent walks through). The difference is Kiro doesn't separate the agent identity from the session — when the session dies, the \"agent\" dies with it, and the next session is a new agent reading the same files. In Gas Town, the agent *persists* and the session is just a temporary vehicle.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> you mentioned teh kiro keeps conversational contexts across convelsatinos but each conversatin is a new contexts and need to load files again when you click creat new file it start from the beginning so is not 100 accuaret but okey.  olso molecules semes simelar to the task file and stil nut sure if beads i alike a git feature I know nothnig about or is just a json file that is fancy formated. can you create ascii diagram eplaining this things",
    "\n\nYou're right, I misspoke — Kiro doesn't carry conversational context across sessions. Each new session starts fresh and reloads the spec files. The only \"memory\" is what's written to disk.\n\nAnd yes, Beads is not a Git feature — it's literally just a JSON file committed to Git. Nothing fancy under the hood. Here's the breakdown:\n\n```\nBEADS = Just a JSON file in your Git repo\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  your-project/\n  └── .beads/\n      └── beads.jsonl          <── one JSON object per line\n                                    that's it. that's beads.\n\n  Each line = one \"bead\" (a ticket/issue):\n\n  {\"id\":\"bd-x7k2\",\"title\":\"Fix navbar\",\"status\":\"open\",\"assignee\":\"polecat-3\",...}\n  {\"id\":\"bd-a1b2\",\"title\":\"Add tests\",\"status\":\"done\",\"assignee\":\"crew-alice\",...}\n  {\"id\":\"bd-m9n4\",\"title\":\"Refactor API\",\"status\":\"open\",\"parent\":\"bd-epic1\",...}\n\n  Because it's in Git:\n    - Every change is versioned\n    - Every agent can read/write it (it's just a file)\n    - History is automatic (git log)\n    - No database server needed\n\n\nMOLECULES vs KIRO'S TASKS.MD\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  Kiro tasks.md:                    Gas Town molecule:\n\n  ## Task 1                         Bead A ──▶ Bead B ──▶ Bead C ──▶ Bead D\n  - [x] Step 1                      (done)     (done)     (working)  (todo)\n  - [x] Step 2                         │          │           │         │\n  - [ ] Step 3  ◄── you are here       │          │           │         │\n  - [ ] Step 4                       each step = separate bead in the\n                                     JSONL file, linked by dependencies\n\n  Same idea:                        Key difference:\n  - Sequential checklist            - If agent crashes at Bead C,\n  - Agent works top to bottom         next session sees C is \"open\"\n  - Checkboxes = progress             and picks up there automatically\n                                    - Any agent can resume it (not\n                                      just the one that started)\n\n\nAGENT IDENTITY — How a \"role\" is assembled\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  In Git (.beads/beads.jsonl):\n\n  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n  │ Role Bead: \"refinery\"                               │\n  │ {\"id\":\"role-ref\",\"type\":\"role\",\"prompt\":\"You are     │\n  │  the Refinery. You process the merge queue...\"}     │\n  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n  │ Agent Bead: \"refinery-1\"                            │\n  │ {\"id\":\"agt-ref1\",\"role\":\"role-ref\",                 │\n  │  \"hook\":\"hook-ref1\",\"inbox\":\"mbx-ref1\"}             │\n  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n  │ Hook Bead: \"hook-ref1\"                              │\n  │ {\"id\":\"hook-ref1\",\"molecule\":\"mol-abc\",             │\n  │  \"current_step\":\"bd-step3\"}                         │\n  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n          │\n          │  On startup, Claude Code session reads these\n          ▼\n\n  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n  │ Claude Code Session (ephemeral)                     │\n  │                                                     │\n  │  \"I am refinery-1.                                  │\n  │   My job: process merge queue (from Role Bead).     │\n  │   My current work: molecule mol-abc, step 3         │\n  │   (from Hook Bead).                                 │\n  │   Let me continue where the last session left off.\" │\n  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\n  If this session crashes:\n\n  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n  │ NEW Claude Code Session (ephemeral)                 │\n  │                                                     │\n  │  Reads same Agent Bead, same Hook Bead.             │\n  │  \"I am refinery-1. Step 3 is still open.            │\n  │   Picking up where the last session left off.\"      │\n  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\n\nKIRO vs GAS TOWN — Context model\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  KIRO:\n  ┌──────────┐    reads     ┌──────────────────┐\n  │ Session  │◄─────────────│ .kiro/specs/     │\n  │ (fresh   │              │  requirements.md │\n  │  every   │              │  design.md       │\n  │  time)   │              │  tasks.md        │\n  └──────────┘              └──────────────────┘\n  Session dies = agent dies.\n  New session = new agent, same files.\n  No identity persists. Files ARE the memory.\n\n  GAS TOWN:\n  ┌──────────┐    reads     ┌──────────────────┐\n  │ Session  │◄─────────────│ .beads/beads.jsonl│\n  │ (fresh   │              │  - Agent Bead    │\n  │  every   │              │  - Role Bead     │\n  │  time)   │              │  - Hook Bead     │\n  │          │              │  - Molecule chain│\n  └──────────┘              │  - Inbox/mail    │\n  Session dies = agent LIVES.└──────────────────┘\n  New session = same agent, resumes work.\n  Identity persists in Beads. Files ARE the memory.\n```\n\nSo the punchline: both systems store everything in files on disk. The difference is Gas Town puts *agent identity* in those files too, so when a session dies, the next session becomes the *same agent* and picks up where it left off. Kiro starts a blank agent every time that just happens to read the same spec files.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> I see so I know the kiro ide is really opitionated but kiro-cli is less so not sure if they use the same standards for decument creation an the rest ?",
    "\n\nI don't have detailed knowledge of the internal differences between Kiro IDE and Kiro CLI in terms of how they handle spec-driven development. What I can tell you from working with you here in the CLI:\n\n- Kiro CLI doesn't enforce the `.kiro/specs/` structure — it'll read those files if they're there (like in your chip8 project), but it doesn't auto-generate them or require them\n- The CLI is more of a general-purpose assistant — you can use it for anything, not just spec-driven coding\n- Features like `/plan` exist for structured planning, but it's opt-in, not mandatory\n\nFor specifics on whether Kiro IDE and CLI share the same spec format, document generation standards, or workflow conventions, I'd suggest checking the official Kiro docs at [kiro.dev](https://kiro.dev) — I don't want to guess and give you wrong info on that.\n\nWhat I can say is that the comparison to Gas Town still holds either way: both Kiro (IDE or CLI) and Gas Town externalize context to files. The main architectural difference is Gas Town adds persistent agent identity and multi-agent parallelism on top of that pattern.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey yeah im trying to recreate something similro to gas town in my tmux nvim, kiro-cli(multilple) lazygit . before going full into something liueg gas town like the article recomends im currently I thnk in stage 5 or maybe 5.5. I need to get to the 7 but im not sure how o do it like get some ideas on how to manage estate I thikn those are some of the moset interesting ideas to mak something hald function wheer I can run like 10+ agenst something custom. after I get used to that maybe I try gastown",
    "\n\nThat's a smart approach — building up gradually rather than jumping straight into Gas Town. You already have good tools (tmux, nvim, kiro-cli, lazygit). Let me sketch out a progression from Stage 5.5 to Stage 7 using what you have.\n\n**Stage 6: 3-5 parallel agents**\n\nStart here. The minimum you need:\n\n```\ntmux layout (3 agents):\n\n┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┐\n│                 │  kiro-cli #1    │\n│    nvim         │  (feature work) │\n│                 ├─────────────────┤\n│                 │  kiro-cli #2    │\n│                 │  (bug fixes)    │\n├─────────────────┼─────────────────┤\n│   lazygit       │  kiro-cli #3    │\n│                 │  (tests/docs)   │\n└─────────────────┴─────────────────┘\n```\n\nAt this stage your main problem is: \"what is each agent doing and did it finish?\" You solve this with a simple shared file:\n\n```\n# work-queue.md  (your poor man's Beads)\n\n## In Progress\n- [kiro-1] Implement user auth endpoint\n- [kiro-2] Fix race condition in cache layer\n- [kiro-3] Write integration tests for API\n\n## Done\n- [kiro-2] Fix navbar overflow bug\n- [kiro-1] Add rate limiting middleware\n\n## Up Next\n- Refactor database connection pooling\n- Add WebSocket support\n- Update API docs\n```\n\nEach agent gets told: \"You are kiro-1. Check work-queue.md for your assignment. When done, update your checkbox and pick the next item from Up Next.\"\n\nThat's it. That's Stage 6. You're the Mayor, the Witness, and the Refinery all in one.\n\n**Stage 6.5: Add state management**\n\nThe first thing that breaks at 5 agents is merge conflicts. Two agents edit the same file. You need rules:\n\n```\n# agents.md (your poor man's Role Beads)\n\n## kiro-1: Frontend\n- Works in: src/components/, src/pages/\n- Branch prefix: feat/k1-\n\n## kiro-2: Backend API\n- Works in: src/api/, src/services/\n- Branch prefix: feat/k2-\n\n## kiro-3: Tests & Docs\n- Works in: tests/, docs/\n- Branch prefix: feat/k3-\n```\n\nEach agent works on its own branch, in its own area. You merge with lazygit. You are the Refinery.\n\n**Stage 7: 10+ agents, the hard part**\n\nThis is where you need actual infrastructure. The problems:\n\n1. You can't watch 10 tmux panes at once\n2. Agents finish and sit idle — you don't notice\n3. Merge conflicts multiply\n4. You lose track of who's doing what\n\nHere's a minimal DIY system using what you have:\n\n```\nProject structure:\n\n.orchestra/\n├── queue.md          # work items (your Beads)\n├── agents.md         # who exists, what area they own\n├── status.md         # auto-updated by agents when they finish\n└── merge-order.md    # which branches merge in what order\n```\n\n**The key trick for Stage 7:** make agents self-reporting. Add this to every agent's initial prompt:\n\n> \"When you finish a task, append a line to .orchestra/status.md with the\n> timestamp, your agent ID, what you did, and your branch name. Then check\n> queue.md for your next task. If nothing is there, say IDLE and wait.\"\n\nThen you write a simple watcher script:\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\n# watch-agents.sh — your poor man's Witness\nwhile true; do\n    clear\n    echo \"=== AGENT STATUS ===\"\n    tail -20 .orchestra/status.md\n    echo \"\"\n    echo \"=== IDLE AGENTS ===\"\n    grep \"IDLE\" .orchestra/status.md | tail -10\n    echo \"\"\n    echo \"=== PENDING MERGES ===\"\n    git branch | grep \"feat/\" | wc -l\n    sleep 10\ndone\n```\n\nRun that in a tmux pane and you have a dashboard.\n\n**tmux setup for 10 agents:**\n\nDon't try to see them all at once. Use tmux sessions, not panes:\n\n```bash\n# start-agents.sh\nfor i in $(seq 1 10); do\n    tmux new-session -d -s \"agent-$i\" \"kiro-cli chat\"\ndone\ntmux new-session -d -s \"dashboard\" \"./watch-agents.sh\"\ntmux new-session -d -s \"nvim\" \"nvim\"\ntmux new-session -d -s \"git\" \"lazygit\"\n```\n\nThen use `C-b s` to jump between sessions. You cycle through agents like Yegge describes with his Crew.\n\n**The graduation path:**\n\n```\nStage 5.5 (you now)\n  │  Just start a second kiro-cli in a tmux split\n  ▼\nStage 6 (weeks 1-2)\n  │  3-5 agents, work-queue.md, manual merging\n  ▼\nStage 6.5 (weeks 3-4)\n  │  Add agents.md, branch conventions, area ownership\n  ▼\nStage 7 (month 2+)\n  │  10+ agents, status reporting, watcher script, tmux sessions\n  ▼\nGas Town (when Stage 7 feels limiting)\n```\n\nThe biggest lesson from the article that applies right now: externalize all state to files. Don't keep anything in your head or in a single agent's context. If it's not written down in a shared file, it doesn't exist. That's the core principle that scales from 2 agents to 30.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> ok so rigt now im not even using tmux split I have a bunch of tabs nvim kiro-cli tabs and lazygit and change quicly between them. so I should probably learn that and im more accuratly level 5. so I want a accuaret representation of how level 5 looks for me how stage 6 you see it working for me. stage 7 im not going to try doing manually im going to code some orchestrator not something as andavced as gastown something in between. this is how I see my progress in probably do my own tol for stage 7 something in between. maybe some of the roles of gas town or oll of them in a simpler configuration. wha do you think ?",
    "\n\nThat makes sense — jumping from manual Stage 6 straight to building your own orchestrator for Stage 7 is actually a good path. You'll understand the problems firsthand before automating them. Here's how I see your progression:\n\n```\nSTAGE 5 — Where you are now\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  Terminal tabs (manual switching):\n\n  ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐\n  │ nvim │ │ kiro │ │ lazy │ │ kiro │  ...more tabs\n  │      │ │ cli  │ │ git  │ │ cli  │\n  └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘\n     Tab 1   Tab 2   Tab 3    Tab 4\n\n  - One agent at a time gets your attention\n  - You context-switch by tabbing around\n  - Work is sequential: finish one thing, start another\n  - State lives in your head + spec files\n  - You ARE the agent's manager, memory, and merge tool\n\n\nSTAGE 6 — Learn tmux, run 3-5 agents intentionally\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  tmux sessions (C-b s to jump between):\n\n  ┌─ session: editor ──────────────────────┐\n  │  nvim (your code, your overview)       │\n  └────────────────────────────────────────┘\n  ┌─ session: agent-1 ────────────────────┐\n  │  kiro-cli (working on feature X)       │\n  └────────────────────────────────────────┘\n  ┌─ session: agent-2 ────────────────────┐\n  │  kiro-cli (working on feature Y)       │\n  └────────────────────────────────────────┘\n  ┌─ session: agent-3 ────────────────────┐\n  │  kiro-cli (tests / docs / small fixes) │\n  └────────────────────────────────────────┘\n  ┌─ session: git ─────────────────────────┐\n  │  lazygit (merge branches, resolve)     │\n  └────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\n  What changes from Stage 5:\n  - Agents run in PARALLEL (not sequential)\n  - Each agent on its own branch\n  - You cycle through with C-b s, check progress\n  - You manually assign work and merge results\n  - Shared work-queue.md so agents know their task\n  - You start noticing: \"agent-2 finished 10 min ago\n    and I didn't notice\" — this is the pain that\n    motivates Stage 7\n\n  What you need to learn:\n  - tmux basics (5-6 commands, maybe 30 min)\n  - Branch-per-agent discipline\n  - How to prompt agents to stay in their lane\n\n\nSTAGE 7 — Your custom orchestrator\n═══════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  After a few weeks at Stage 6, you'll feel these pains:\n\n  1. \"Who's idle? Who's stuck?\"        → need a Witness\n  2. \"Merging 5 branches is hell\"       → need a Refinery\n  3. \"I keep forgetting to assign work\" → need a Dispatcher\n  4. \"Agents die and I don't notice\"    → need a Heartbeat\n\n  Your tool doesn't need all 7 Gas Town roles.\n  Start with the 3 that solve real pain:\n\n  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n  │            YOUR ORCHESTRATOR                │\n  │                                             │\n  │  ┌───────────┐  What it does:               │\n  │  │ Dispatcher│  - Reads a task queue file    │\n  │  │           │  - Assigns tasks to agents    │\n  │  │ (Mayor)   │  - Spins up kiro-cli in tmux │\n  │  └─────┬─────┘  - Tracks who has what        │\n  │        │                                     │\n  │  ┌─────▼─────┐  What it does:               │\n  │  │  Monitor  │  - Watches agent sessions     │\n  │  │           │  - Detects idle/stuck/dead    │\n  │  │ (Witness) │  - Alerts you or restarts     │\n  │  └─────┬─────┘                               │\n  │        │                                     │\n  │  ┌─────▼─────┐  What it does:               │\n  │  │  Merger   │  - Queues finished branches   │\n  │  │           │  - Merges one at a time       │\n  │  │(Refinery) │  - Handles conflicts          │\n  │  └───────────┘                               │\n  │                                             │\n  │  State file: .orchestra/state.json          │\n  │  (your simplified Beads)                    │\n  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\nI think this is a solid plan. Some thoughts on building it:\n\n**Language choice** — You could build it in Go (like Gas Town), or even as a set of shell scripts + a simple JSON state file to start. Shell scripts are ugly but you'd have something working in a day. Go or Python if you want it to last.\n\n**Start with just the Dispatcher.** A script that reads a task list, spins up a tmux session with kiro-cli, and passes it the task. That alone is a huge upgrade from manual tab management. The Monitor and Merger can come later when you feel the pain.\n\n**The state file is the whole game.** Gas Town's big insight is: put everything in a file that any agent can read. Your orchestrator's state.json tracks which agents exist, what they're working on, and their status. That's your Beads equivalent. Keep it simple:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"agents\": [\n    {\"id\": \"agent-1\", \"status\": \"working\", \"task\": \"Add auth\", \"branch\": \"feat/auth\"},\n    {\"id\": \"agent-2\", \"status\": \"idle\", \"task\": null, \"branch\": null},\n    {\"id\": \"agent-3\", \"status\": \"done\", \"task\": \"Fix CSS\", \"branch\": \"feat/css-fix\"}\n  ],\n  \"queue\": [\n    {\"id\": \"task-4\", \"title\": \"Add WebSocket support\"},\n    {\"id\": \"task-5\", \"title\": \"Write API docs\"}\n  ]\n}\n```\n\n**Skip the Deacon/Dogs/Boot entirely.** Those exist because Gas Town runs 30 agents autonomously for hours. At 10 agents with you at the keyboard, you don't need a heartbeat daemon — you're the heartbeat. Add that layer only if you start running agents overnight.\n\n**The progression timeline I'd suggest:**\n\n```\nNow          → Learn tmux (one afternoon)\nWeek 1-3     → Stage 6 manually with 3-5 agents\nWeek 4       → Start building Dispatcher script\nWeek 5-6     → Add Monitor (detect idle/stuck)\nWeek 7-8     → Add Merger (automated branch merging)\nMonth 3+     → Iterate, add features as pain demands\nEventually   → Try Gas Town, or keep evolving yours\n```\n\nThe key thing: don't design the orchestrator upfront. Use Stage 6 manually until you're annoyed enough to automate specific pain points. That way you build exactly what you need, not what you think you'll need.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> im actually using tmux righ now is ust I change terminals instand of splitting the screen now based on whot we have talked about seems to me like I need to olso change how I do spec and design files, task files. I wont some element of that to remain because is really pewerfull benig able to use and keep the same style of code muliple opereation I don't want to droop it just yet I think is really powerful for code quality. or multilpe task ",
    "\n\nYou're already further along than I assumed — you're using tmux, just sessions instead of splits. That's fine, that's basically how Yegge uses it too (`C-b s` to jump between sessions).\n\nAnd you're right to keep the spec/design/tasks approach. It's doing exactly what Beads does — externalizing context to files so any session can pick it up. Don't drop it. Evolve it.\n\nThe problem you'll hit with multiple agents is: one tasks.md file doesn't scale. Two agents reading the same tasks.md will step on each other. Here's how to adapt what you have:\n\n```\nCURRENT (single agent):\n\n.kiro/specs/\n├── requirements.md      ← what to build\n├── design.md            ← how to build it\n└── tasks.md             ← sequential checklist\n                            one agent works top to bottom\n\n\nEVOLVED (multi-agent):\n\n.kiro/specs/\n├── requirements.md      ← shared, read-only for agents\n├── design.md            ← shared, read-only for agents\n└── tasks/               ← one file PER agent\n    ├── agent-1.md       ← \"you are agent-1, here's your work\"\n    ├── agent-2.md       ← \"you are agent-2, here's your work\"\n    └── agent-3.md       ← \"you are agent-3, here's your work\"\n```\n\nEach agent gets told: \"Read requirements.md and design.md for context. Your tasks are in tasks/agent-1.md. Stay in your lane.\"\n\nThe spec files stay shared — that's your code quality guarantee. Every agent reads the same design, follows the same patterns, produces consistent code. You just split the *work* across files, not the *standards*.\n\nA task file per agent would look like:\n\n```markdown\n# Agent 1 — Auth Module\n\n## Context\nRead: requirements.md, design.md\nWork in: src/auth/, src/middleware/\nBranch: feat/a1-auth\n\n## Tasks\n- [ ] Implement JWT token generation\n- [ ] Add refresh token endpoint\n- [ ] Wire up auth middleware\n\n## Rules\n- Follow patterns in design.md\n- Don't touch files outside your area\n- When done, update status to DONE at the top of this file\n```\n\nThe design.md and requirements.md do the heavy lifting for consistency. They're your \"Role Bead\" — every agent reads them and produces code in the same style. The per-agent task files are your \"molecules\" — isolated work that doesn't conflict.\n\n```\nHow it flows:\n\n  requirements.md ──────┐\n  design.md ─────────┐  │\n                     │  │  (shared context = consistent code)\n          ┌──────────┤  ├──────────┐\n          ▼          ▼  ▼          ▼\n    ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐\n    │ agent-1  │ │ agent-2  │ │ agent-3  │\n    │ reads:   │ │ reads:   │ │ reads:   │\n    │ design + │ │ design + │ │ design + │\n    │ its own  │ │ its own  │ │ its own  │\n    │ tasks    │ │ tasks    │ │ tasks    │\n    └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘\n         │            │            │\n    feat/a1-auth feat/a2-api  feat/a3-tests\n         │            │            │\n         └────────────┼────────────┘\n                      ▼\n                  you merge\n                 (lazygit)\n```\n\nSo you keep everything that's working for you — spec-driven, design-first, consistent code quality. You just add the multi-agent layer on top by splitting tasks into separate files and giving each agent its own branch and area.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Clean setup. You have 4 windows — nvim, one kiro-cli, lazygit, and a spare terminal. That's textbook Stage 5.\n\nHere's what Stage 6 looks like as an evolution of your script:\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\n# Tmux session setup for chip8 project — Stage 6\n\nSESSION=\"chip8\"\nPROJECT=~/Documents/code/projects/chip8-emulator\n\ntmux has-session -t $SESSION 2>/dev/null\n\nif [ $? != 0 ]; then\n  tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION -c $PROJECT\n\n  # Window 1: nvim\n  tmux rename-window -t $SESSION:1 'nvim'\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:1 'nvim .' C-m\n\n  # Window 2-4: kiro-cli agents\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:2 -n 'agent-1' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:2 'kiro-cli chat' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:3 -n 'agent-2' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:3 'kiro-cli chat' C-m\n\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:4 -n 'agent-3' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:4 'kiro-cli chat' C-m\n\n  # Window 5: lazygit\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:5 -n 'lazygit' -c $PROJECT\n  tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:5 'lazygit' C-m\n\n  # Window 6: terminal\n  tmux new-window -t $SESSION:6 -n 'terminal' -c $PROJECT\n\n  tmux select-window -t $SESSION:1\nfi\n\ntmux attach -t $SESSION\n```\n\nThe only real change: 3 kiro-cli windows instead of 1. Same workflow you already know — `C-b` + window number to jump around. You'd tell each agent its role on startup (\"You are agent-1, read design.md, your tasks are in tasks/agent-1.md, work on branch feat/a1-xxx\").\n\n**Where does this put you on the scale?**\n\n```\nStage 5   — 1 agent, sequential          ← your script now\nStage 6   — 3-5 agents, parallel         ← the script above\n                                             + per-agent task files\n                                             + branch-per-agent\nStage 6.5 — same but with conventions    ← add agents.md with\n              that are second nature          area ownership rules\n\nStage 7   — needs automation             ← your custom orchestrator\n```\n\nThe per-agent task files + the multi-agent tmux script puts you solidly at Stage 6. You stay there until the manual parts get painful (forgetting to check on agents, merge conflicts piling up, losing track of who's doing what). That pain is what tells you exactly what your Stage 7 orchestrator needs to automate.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "\n\nYes, I still have the full Gas Town article in context. Ask away — whether it's about GUPP, the MEOW stack, convoys, molecules, wisps, or anything else from it.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yeah I think the MEOW stack is one of the more fundametal innovations I think there are lessons there that we can apply to our implmenteation. can you explain it to me. do o few diagrems (ascii",
    "\n\nThe MEOW stack (Molecular Expression of Work) is basically 5 layers of abstraction for representing work, each building on the one below. Think of it as: how do you go from a vague idea to a crash-proof workflow that agents execute autonomously?\n\n```\nTHE MEOW STACK — Bottom to Top\n═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n  Layer 5:  FORMULAS          (source code for workflows, TOML)\n               │ \"cook\"\n               ▼\n  Layer 4:  PROTOMOLECULES    (templates, reusable workflow patterns)\n               │ \"instantiate\"\n               ▼\n  Layer 3:  MOLECULES         (live workflows, chained beads)\n               │ composed of\n               ▼\n  Layer 2:  EPICS             (beads with children, tree structure)\n               │ composed of\n               ▼\n  Layer 1:  BEADS             (atomic work units, JSON in Git)\n```\n\nLet me break each layer down.\n\n**Layer 1: Beads — The Atom**\n\nThe smallest unit. One task, one ticket, one line of JSON.\n\n```\n.beads/beads.jsonl:\n\n{\"id\":\"bd-001\",\"title\":\"Add login button\",\"status\":\"open\",\"assignee\":null}\n{\"id\":\"bd-002\",\"title\":\"Write auth tests\",\"status\":\"done\",\"assignee\":\"agent-1\"}\n{\"id\":\"bd-003\",\"title\":\"Fix token expiry\",\"status\":\"open\",\"assignee\":\"polecat-2\"}\n\nThat's it. One line = one bead. Stored in Git.\n```\n\n**Layer 2: Epics — The Tree**\n\nA bead that has children. The children can themselves be epics. This gives you hierarchical planning — break a big feature into sub-features into tasks.\n\n```\nEpic: \"User Authentication\"  (bd-100)\n├── Epic: \"Frontend Auth\"    (bd-101)\n│   ├── Bead: \"Login form\"   (bd-102)\n│   ├── Bead: \"Signup form\"  (bd-103)\n│   └── Bead: \"Token storage\"(bd-104)\n└── Epic: \"Backend Auth\"     (bd-105)\n    ├── Bead: \"JWT endpoint\" (bd-106)\n    ├── Bead: \"Refresh token\"(bd-107)\n    └── Bead: \"Rate limiting\"(bd-108)\n\nChildren are PARALLEL by default.\nYou add explicit dependencies to force ordering.\n```\n\nEpics are good for planning but they don't enforce execution order. That's what molecules are for.\n\n**Layer 3: Molecules — The Workflow Chain**\n\nA sequence of beads linked together that an agent walks through step by step. This is the key innovation — it makes work crash-proof.\n\n```\nMolecule: \"Release Process\"\n\n  Bead A ──▶ Bead B ──▶ Bead C ──▶ Bead D ──▶ Bead E\n  \"bump      \"run       \"wait      \"deploy    \"announce\n  version\"    tests\"     for CI\"    to prod\"   release\"\n  (done)     (done)     (working)  (todo)     (todo)\n                            │\n                     agent is HERE\n                            │\n              if agent crashes, next session\n              sees C is \"open\" and picks up here\n```\n\nCompare this to your tasks.md:\n\n```\nMolecule                          Your tasks.md\n─────────────────────────────     ─────────────────────\nBead A (done)                     - [x] Step 1\nBead B (done)                     - [x] Step 2\nBead C (open)  ◄── resume here    - [ ] Step 3  ◄── resume here\nBead D (todo)                     - [ ] Step 4\nBead E (todo)                     - [ ] Step 5\n\nSame concept. Molecules just store each step as\na separate bead so ANY agent can resume it,\nnot just the one that started.\n```\n\nBut molecules can also have complex shapes — not just linear chains:\n\n```\n                 ┌──▶ Bead B ──┐\n  Bead A ────────┤             ├──▶ Bead D ──▶ Bead E\n                 └──▶ Bead C ──┘\n                   (parallel)       (gate:       (final)\n                                    wait for\n                                    B and C)\n```\n\n**Layer 4: Protomolecules — The Template**\n\nA molecule that's not \"live\" yet. It's a reusable pattern with placeholder variables. Think of it as a class vs an instance.\n\n```\nProtomolecule: \"Standard Feature Workflow\"\n\n  ${FEATURE_NAME}\n  \n  [design] ──▶ [plan] ──▶ [implement] ──▶ [review] ──▶ [test]\n     │            │            │              │            │\n  \"Design       \"Break       \"Write        \"Code        \"Write\n  ${FEATURE}\"   into tasks\"  the code\"     review\"      tests\"\n\nInstantiate with FEATURE = \"User Auth\":\n\n  [Design      ──▶ [Break       ──▶ [Write     ──▶ [Code    ──▶ [Write\n   User Auth]       into tasks]      the code]      review]      tests]\n\nNow it's a live molecule with real beads in the database.\n```\n\nYou define the template once, reuse it for every feature. Every feature goes through the same quality gates.\n\n**Layer 5: Formulas — The Source Code**\n\nProtomolecules are made of actual beads, which is clunky to compose. Formulas are TOML files that describe workflows with loops, gates, and composition. They get \"cooked\" into protomolecules.\n\n```toml\n# release.toml\n\n[meta]\nname = \"Release Process\"\n\n[[steps]]\nname = \"Bump version\"\ntype = \"task\"\n\n[[steps]]\nname = \"Run tests\"\ntype = \"task\"\n\n[[steps]]\nname = \"Wait for CI\"\ntype = \"gate\"              # pauses until CI passes\n\n[[steps]]\nname = \"Deploy\"\ntype = \"task\"\n\n[[steps]]\nname = \"Announce\"\ntype = \"task\"\n```\n\n```\nFormula (TOML)\n    │\n    │  \"cook\"\n    ▼\nProtomolecule (template beads in Git)\n    │\n    │  \"instantiate\" with variables\n    ▼\nMolecule (live workflow, agent walks through it)\n    │\n    │  composed of\n    ▼\nBeads (individual steps, each a JSON line)\n```\n\n**How the whole stack fits together:**\n\n```\nYou have an idea: \"Add user authentication\"\n    │\n    ▼\nFormula: \"standard-feature.toml\"\n    │  cook\n    ▼\nProtomolecule: design ──▶ plan ──▶ implement ──▶ review ──▶ test\n    │  instantiate with feature=\"User Auth\"\n    ▼\nMolecule: 5 live beads chained together\n    │  hung on agent's hook via gt sling\n    ▼\nAgent walks the chain:\n    Bead 1: \"Design User Auth\"        ✓ done\n    Bead 2: \"Plan User Auth\"          ✓ done\n    Bead 3: \"Implement User Auth\"     ← working\n    Bead 4: \"Review User Auth\"          todo\n    Bead 5: \"Test User Auth\"            todo\n    │\n    │  agent crashes at step 3\n    ▼\nNew session starts, reads hook, sees step 3 is open\n    → picks up where it left off\n```\n\n**What's applicable to your Stage 7 orchestrator:**\n\nYou don't need the full MEOW stack. But the lessons are:\n\n1. **Beads** — You already have this. Your tasks.md checkboxes are beads. Moving to a JSON file (state.json) just makes it machine-readable.\n\n2. **Molecules** — Your per-agent task files are simple molecules (linear chains). The key upgrade: if an agent dies mid-task, your orchestrator can reassign the same task file to a new session.\n\n3. **Protomolecules/Formulas** — This is where it gets powerful for you. If every feature should go through design → implement → review → test, define that template once. Your orchestrator generates the per-agent task file from the template. Consistent quality, every time.\n\n```\nWhat you could build:\n\ntemplates/\n├── feature.md        ← \"design, implement, review, test\"\n├── bugfix.md         ← \"reproduce, fix, test\"\n└── refactor.md       ← \"analyze, refactor, verify tests\"\n\nOrchestrator reads template + feature name\n    → generates tasks/agent-1.md with the steps filled in\n    → assigns to agent\n```\n\nThat gives you the protomolecule pattern without any of the Beads/JSONL complexity. Just markdown templates that get stamped out into agent task files.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> beads are tasks. molecules are task files right ? what are protomolecurles or furmuralse ?",
    "\n\nYeah exactly — beads = tasks, molecules = task files (ordered checklists).\n\nProtomolecules and formulas are just **templates for task files**.\n\nSay every feature you build should go through the same steps. Instead of writing a new task file from scratch each time, you have a template:\n\n```\nFORMULA (the template source):\n\n  \"Every feature must: design → plan → implement → review → test\"\n\n\nPROTOMOLECULE (the template with blanks):\n\n  # Feature: ___________\n  - [ ] Design ___________\n  - [ ] Plan ___________\n  - [ ] Implement ___________\n  - [ ] Code review ___________\n  - [ ] Write tests for ___________\n\n\nMOLECULE (filled-in task file, assigned to an agent):\n\n  # Feature: User Auth\n  - [ ] Design User Auth\n  - [ ] Plan User Auth\n  - [ ] Implement User Auth\n  - [ ] Code review User Auth\n  - [ ] Write tests for User Auth\n```\n\nThat's it. Formula is the recipe. Protomolecule is the blank form. Molecule is the filled-in form an agent actually works through.\n\nThe reason they're separate layers: formulas can have loops and conditionals (written in TOML), which get expanded (\"cooked\") into a flat protomolecule. Like a macro that expands before execution. But conceptually it's just: **template → fill in the blanks → hand to agent**.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "\n[Tool uses: glob]",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "This is really thorough. You already have something more structured than basic Gas Town molecules. Here's how your spec style guide maps to the MEOW stack:\n\n```\nMEOW Stack              Your Spec Style Guide\n──────────────          ──────────────────────────────────\nBeads                   Individual acceptance criteria + subtasks\n                          (AC 1.1, Task 2.1, etc.)\n\nMolecules               tasks.md\n                          (ordered checklist with checkpoints)\n\nProtomolecules          The SPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md itself\n                          (it's a template — every feature\n                           follows the same structure)\n\nFormulas                The document flow at the bottom\n                          (requirements → design → tasks\n                           is your \"recipe\" for all work)\n```\n\nThe big thing you have that Gas Town doesn't emphasize: **traceability**. Your chain from acceptance criteria → design properties → task subtasks → tests is stronger than Gas Town's approach. Gas Town optimizes for throughput and crash recovery. Your spec guide optimizes for correctness and code quality.\n\nFor multi-agent, this actually works in your favor. The spec files become the shared \"brain\" that keeps all agents consistent. The question is just how to split tasks.md across agents without losing the traceability. Want me to add a section to the progression doc about adapting your spec style for multi-agent?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yeah I don't want to copy gass town exactly as it is I want to decover myself what is best for my but it has great ideas. if I arrive at the same place so be it but I mith take a more taceable path with more obesrvavility I don't know is the start of the precess at this point. There are alot of cencepts there is also the concept of patrol which has different workflows there is even mentien of the SHINY which is the cannonical more porished workflow I want to understand that as well I think at this point you understand really good whot is my workflow. so I want tu understand what parts of gas town are similar to mine so I can see the similarities and which are differtn so I can learn something from them later add slowly what I think might help me",
    "\n\nGood approach — map what you already have, then see what's new. Let me do a thorough comparison.\n\n**What you already have (mapped to Gas Town concepts):**\n\n```\nYOUR WORKFLOW                          GAS TOWN EQUIVALENT\n────────────────────────────────────   ────────────────────────────────\n.kiro/specs/requirements.md            Role Bead (shared context that\n.kiro/specs/design.md                  defines how agents should behave\n                                       and what standards to follow)\n\n.kiro/specs/tasks.md                   Molecule (ordered workflow chain\n  - [ ] Task 1                         with checkboxes = bead status)\n  - [x] Task 2\n\nSPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md                    Formula / Protomolecule\n  (every feature follows the same      (template that generates\n   requirements → design → tasks       consistent workflows)\n   structure)\n\nAcceptance criteria traceability       NO EQUIVALENT — Gas Town\n  AC 1.2 → Property 3 → Task 4.1      doesn't have this. You're\n                                       ahead here.\n\ntmux sessions + tab switching          tmux sessions (same)\n\nlazygit                                Refinery (you do it manually)\n\nYou deciding what to work on next      Mayor (you are the dispatcher)\n\nYou checking if things are done        Witness (you are the monitor)\n\n/compact or new session                GUPP + Hooks (Gas Town's version\n                                       is automatic, yours is manual)\n```\n\n**What Gas Town has that you don't (yet):**\n\n```\nGAS TOWN CONCEPT        WHAT IT DOES                    DO YOU NEED IT?\n────────────────────    ──────────────────────────────   ───────────────\nPatrols                 Repeating workflow loops.        Not yet. Useful\n                        Agents run the same checklist    when you have\n                        over and over: check workers,    automated monitors\n                        process queue, clean up, sleep,  running in loops.\n                        repeat. Exponential backoff      Stage 7+.\n                        when idle.\n\n\"Shiny/Chrome\"          Named workflow variants.         YES — you already\npolecat workflows       \"Shiny\" = the polished one:      have this concept.\n                        implement → code review →         Your spec guide IS\n                        test → merge.                    the \"shiny\" workflow.\n                        \"Quick\" = just implement →        You could define a\n                        merge (skip review/test).        \"quick\" variant for\n                        Same template, different          small fixes that\n                        quality gates.                   skips design doc.\n\nConvoys                 Wraps a batch of related work    Useful at Stage 6+.\n                        into one trackable unit.         \"Add auth\" = convoy\n                        \"Is the auth feature done?\"      containing 5 tasks\n                        instead of tracking 5 separate   across 3 agents.\n                        tasks individually.              Your work-queue.md\n                                                         is a simple version.\n\nHooks                   Persistent \"current assignment\"  Stage 7. Your\n                        that survives session death.     orchestrator's\n                        Agent restarts → reads hook →    state.json would\n                        continues automatically.         serve this purpose.\n\nWisps                   Ephemeral beads that don't       Stage 7+. Useful\n                        pollute Git history.             for orchestration\n                        Patrol steps, temp work.         overhead you don't\n                        Burned after completion.         want in your repo.\n\ngt sling                One command to assign work       Stage 7. Your\n                        to an agent and start it.        orchestrator's\n                        \"sling this to a polecat\"        dispatch command.\n\ngt seance               Talk to an agent's previous      Niche. Cool but\n                        session to recover lost          not essential.\n                        context.                         /chat save covers\n                                                         most of this.\n\nMail / Messaging        Agents send messages to each     Stage 7+. Needed\n                        other through Beads inbox.       when agents need\n                        \"Hey Refinery, my MR is ready\"   to coordinate\n                                                         without you.\n\nGUPP Nudge              Poke idle agents to start        Stage 7. Your\n                        working. Workaround for          monitor component\n                        agents that wait for input       would handle this.\n                        instead of self-starting.\n```\n\n**The \"Shiny\" workflow — this is the one most relevant to you:**\n\nGas Town has named workflow tiers for polecats:\n\n```\n\"Quick\" workflow:          \"Shiny/Chrome\" workflow:\n\n  implement                  design\n      │                        │\n      ▼                        ▼\n    merge                    plan\n                               │\n                               ▼\n                           implement\n                               │\n                               ▼\n                          code review\n                               │\n                               ▼\n                             test\n                               │\n                               ▼\n                            merge\n```\n\nYour spec guide is essentially the \"shiny\" workflow — every feature gets requirements, design, implementation, tests, and traceability. But you don't have a \"quick\" variant. Sometimes a one-line CSS fix doesn't need a design doc. Having named tiers lets you match effort to task size:\n\n```\nYOUR POSSIBLE WORKFLOW TIERS:\n\n\"Full\" (your current spec guide):\n  requirements.md → design.md → tasks.md (with PBT checkpoints)\n  For: new features, complex changes\n\n\"Standard\":\n  brief requirements → tasks.md (with unit tests, no PBT)\n  For: medium features, refactors\n\n\"Quick\":\n  just a task description, implement, basic test\n  For: bug fixes, small tweaks, docs\n```\n\n**Patrols — what they actually are:**\n\nA patrol is just a checklist that runs in a loop. The Witness patrol looks like:\n\n```\nWITNESS PATROL (repeats forever):\n\n  1. Check each polecat:\n     - stuck? → nudge it\n     - dead? → restart it\n     - done? → collect its MR\n  2. Check the Refinery:\n     - MQ backed up? → hustle it\n     - stuck? → restart it\n  3. Check the Deacon:\n     - alive? → good\n     - stuck? → alert\n  4. Run rig-level plugins\n  5. Any work left? \n     - yes → start next patrol immediately\n     - no → wait (exponential backoff: 30s, 1m, 2m, 4m...)\n  6. GOTO 1\n```\n\nYou don't need patrols until you have automated monitors. But the concept is useful: a repeating checklist that an agent (or script) runs continuously. Your `watch-agents.sh` from the progression doc is a baby patrol.\n\n**The similarity map — what's close, what's different:**\n\n```\n                    SIMILAR                      DIFFERENT\n              (you already have it)        (new concept to learn from)\n             ─────────────────────────    ─────────────────────────────\nContext:      spec files = beads          agent identity persists\n              tasks.md = molecules          across sessions (yours\n              style guide = formula         doesn't)\n\nQuality:      traceability chain          Gas Town has NONE of this.\n              PBT checkpoints               You're ahead. Keep it.\n              acceptance criteria\n\nWorkflow:     sequential checklist        workflow TIERS (shiny/quick)\n              design → implement →          match effort to task size\n              test\n\nCoordination: you are the dispatcher     automated dispatch, monitoring\n              you are the monitor          agents message each other\n              you merge manually           merge queue with Refinery\n\nRecovery:     /compact, new session       automatic via GUPP + hooks\n              /chat save                    agent resumes without you\n              re-read spec files\n```\n\n**Bottom line — what to steal from Gas Town, in order of value:**\n\n1. **Workflow tiers** (quick/standard/full) — easy to add now, immediately useful\n2. **Per-agent task files** — split tasks.md when you go multi-agent\n3. **State file** (state.json) — machine-readable work tracking for your orchestrator\n4. **Hooks** — persistent \"current assignment\" that survives session death\n5. **Patrols** — automated monitoring loops (only when you build the orchestrator)\n6. **Mail** — agent-to-agent messaging (only at 10+ agents)\n\nWant me to update the progression doc with this comparison?\n[Tool uses: none]",

Here's what I added/changed:\n\n- **New \"Concepts\" section** at the top — maps everything from Gas Town to our workflow before diving into stages\n- **MEOW stack mapping** — shows how beads/molecules/protomolecules/formulas correspond to our spec files\n- **Workflow tiers** — full/standard/quick, integrated into Stage 6 onward\n- **Convoys** — feature-level tracking added to Stage 6.5\n- **Hooks** — added to the Stage 7 state.json so the orchestrator can reassign tasks after session death\n- **Workflow templates** — the protomolecule pattern for Stage 7 (templates/full.md, standard.md, quick.md)\n- **Patrol loop** — example monitor script with exponential backoff for Stage 7\n- **Similarity map** — what we have vs what's new, so you can see at a glance where to focus\n- **Adoption order** — prioritized list of what to steal from Gas Town and when\n- **Updated key principles** — added traceability, workflow tiers, and \"automate what hurts\"\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "> perfect I aded a new document. the authore apperently made a guide start off with gastown is on the docs folder as gas-town-user-manual can you read me and give me your opinions spacially on the whole thing of how to start out and compare it with what we thoght in multi-agent-progression document. specially now that you know exactly my current workflow you can have insight in what are the most logical progress from heer",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "This is really practical — much more grounded than the first article. Here's my take, especially compared to our progression plan:\n\n**Where Yegge's guide validates our plan:**\n\n- \"You technically only need the Mayor\" — this confirms our Stage 6 approach. Start with just you as the Mayor, talking to a few agents. Don't spin up the full machinery.\n- \"Bring your own workflow\" — he explicitly says Gas Town doesn't force a workflow. Our spec-driven approach with traceability is fine to keep.\n- \"It's OK not to swarm all the time\" — serial bottlenecks are normal. You don't need 10 agents running 24/7 to benefit.\n- \"Don't watch your agents work\" — this is the key Stage 6 skill. Assign work, move on, come back when they're done.\n\n**Where his guide reveals things we missed:**\n\n1. **The Crew cycling workflow is the real killer feature.** This is more important than polecats for where you are. His actual daily loop is:\n   - Cycle through crew members (`C-b n/p`)\n   - Give each one a task\n   - Let them all spin\n   - Come back, read results, act on them\n   - Repeat\n\n   This is basically Stage 6 but with a deliberate rhythm. We have the tmux setup for it, we just didn't describe the *cadence* — the loop of assign → ignore → harvest results.\n\n2. **The PR Sheriff pattern.** A crew member with a permanent standing order (a hook) that runs automatically on every session startup. This is a lightweight version of a patrol that doesn't need an orchestrator. You could do this right now — give one of your 3 agents a permanent task file that says \"on startup, check X and do Y.\"\n\n3. **Code review sweeps as a workflow.** He runs review swarms that *generate* work (file bugs), then fix swarms that *consume* that work. This is a two-phase pattern we didn't account for. In our terms:\n   ```\n   Phase 1: agent reviews code → files issues in work-queue.md\n   Phase 2: agents pick up those issues and fix them\n   ```\n\n4. **Heresies — wrong patterns spreading through the codebase.** This is the biggest risk of multi-agent that we didn't address. When multiple agents work in parallel, one makes a wrong assumption, it gets into the code, and other agents copy it. His solution: capture guiding principles in agent priming. Our design.md already does this, but we should be more deliberate about it — add a \"principles\" or \"anti-patterns\" section that agents read.\n\n5. **Handoff liberally.** He says don't let sessions run long unless they're accumulating important context. Short sessions, frequent handoffs. This is different from our current approach where we let sessions run until context fills up.\n\n**Where our plan is actually better than his advice:**\n\n- **Traceability.** He says \"tend the invisible garden\" with code review sweeps because you can't see the code. We *can* verify quality structurally through our AC → property → task → test chain. We don't need to hope the garden is healthy — we can prove it.\n- **Workflow tiers.** He doesn't mention this explicitly. Everything seems to go through the same process. Our full/standard/quick tiers are more efficient.\n- **Gradual progression.** His guide assumes you're jumping into Gas Town. Our staged approach (5 → 6 → 6.5 → 7) is safer and builds understanding incrementally.\n\n**What I'd update in our progression doc based on this:**\n\n1. Add the **crew cycling cadence** to Stage 6 — the assign-all → ignore → harvest loop\n2. Add the **PR Sheriff / standing orders** pattern to Stage 6.5 — a permanent task file that runs on every session\n3. Add **review sweep → fix sweep** as a two-phase workflow pattern\n4. Add a **principles/anti-patterns section** to the shared context files to prevent heresies\n5. Emphasize **short sessions + frequent handoffs** over long-running sessions\n\nWant me to update the progression doc with these additions?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "\n\nWe read and cleaned up Steve Yegge's Gas Town article (removed emojis, added markdown headers), then had a deep dive into its concepts:\n\n1. **7 worker roles** — built ASCII diagrams explaining Mayor, Crew, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs, Boot and how they interact\n2. **MEOW stack** — mapped beads (tasks), molecules (task files), protomolecules (templates), formulas (recipes) to our existing spec-driven workflow\n3. **Compared Gas Town to our Kiro CLI workflow** — found we already have equivalents for most concepts through spec files, tasks.md, and SPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md, with traceability being our advantage\n4. **Built a personal progression plan** — created `docs/multi-agent-progression.md` mapping Stage 5 (current: single agent, tmux windows) through Stage 6 (3-5 parallel agents), Stage 6.5 (conventions), Stage 7 (custom orchestrator), to Stage 8 (Gas Town)\n5. **Read the Gas Town User Manual** — discovered the crew cycling cadence, PR Sheriff pattern, review/fix sweep workflows, and heresy prevention as new concepts to incorporate\n\nThe core approach: don't copy Gas Town wholesale, evolve gradually from our current workflow, keep our traceability advantage, and automate only what hurts.\n[Tool uses: none]",

Start with just you as the Mayor, talking to a few agents. Don't spin up the full machinery.\n- \"Bring your own workflow\" — he explicitly says Gas Town doesn't force a workflow. His actual daily loop is:\n   - Cycle through crew members (`C-b n/p`)\n   - Give each one a task\n   - Let them all spin\n   - Come back, read results, act on them\n   - Repeat\n\n   This is basically Stage 6 but with a deliberate rhythm. This is a lightweight version of a patrol that doesn't need an orchestrator. You could do this right now — give one of your 3 agents a permanent task file that says \"on startup, check X and do Y.\"\n\n3. **Code review sweeps as a workflow.** He runs review swarms that *generate* work (file bugs), then fix swarms that *consume* that work. Short sessions, frequent handoffs. This is different from our current approach where we let sessions run until context fills up.\n\n**Where our plan is actually better than his advice:**\n\n- **Traceability.** He says \"tend the invisible garden\" with code review sweeps because you can't see the code. We *can* verify quality structurally through our AC → property → task → test chain. Add the **PR Sheriff / standing orders** pattern to Stage 6.5 — a permanent task file that runs on every session\n3. Add **review sweep → fix sweep** as a two-phase workflow pattern\n4.
