    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Done. Here's what I added:\n\n**Stage 6 — two new sections:**\n- **Crew cycling cadence** — the assign → ignore → harvest loop with the discipline of reading agent output before moving on\n- **Session hygiene** — short sessions with frequent handoffs instead of running until context fills up\n\n**Stage 6.5 — three new sections:**\n- **Principles file** (`principles.md`) — shared guardrails and anti-patterns to prevent heresies spreading across agents\n- **Standing orders / PR Sheriff pattern** — one agent with a permanent task that runs health checks on every session startup\n- **Review sweep / fix sweep workflow** — two-phase pattern where one agent finds problems and others fix them\n\n**Key Principles — three new entries:**\n- Short sessions, frequent handoffs\n- Assign, ignore, harvest rhythm\n- Guard against heresies\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "\n\nWe studied Steve Yegge's Gas Town articles and built a personal multi-agent development roadmap:\n\n1. **Cleaned up the Gas Town article** — removed emojis, added markdown headers\n2. **Deep-dived Gas Town concepts** — 7 worker roles (ASCII diagrams), MEOW stack (beads/molecules/protomolecules/formulas), GUPP, patrols, convoys\n3. **Mapped Gas Town to our existing workflow** — found our spec files, tasks.md, and SPEC_STYLE_GUIDE.md already cover most MEOW layers, with traceability as our unique advantage\n4. **Created `docs/multi-agent-progression.md`** — staged roadmap from Stage 5 (current: single agent, tmux windows) through Stage 6 (3-5 parallel agents), Stage 6.5 (conventions), Stage 7 (custom orchestrator), to Stage 8 (Gas Town)\n5. **Analyzed the Gas Town User Manual** — discovered crew cycling cadence, PR Sheriff pattern, review/fix sweeps, heresy prevention, short session discipline\n6. **Updated the progression doc** with those findings — added the assign-ignore-harvest loop, principles.md for anti-patterns, standing orders pattern, review/fix sweep workflow, session hygiene, and 3 new key principles\n\nCore approach: evolve gradually from our current workflow, keep traceability, automate only what hurts, and learn from Gas Town without copying it wholesale.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "\n\nWe studied Steve Yegge's Gas Town articles and built a personal multi-agent development roadmap:\n\n1. **Cleaned up the Gas Town article** (`gastown.md`) — removed emojis, added markdown headers\n2. **Deep-dived Gas Town concepts** — 7 worker roles with ASCII diagrams (Mayor, Crew, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs/Boot), MEOW stack, GUPP, patrols, convoys\n3. **Mapped Gas Town to our existing Kiro CLI workflow** — spec files = role beads, tasks.md = molecules, SPEC_STYLE_GUIDE = formulas/protomolecules, with traceability (AC → properties → tasks → tests) as our unique advantage over Gas Town\n4. **Created `docs/multi-agent-progression.md`** — staged roadmap: Stage 5 (current: single agent, tmux windows) → Stage 6 (3-5 parallel agents, per-agent task files, workflow tiers) → Stage 6.5 (conventions, convoys, principles file, standing orders) → Stage 7 (custom orchestrator with dispatcher/monitor/merger) → Stage 8 (Gas Town)\n5. **Analyzed the Gas Town User Manual** — extracted crew cycling cadence (assign → ignore → harvest), PR Sheriff pattern, review/fix sweep workflow, heresy prevention, short session discipline\n6. **Updated progression doc** with all findings — 8 key principles, workflow templates, patrol loops, context preservation comparison across approaches\n\nCore approach: evolve gradually, keep our traceability advantage, automate only what hurts, learn from Gas Town without copying it wholesale.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yeah so lets continue this conversation",
    "\n\nSure! Where do you want to pick up? A few directions we could go:\n\n- **Start implementing Stage 6** — update your `tmux-session.sh` to spin up 3 agents, create the per-agent task files structure, and try the crew cycling cadence on your chip8 project\n- **Create the principles.md** file for your chip8 project so it's ready when you go multi-agent\n- **Dig deeper into a Gas Town concept** we haven't fully explored yet (convoys, wisps, formulas in TOML, the Mol Mall idea)\n- **Start designing your Stage 7 orchestrator** — pick a language, sketch the dispatcher, figure out the state file format\n- **Something else entirely** — maybe you've been thinking about this over the last couple days and have new ideas\n\nWhat's on your mind?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yea lets tas kbaotu stage 6 and 6.5. right now like you know and if you don't you can check the tmux file in the root .but I have 1-2 agents in difeerent tmux windews tabs. but I think if we or going to do 3-6 we need tabs to have mulmiple agents and to somewhat comunicat throught the \"mayor\" plits where the agenst are tasking to themselves in some way. not sure if I explain miself. maybe a ascii diagram of how the different tabs and splits look like ?",
    "\n\nI get what you're saying — with 3-6 agents, you need a way to see multiple agents at once AND have a \"command center\" where you dispatch work. Let me sketch some layouts:\n\n**Option A: Mayor window with agent splits**\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim\nWindow 2: MAYOR (you dispatch from here)\nWindow 3: agents (split view)\nWindow 4: lazygit\nWindow 5: terminal\n\nWindow 2 — Mayor:\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                                         │\n│  kiro-cli (your \"mayor\" session)        │\n│  This is where you plan, assign work,   │\n│  update work-queue.md, read reports     │\n│                                         │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\nWindow 3 — Agents (horizontal splits):\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│  agent-1 (kiro-cli)                     │\n├─────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│  agent-2 (kiro-cli)                     │\n├─────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│  agent-3 (kiro-cli)                     │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\nProblem: 3 splits gets cramped. You can't read much in each pane.\n\n**Option B: Agents in separate windows, Mayor as home base**\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim\nWindow 2: mayor (your command center)\nWindow 3: agent-1\nWindow 4: agent-2\nWindow 5: agent-3\nWindow 6: lazygit\nWindow 7: terminal\n\nYou cycle agents with C-b 3/4/5\nMayor is always C-b 2\n```\n\nThis is what you're already doing but with a dedicated mayor window. Simple, but you can't glance at multiple agents at once.\n\n**Option C: Hybrid — Mayor + dashboard split, agents in tabs**\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim\nWindow 2: command center (split)\nWindow 3: agent-1\nWindow 4: agent-2\nWindow 5: agent-3\nWindow 6: lazygit\n\nWindow 2 — Command Center:\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                                         │\n│  kiro-cli (mayor session)               │\n│  Plan, dispatch, review                 │\n│                                         │\n├─────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│  $ watch cat .kiro/specs/work-queue.md  │\n│                                         │\n│  ACTIVE:                                │\n│  [agent-1] Auth endpoints    WORKING    │\n│  [agent-2] Fix cache bug     WORKING    │\n│  [agent-3] Write tests       DONE       │\n│                                         │\n│  UP NEXT:                               │\n│  - WebSocket support                    │\n│  - API docs                             │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\nThe bottom pane is a live dashboard — just `watch` on your work-queue file. You always know what's happening without leaving the mayor window.\n\n**Option D: What I'd actually recommend for you**\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim\nWindow 2: mayor + dashboard (split)\nWindow 3: agent pair (2 panes, vertical split)\nWindow 4: agent pair (2 panes, vertical split)  ← only if 4+ agents\nWindow 5: lazygit\nWindow 6: terminal\n\nWindow 2 — Mayor + Dashboard:\n┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐\n│                      │                  │\n│  kiro-cli            │  live dashboard  │\n│  (mayor)             │  work-queue.md   │\n│                      │  agent status    │\n│                      │                  │\n└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘\n\nWindow 3 — Agent Pair:\n┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐\n│                      │                  │\n│  agent-1             │  agent-2         │\n│  (kiro-cli)          │  (kiro-cli)      │\n│                      │                  │\n│                      │                  │\n└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘\n\nWindow 4 — Agent Pair (when needed):\n┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐\n│                      │                  │\n│  agent-3             │  agent-4         │\n│  (kiro-cli)          │  (kiro-cli)      │\n│                      │                  │\n│                      │                  │\n└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘\n```\n\nWhy this works:\n- **Mayor is separate** — your command center, never cluttered by agent output\n- **Dashboard is always visible** next to the mayor — you see status at a glance\n- **Agent pairs** let you glance at two agents side by side without being too cramped\n- **You scale by adding windows** — 3 agents = window 3 has a pair + one solo. 6 agents = 3 pair windows.\n- Agents \"communicate\" through the mayor — you read agent-1's output, switch to mayor, update work-queue.md or assign new work, switch to agent-2\n\nThe \"communication\" between agents isn't direct — it flows through you and the shared files:\n\n```\nagent-1 finishes ──> you read output in window 3\n    │\n    v\nyou switch to mayor (window 2) ──> update work-queue.md\n    │                               assign next task\n    v\nyou switch to agent-1 ──> give it new work\n    or\nyou switch to agent-3 ──> \"agent-1 finished auth,\n                            you can now write tests for it\"\n```\n\nWant me to write the updated tmux-session.sh for one of these layouts?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey so they communicate throught the files system. they don't send messages thrught each other they write. this is cool ends up working like a poor mans queue of some sort. without concurency protections. how do we ping so that agents donat stop get context filled up or simply die. in the gastown.md articol it talks about htis the pinging sstem and actually there are ther agests that do that. I want a simpler version that still works. I want to probably code it myself so as to lern how it works deeply. well code it with help of the agent of course. what do you think of my thoguhts.",
    "\n\nYeah exactly — the filesystem is the message bus. Agents write, other agents read. No concurrency protection needed at Stage 6 because you're the router — you read agent-1's output and manually tell agent-3 about it. At Stage 7 the orchestrator becomes the router.\n\nOn the pinging/nudging problem — this is one of the most practical things to solve early. In Gas Town, the whole Deacon → Boot → Witness hierarchy exists just to answer: \"is this agent still alive and working?\" That's a lot of machinery. Here's a simpler version you could build:\n\n**The problem, concretely:**\n\n```\nAgent states you need to detect:\n\n1. WORKING    — agent is actively doing stuff (leave it alone)\n2. IDLE       — agent finished, waiting for input (assign new work)\n3. STUCK      — agent hit an error or is going in circles (nudge it)\n4. DEAD       — context filled up, session crashed (restart it)\n5. COMPACTED  — agent auto-compacted, lost context (re-prime it)\n```\n\n**The simplest possible monitor — a heartbeat file:**\n\nEach agent writes a timestamp to a file periodically. A watcher script checks the timestamps.\n\n```\n.orchestra/\n├── heartbeats/\n│   ├── agent-1.txt    ← \"2026-02-28T19:45:00 WORKING auth endpoint\"\n│   ├── agent-2.txt    ← \"2026-02-28T19:32:00 IDLE\"\n│   └── agent-3.txt    ← \"2026-02-28T19:10:00 WORKING tests\"\n│                          ↑ 40 min old = probably dead\n└── watcher.sh\n```\n\nThe problem: kiro-cli agents don't automatically write heartbeat files. You'd need to tell each agent \"update your heartbeat file every time you complete a subtask.\" That's fragile — they'll forget.\n\n**A more reliable approach — tmux-based detection:**\n\nYou don't need agents to cooperate. You can detect their state from outside by watching the tmux pane:\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\n# watcher.sh — poor man's Witness\n\nwhile true; do\n    clear\n    echo \"=== AGENT STATUS $(date) ===\"\n    echo \"\"\n\n    for pane in $(tmux list-panes -a -F '#{session_name}:#{window_name}' | grep agent); do\n        # Get last line of output in the pane\n        last_line=$(tmux capture-pane -t \"$pane\" -p | tail -5 | head -1)\n\n        # Check if pane has had new output recently\n        # (compare captured content to last check)\n        current=$(tmux capture-pane -t \"$pane\" -p | md5)\n        previous=$(cat \"/tmp/pane_${pane//[:\\/]/_}.md5\" 2>/dev/null)\n        echo \"$current\" > \"/tmp/pane_${pane//[:\\/]/_}.md5\"\n\n        if [ \"$current\" = \"$previous\" ]; then\n            age=$(($(date +%s) - $(stat -f %m \"/tmp/pane_${pane//[:\\/]/_}.md5\")))\n            if [ $age -gt 300 ]; then\n                status=\"DEAD (no output for 5+ min)\"\n            else\n                status=\"IDLE (waiting for input)\"\n            fi\n        else\n            status=\"WORKING\"\n        fi\n\n        echo \"  $pane: $status\"\n        echo \"  last: $last_line\"\n        echo \"\"\n    done\n\n    sleep 30\ndone\n```\n\nThis watches tmux panes from outside — no agent cooperation needed. If a pane's content hasn't changed in a while, the agent is either idle or dead.\n\n**The nudge — sending input to a stuck/idle agent:**\n\nGas Town's `gt nudge` is basically `tmux send-keys`. You can do the same:\n\n```bash\n# Send a message to an agent's tmux pane\ntmux send-keys -t \"chip8:agent-1\" \"Continue working on your current task from tasks/agent-1.md\" Enter\n```\n\nThat's it. That's the nudge. The agent receives it as if you typed it.\n\n**Putting it together — what you'd build:**\n\n```\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│  watcher.sh (runs in your dashboard pane)    │\n│                                              │\n│  Every 30 seconds:                           │\n│  1. Check each agent's tmux pane             │\n│  2. Detect: WORKING / IDLE / DEAD            │\n│  3. If IDLE > 2 min:                         │\n│     → nudge: \"check tasks/agent-N.md\"        │\n│  4. If DEAD > 5 min:                         │\n│     → kill pane, restart kiro-cli            │\n│     → send: \"resume from tasks/agent-N.md\"   │\n│  5. Display status dashboard                 │\n└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n**What I think of your approach:**\n\nBuilding this yourself is the right call. Here's why:\n\n- It's a ~100 line bash script to start. Small enough to understand completely.\n- You'll learn exactly why Gas Town needs the Deacon/Witness/Boot hierarchy — because the simple version breaks in specific ways that force you to add layers.\n- The failure modes are educational: What happens when you nudge an agent that's actually thinking? What if the restart loses uncommitted work? What if two agents edit the same file? You'll discover these naturally.\n- You can iterate: start with just detection (watcher), add nudging later, add auto-restart after that.\n\n**Build order I'd suggest:**\n\n```\nDay 1:  Detection only — watcher.sh that shows agent status\nDay 2:  Manual nudge — a helper script you run by hand\nDay 3:  Auto-nudge — watcher nudges idle agents automatically\nDay 4:  Auto-restart — watcher restarts dead agents with their task file\nDay 5+: Edge cases — handle compaction, lost context, file conflicts\n```\n\nStart with detection. Just seeing which agents are idle vs working vs dead is already a huge upgrade. The automation can come after you've manually nudged agents enough times to understand the patterns.\n\nWant to start with the watcher script, or do you want to think about the design more first?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey you already know what I want i think I want a smaller simpler beginner version of gastown that handles a small amount of agents as a learning exercise and seeing what works for me. you alrady know  the tools that are probably best and the concepts that are somewhat proven (not really but is the best we got). so lets create some sort of design document. with architectur diagrams and some implomentation details. I have a prefernce for go, typscript (if needed) or python(if needed). if you have any questions feel free to ask",
    "\n\nA few questions before I draft the design:\n\n1. **Scope** — Are we targeting 3-6 agents (Stage 6) or 10+ (Stage 7)? I'm thinking we design for 3-6 but make it extensible to 10+.\n\n2. **Where does this tool live?** — Separate repo, or inside your chip8 project as a side project? I'd suggest a separate repo since it's a general-purpose tool.\n\n3. **CLI or daemon?** — Two approaches:\n   - **CLI commands** (like Gas Town's `gt`): you run `orch start`, `orch status`, `orch sling agent-1 \"fix the bug\"`, etc. Simpler, you're always in control.\n   - **Background daemon** that watches and nudges automatically, plus CLI commands to interact with it. More complex but closer to Gas Town.\n   - Or start with CLI-only and add the daemon later?\n\n4. **Agent tool** — Is it always kiro-cli, or do you want it to be agent-agnostic (could work with Claude Code, etc. later)?\n\n5. **State storage** — JSON file in the project repo (like we discussed), or a central SQLite db in the tool's own directory? JSON-in-repo is simpler and more Gas Town-like. SQLite is better for querying across multiple projects.\n\n6. **Name?** — Got a preference? Keeps things fun and makes it easier to reference in docs/CLI.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> /model",

",
    "\n\nSome name ideas:\n\n- **herd** — you're herding agents. `herd start`, `herd status`, `herd sling`\n- **forge** — a workshop where agents work. `forge start`, `forge status`\n- **crew** — straightforward. `crew start`, `crew assign`\n- **swarm** — what the agents do. `swarm start`, `swarm status`\n- **loom** — weaving threads of work together. `loom start`, `loom status`\n- **pit** — like a pit crew, short and punchy. `pit start`, `pit status`\n- **mux** — plays on tmux, multiplexing agents. `mux start`, `mux status`\n\nPick one (or suggest your own) and I'll draft the design doc.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> im between swarm or pit sound cool. something tolkien or doom related. im fan of those series so if is something referenc to that migt be cool",
    "\n\nTolkien-flavored:\n\n- **shire** — where the work gets done quietly. `shire start`, `shire status`\n- **palantir** — the seeing stones, you oversee all agents. `palantir start` (but there's a company with that name)\n- **moria** — the mines, agents dig through work. `moria start`, `moria sling`\n- **ent** — the shepherds of the forest, managing workers. `ent start`, `ent status`\n- **nazgul** — nine agents hunting tasks. `nazgul start`, `nazgul sling`\n- **uruk** — the efficient orc soldiers. `uruk start`, `uruk status`\n\nDoom-flavored:\n\n- **slayer** — ripping through tasks. `slayer start`, `slayer sling`\n- **argent** — the Argent energy that powers everything (also sounds like \"agent\"). `argent start`, `argent status`\n- **vega** — the AI that helps the Doom Slayer. `vega start`, `vega sling`\n- **sentinel** — the Night Sentinels. `sentinel start`, `sentinel status`\n\nMy favorites:\n\n- **argent** — it's a Doom reference AND sounds like \"agent\". Perfect double meaning. Short, punchy CLI commands.\n- **moria** — agents mining through work in the deep. Has a nice ring to it.\n- **vega** — the AI assistant angle fits perfectly for an agent orchestrator.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> net doom dune sory",
    "\n\nDune-flavored:\n\n- **sietch** — the Fremen hideout where work gets done. `sietch start`, `sietch status`\n- **spice** — the substance that makes everything flow. `spice start`, `spice sling`\n- **worm** — the sandworms, powerful and relentless. `worm start`, `worm status`\n- **crysknife** — the Fremen weapon, short: **crys**. `crys start`, `crys sling`\n- **fremkit** — Fremen survival kit. `fremkit start`, `fremkit status`\n- **muaddib** — the one who commands. `muaddib start`, `muaddib sling`\n- **mentat** — the human computers that process and advise. `mentat start`, `mentat status`\n- **sardaukar** — the elite soldiers executing tasks. `sard start`, `sard sling`\n- **stillsuit** — captures every drop of work, nothing wasted. `still start`, `still status`\n\nTolkien + Dune combos:\n\n- **ent** (Tolkien) — still my favorite from that list. Short, memorable.\n- **mentat** (Dune) — human computers orchestrating work. Fits perfectly for an AI agent orchestrator.\n- **spice** (Dune) — \"the spice must flow\" = \"the work must flow\". Very Gas Town energy.\n\nMy top pick: **mentat**. The Mentats are literally human computers that process information and coordinate — that's exactly what this tool does. Plus `mentat start`, `mentat status`, `mentat sling` all sound clean.\n\nRunner up: **spice**. Short, fun, and \"the spice must flow\" is basically GUPP.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "> y",
    "No conflicts. `crys` doesn't exist as any known CLI tool. The only thing close is `cryosparc` (cryo-electron microscopy software) which uses `cryosparcm` as its command, not `crys`. And Crysis the game uses `crysis` not `crys`.\n\nYou're clear — `crys` is all yours. Go for it.\n\nI'll start drafting the design doc now.\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "> y",
    "Done. Created `docs/crys-design.md`. Here's what's in it:\n\n- **Architecture diagram** — how you, crys CLI, state.json, tmux, and project files connect\n- **8 CLI commands** — `init`, `start`, `stop`, `status`, `sling`, `queue`, `watch`, `nudge`, `convoy`\n- **State file spec** — full JSON schema for agents, queue, and convoys\n- **3 workflow templates** — full, standard, quick tiers with variable substitution\n- **Agent detection** — tmux pane content diffing to detect working/idle/dead without agent cooperation\n- **Nudge and restart sequences** — exactly what gets sent to tmux and when\n- **Go project structure** — packages for cmd, state, tmux, monitor, template\n- **4 implementation phases** — foundation → work assignment → monitoring → tracking\n- **Gas Town mapping** — how every Crys concept maps back to Gas Town\n- **Explicit non-goals** — what Crys intentionally doesn't do\n\nTake a look and let me know what you'd change or what needs more detail before you start building.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "Clean. Every \"Crys\" in the doc is now either:\n- **Crysknife** — the project name in prose\n- **crys** — the CLI command in code blocks and backticks\n\nFile renamed to `docs/crysknife-design.md` as well.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> thank you. another natural quentios how I strucutre design and task file is quite linear you need to do one thing and then another. is single agent sort of based. if ym going to be using 6 at the same time I need to do my planning a littel bit different more in a pararel sort of way. not ser how is that done any thought in that. have we thought of that part. how to structure a project or a gol where the exectuion of some of the tasks are paralel. im going to need to create branches and merge. and deal with that as well probably",
    "\n\nGreat observation — this is a real gap. Your current tasks.md is a linear chain: step 1 → step 2 → step 3. That works for one agent but wastes 5 agents sitting idle waiting for step 1 to finish.\n\nYou need to think about work as a **dependency graph**, not a list. Some tasks can run in parallel, others have to wait. This is exactly what Gas Town's molecules with branching do:\n\n```\nLINEAR (current — single agent):\n\n  Task 1 → Task 2 → Task 3 → Task 4 → Task 5 → Task 6\n\n\nPARALLEL (multi-agent — dependency graph):\n\n  Task 1 (design) ─────────────────────────────────┐\n       │                                            │\n       ├──→ Task 2 (auth backend)    ──┐            │\n       │                               │            │\n       ├──→ Task 3 (auth frontend)  ──┤            │\n       │                               ├──→ Task 6 (integration tests)\n       ├──→ Task 4 (database models) ──┘\n       │\n       └──→ Task 5 (API docs)       ──────→ (independent, no blocker)\n\n  Tasks 2, 3, 4, 5 can ALL run in parallel after Task 1\n  Task 6 waits for 2, 3, 4 to finish\n```\n\nThe key question is: how do you express this in your planning files so Crysknife can assign work correctly?\n\nHere's how I'd structure it. Instead of a single tasks.md, you'd have a **project plan** that defines phases:\n\n```markdown\n# .kiro/specs/plan.md\n\n## Feature: User Authentication\n\n### Phase 1 — Design (sequential, 1 agent)\n- [ ] Design auth system architecture\n- [ ] Write requirements.md and design.md\n- Agents: 1\n- Blocks: Phase 2, Phase 3\n\n### Phase 2 — Implementation (parallel, 3 agents)\n- [ ] Task A: Auth backend (JWT, refresh tokens)\n      Area: src/auth/\n      Branch: feat/auth-backend\n      Tier: full\n\n- [ ] Task B: Auth frontend (login form, token storage)\n      Area: src/components/auth/\n      Branch: feat/auth-frontend\n      Tier: full\n\n- [ ] Task C: Database models and migrations\n      Area: src/models/\n      Branch: feat/auth-models\n      Tier: standard\n\n- Agents: 3 (one per task)\n- Requires: Phase 1 complete\n- Blocks: Phase 4\n\n### Phase 3 — Independent (parallel with Phase 2, 1 agent)\n- [ ] Task D: API documentation\n      Area: docs/\n      Branch: feat/auth-docs\n      Tier: quick\n\n- Agents: 1\n- Requires: Phase 1 complete\n- Blocks: nothing\n\n### Phase 4 — Integration (sequential after merge, 1-2 agents)\n- [ ] Merge all Phase 2 branches to main\n- [ ] Integration tests across auth system\n- [ ] Fix any merge conflicts or integration bugs\n\n- Agents: 1-2\n- Requires: Phase 2 complete + merged\n```\n\nThe flow looks like this:\n\n```\n         Phase 1 (1 agent, sequential)\n         Design\n            │\n     ┌──────┼──────────┐\n     v      v          v\n  Phase 2          Phase 3\n  (3 agents,       (1 agent,\n   parallel)        parallel)\n     │                 │\n     ├─ Task A ────┐   │\n     │  backend    │   │\n     │             │   │\n     ├─ Task B ────┤   └─ Task D\n     │  frontend   │      docs\n     │             │      (independent)\n     ├─ Task C ────┤\n     │  models     │\n     │             │\n     └─────────────┘\n            │\n         MERGE (you + lazygit)\n            │\n         Phase 4 (1-2 agents)\n         Integration tests\n```\n\n**The branching and merging strategy:**\n\n```\nmain ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────→\n  │                                                    ↑\n  │  Phase 1                                          │\n  ├──→ feat/auth-design ──→ merge to main             │\n  │                           │                        │\n  │  Phase 2 (branch from main after Phase 1 merge)   │\n  ├──→ feat/auth-backend  ──┐                          │\n  ├──→ feat/auth-frontend ──┼──→ merge one at a time ──┘\n  ├──→ feat/auth-models   ──┘    (you are the Refinery)\n  │\n  │  Phase 3 (branches from main, independent)\n  └──→ feat/auth-docs ──→ merge anytime\n```\n\nMerge order matters. You merge one branch at a time to main:\n1. Merge `feat/auth-models` first (least likely to conflict)\n2. Merge `feat/auth-backend` (may need to rebase on models)\n3. Merge `feat/auth-frontend` (may need to rebase on backend)\n4. Merge `feat/auth-docs` (independent, merge whenever)\n\n**How Crysknife would handle this:**\n\nThe `crys sling` command already assigns one task to one agent. The new piece is `plan.md` awareness — knowing which tasks can run now vs which are blocked:\n\n```\n$ crys status\n\n  PHASE     STATUS       TASKS\n  Phase 1   DONE         Design complete, merged\n  Phase 2   IN PROGRESS  3 tasks running in parallel\n  Phase 3   IN PROGRESS  1 task running\n  Phase 4   BLOCKED      Waiting for Phase 2\n\n  AGENT     STATUS    TASK                    BRANCH\n  agent-1   WORKING   Auth backend            feat/auth-backend\n  agent-2   WORKING   Auth frontend           feat/auth-frontend\n  agent-3   WORKING   Database models         feat/auth-models\n  agent-4   WORKING   API docs                feat/auth-docs\n  agent-5   IDLE      —                       —\n  agent-6   IDLE      —                       —\n\n  MERGE QUEUE:\n  (none ready — Phase 2 tasks still in progress)\n```\n\nWhen agent-3 finishes:\n\n```\n$ crys status\n\n  agent-3   DONE      Database models         feat/auth-models\n\n  MERGE QUEUE:\n  - feat/auth-models (ready to merge)\n```\n\nYou merge it with lazygit, then either:\n- Assign agent-3 something from the queue\n- Or let it sit idle until Phase 4\n\n**What to add to the Crysknife design:**\n\nI'd add a `plan.md` spec to the design doc — a simple format for expressing phases with dependencies. Crysknife doesn't need to be smart about it. It just needs to:\n\n1. Read plan.md to know which phase is current\n2. Show which tasks are runnable vs blocked in `crys status`\n3. Let you sling all parallel tasks at once: `crys sling --phase 2`\n\nThe dependency resolution stays simple — phases are sequential, tasks within a phase are parallel. No complex DAG solver needed.\n\nWant me to add this to the Crysknife design doc?\n[Tool uses: none]",

I think and resolve the conflicts.",
    "\n\nYeah that makes total sense. With 6 agents producing branches, you become the bottleneck if you're merging manually. That's exactly why Gas Town has the Refinery as a dedicated role.\n\nHere's how I see it working — you'd have 3 types of agents instead of treating them all the same:\n\n```\nYOUR AGENT ROLES:\n\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                                                      │\n│  MAYOR (1 agent — your command center)               │\n│  - Updates plan.md, design.md, tasks                 │\n│  - Reads feedback from workers and merger            │\n│  - Decides what to do next                           │\n│  - You talk to this one the most                     │\n│                                                      │\n├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│                                                      │\n│  WORKERS (3-4 agents — do the actual coding)         │\n│  - Each gets a task file + branch + area             │\n│  - Code, test, commit to their branch                │\n│  - When done: write summary in their task file       │\n│  - Don't touch main, don't merge                     │\n│                                                      │\n├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│                                                      │\n│  MERGER (1 agent — the Refinery)                     │\n│  - Watches for completed worker branches             │\n│  - Merges them to main one at a time                 │\n│  - Resolves conflicts                                │\n│  - Writes merge report: what conflicted, what        │\n│    it changed, any concerns                          │\n│  - Flags issues back to you / mayor                  │\n│                                                      │\n└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\nThe feedback loop:\n\n```\n                    ┌─────────┐\n          ┌────────│  MAYOR  │◄────────────────────┐\n          │        │ (plans) │                      │\n          │        └────┬────┘                      │\n          │             │                           │\n          │  updates    │ assigns tasks             │ merge reports\n          │  plan.md    │                           │ + feedback\n          │             v                           │\n          │   ┌──────────────────┐           ┌─────┴──────┐\n          │   │     WORKERS      │           │   MERGER   │\n          │   │                  │  done     │            │\n          │   │  agent-1 ──────────────────►│  merges    │\n          │   │  agent-2 ──────────────────►│  one at    │\n          │   │  agent-3 ──────────────────►│  a time    │\n          │   │  agent-4 ──────────────────►│  to main   │\n          │   │                  │           │            │\n          │   └──────────────────┘           └────────────┘\n          │         │                              │\n          │         │ worker feedback               │\n          │         │ \"I found X doesn't work\"      │\n          │         │ \"design.md is wrong about Y\"  │\n          └─────────┘                              │\n                                                   │\n          merger feedback:                         │\n          \"auth-backend conflicted with models\"    │\n          \"had to refactor the interface\"          │\n          \"suggest updating design.md section 3\"───┘\n```\n\n**How the merger agent works in practice:**\n\nThe merger gets a permanent task file (like the PR Sheriff pattern):\n\n```markdown\n# .kiro/specs/tasks/merger.md\n\n## Standing Orders\n\nYou are the Merger. Your job is to merge completed worker branches\nto main, one at a time, in the correct order.\n\n## Process\n1. Check .crysknife/state.json for agents with status \"done\"\n2. Pick the branch least likely to conflict (smallest diff first)\n3. Checkout main, pull latest\n4. Merge the branch (or rebase if cleaner)\n5. If conflicts:\n   - Resolve them intelligently using design.md as reference\n   - Document what you changed in the merge report\n6. Run tests after merge\n7. If tests pass: push to main, update state.json\n8. If tests fail: document the failure, flag for mayor\n9. Write merge report below\n10. Move to next completed branch\n\n## Rules\n- NEVER merge two branches at the same time\n- ALWAYS run tests after each merge\n- If a conflict is too complex, flag it — don't guess\n- Read design.md and principles.md to resolve conflicts correctly\n\n## Merge Reports\n(merger writes here after each merge)\n\n### feat/auth-models — merged 2026-02-28 20:30\n- Clean merge, no conflicts\n- Tests pass\n\n### feat/auth-backend — merged 2026-02-28 20:35\n- Conflict in src/auth/handler.go (both backend and models\n  defined the User struct differently)\n- Resolved: kept models version, updated backend imports\n- Tests pass\n- NOTE: design.md section 3.2 should be updated to clarify\n  User struct ownership — flagging for mayor\n```\n\n**The tmux layout with roles:**\n\n```\nWindow 1: nvim\nWindow 2: mayor + dashboard (split)\n┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐\n│  kiro-cli (mayor)    │  crys watch      │\n│                      │  dashboard       │\n└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘\n\nWindow 3: workers (split)\n┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐\n│  worker-1            │  worker-2        │\n└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘\n\nWindow 4: workers (split)\n┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────┐\n│  worker-3            │  worker-4        │\n└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┘\n\nWindow 5: merger\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│  kiro-cli (merger agent)                │\n│  dedicated to merging + conflict        │\n│  resolution                             │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\nWindow 6: lazygit (for when YOU need to intervene)\nWindow 7: terminal\n```\n\n**On pre-planned vs on-the-fly:**\n\nBoth. The phases in plan.md are pre-planned — you know upfront that \"auth backend\" and \"auth frontend\" can run in parallel. But the merge order is on-the-fly — whoever finishes first gets merged first. And feedback from workers and the merger can change the plan mid-flight:\n\n```\nPre-planned:\n  \"Phase 2 has 4 parallel tasks\"\n\nOn-the-fly:\n  Worker-2 says: \"The API design in design.md won't work,\n  we need a different approach for token refresh\"\n      │\n      v\n  Mayor reads feedback, updates design.md\n      │\n      v\n  Mayor tells worker-2: \"Updated design, try again\"\n  Mayor tells merger: \"Hold off merging worker-2's branch\"\n```\n\nThis is a meaningful addition to the Crysknife design — agent roles (mayor/worker/merger) instead of treating all agents the same. Want me to update the design doc with this?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> im I missing something ? ",
    "\n\nLooking at Gas Town's roles mapped to what we just defined:\n\n```\nGas Town Role        Crysknife          Status\n─────────────────    ────────────────   ──────────\nOverseer (you)       You                ✓\nMayor (dispatcher)   Mayor agent        ✓\nCrew/Polecats        Workers            ✓\nRefinery             Merger agent       ✓\nWitness (monitor)    crys watch         ✓\nDeacon (heartbeat)   —                  skipped (you are this)\nDogs (helpers)       —                  skipped\nBoot (health check)  —                  skipped\n```\n\nThe one thing that might be missing: **the Witness role is just a script right now** (`crys watch`). But with a mayor, 4 workers, and a merger, that's already 6 agents. If `crys watch` can't nudge agents effectively enough from outside, you might eventually want a dedicated agent that checks on workers — but I'd say skip it for now and see if the script is enough.\n\nOther than that, I think you've got the right pieces. The feedback loop between workers → merger → mayor is the part that makes this more than just \"6 agents in tmux.\" That's the real coordination layer.\n\nWant me to update the design doc with the three roles?\n[Tool uses: none]",

Here's what I added/changed in the Crysknife design doc:\n\n- **New \"Agent Roles\" section** — defines the three roles (mayor, workers, merger) with responsibilities\n- **Feedback loop diagram** — shows how workers → merger → mayor communicate through files\n- **Merger standing orders** — permanent task file template with merge process and report format\n- **New \"Parallel Planning\" section** — plan.md format with phases, dependencies, and parallel tasks\n- **Execution flow diagram** — shows phases running in parallel with branch strategy\n- **Updated tmux layout** — now includes dedicated merger window and role-based naming\n- **Updated state.json** — added `role` field, `merge_queue` array, phase tracking in convoys\n- **Updated Gas Town mapping** — mayor agent, workers, merger agent instead of \"you do everything\"\n- **Updated non-goals** — removed \"no automated merging\" since the merger agent handles it\n- **Updated implementation phases** — role-based startup, merger template, phase-based sling\n[Tool uses: none]",

I see that as a more likily think to work because if we preplan something is going to come up and one agen is going to finish super quuicly and is not going to do anything and anothir is going to struggle. We need more flexibily I hink. im going to be availabel for uestions doing th eexecution so the mayor and ask me more product sort of questions and more usecase sort of questions and also tuch stack desitions if he thinks he don't have the contexts and the implementatino detals he takes caro of and the actuall implementation is by the agents.",
    "\n\nYou're right — pre-planning rigid phases is too brittle. Real work is messy. One worker finishes in 5 minutes, another gets stuck for an hour, a third discovers the design was wrong. You need the plan to be a living thing that the mayor adapts on the fly, with you making the product decisions.\n\nLet me update the planning section in the design doc to reflect this:\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Also update the key design decisions to reflect this:\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "And update the Phase 4 implementation plan:\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Done. The big changes:\n\n- **Replaced rigid phase graph** with an adaptive living plan — tasks grouped by readiness (ready/blocked/discovered), not numbered phases\n- **Mayor's decision loop** — a clear cycle of checking idle workers, stuck workers, merged branches, new discoveries\n- **Your role defined** — you're the product owner answering the mayor's questions, not managing individual workers\n- **Branches created on-the-fly** — not pre-planned, just created as the mayor assigns work\n- **Plan grows during execution** — \"Discovered During Execution\" section captures work that emerges mid-flight\n\nThe core shift: the plan is a snapshot of current understanding that the mayor constantly updates, not a contract that must be followed.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> do you see any issues over sights that migt bit us.",
    "\n\nA few things I'd flag:\n\n**1. The mayor is a kiro-cli agent, not a script — but we're treating it like both.**\n\nThe design says the mayor \"continuously runs a decision loop\" and \"updates plan.md.\" But the mayor is just a kiro-cli session like any other. It doesn't poll. It doesn't watch state.json. It sits there waiting for you to talk to it. So who triggers the mayor to check on workers?\n\nRight now the answer is: you do, manually. \"Hey mayor, worker-2 finished, what's next?\" That works at 4 workers. At 6 it gets tedious. We might need `crys watch` to nudge the mayor too — not just workers. Something like: \"Worker-3 just went idle. Check plan.md and assign next task.\"\n\n**2. The merger and workers share the same Git worktree.**\n\nThis is the biggest practical problem. Worker-1 is on branch `feat/auth-backend`. Worker-2 is on `feat/auth-frontend`. The merger needs to checkout main and merge. But they're all in the same project directory. You can't have 6 agents on 6 different branches in one directory.\n\nSolutions:\n- **Git worktrees** — each worker gets its own worktree (`git worktree add`). The merger works in the main worktree. This is how Gas Town does it.\n- **Workers commit and push, merger works in a separate clone** — simpler but messier.\n\nThis needs to be in the design. It's a blocker for Phase 1.\n\n**3. State.json concurrent writes.**\n\nTwo agents finish at the same time. Both try to update state.json. One overwrites the other's changes. No locking, no merge. Data lost.\n\nAt 3-4 workers this is rare. At 6 it'll happen. Options:\n- Only `crys` CLI writes state.json (agents never touch it directly)\n- File locking (simple `flock` wrapper)\n- Accept occasional corruption and make state.json recoverable from task files on disk\n\nThe simplest: agents never write state.json. Only `crys` commands and `crys watch` write it. Agents write to their own task files only.\n\n**4. The merger has no way to know a worker is \"done.\"**\n\nWorker finishes, writes \"DONE\" in its task file. But the merger doesn't read worker task files — it reads state.json. Who updates state.json when a worker finishes? The worker can't (see point 3). You could do it manually (`crys done worker-1`), or `crys watch` could detect it and update state.\n\n**5. What happens when the merger is busy and branches pile up?**\n\nWorker-1, worker-2, and worker-3 all finish within minutes. The merger is still resolving a conflict from worker-1's branch. Two branches sit in the merge queue. Meanwhile those workers are idle because the mayor might want to assign work that depends on the merged code.\n\nNot a design flaw — just something to be aware of. The merger becomes a bottleneck during burst completions. The answer is probably: assign workers independent tasks while they wait, and let the merger catch up.\n\n**6. No rollback if a merge breaks things.**\n\nThe merger merges to main, runs tests, tests fail. Now main is broken. The design says \"flag for mayor\" but doesn't say what happens to main. Should the merger revert the merge? Leave it broken? This needs a policy.\n\nSimplest: merger always merges to a staging branch first (`merge/staging`), runs tests there, and only fast-forwards main if tests pass.\n\n**The ones I'd fix in the design now (before building):**\n\n- **Git worktrees** — this is a hard blocker, needs to be in the architecture\n- **State.json ownership** — only `crys` CLI writes it, agents write task files only\n- **Done detection** — `crys watch` detects worker completion and updates state.json\n\nThe rest (merger bottleneck, rollback policy) can be figured out during use. Want me to add these to the design doc?\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Done. Here's what was added:\n\n**Key Design Decisions (4 new):**\n- 9: Git worktrees for isolation\n- 10: Only `crys` CLI writes state.json\n- 11: Staging branch for safe merges\n- 12: `crys watch` nudges the mayor on worker completion\n\n**New Architecture sections:**\n- **Git Worktree Layout** — each worker gets its own worktree, merger works in main\n- **Merge Flow** — worker branch → `merge/staging` → tests → main (main stays clean if tests fail)\n- **State Ownership** — clear table of who writes what file\n- **Done Detection** — `crys done` command updates state, notifies mayor and merger, with `crys watch` as fallback\n\n**Updated templates** — all three tiers now include `crys done {{AGENT_ID}}` as the final step and a Feedback section for workers to flag issues\n\n**New CLI command** — `crys done <worker>` added to commands section and project structure\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> any other issues that you see that are worth addersing ?",
    "\n\nA few more, smaller than the last batch but worth thinking about:\n\n**1. Worktrees share .crysknife/ and .kiro/specs/ — but do they really?**\n\nGit worktrees share the `.git` directory but each worktree has its own working tree. So `.crysknife/state.json` and `.kiro/specs/tasks/worker-1.md` exist independently in each worktree. If worker-1 writes to its task file in its worktree, the mayor in a different worktree won't see it until it's committed and pulled.\n\nOptions:\n- Symlink `.crysknife/` and `.kiro/specs/` to the main worktree so all agents read/write the same files\n- Or put shared state outside the worktrees entirely (e.g. `project-worktrees/.shared/`)\n- Or just accept that agents commit + push frequently and others pull\n\nThis is a practical detail that'll bite you on day one. Symlinks are probably simplest.\n\n**2. How does the mayor actually know what workers are doing?**\n\nThe mayor reads worker task files for feedback. But if worktrees are isolated (see above), the mayor can't read worker-1's task file unless it's committed. And the mayor reads plan.md to decide what to assign — but workers don't update plan.md, only the mayor does. So the mayor's view of the world depends on:\n- `crys status` (reads state.json — always current because only `crys` writes it)\n- Worker task files (might be stale if worktrees are isolated)\n\nIf we solve #1 with symlinks, this goes away. Just flagging the dependency.\n\n**3. What's the mayor's initial prompt?**\n\nEvery worker gets a task file template. The merger gets standing orders. But the mayor doesn't have a template — it's just a kiro-cli session you talk to. It needs to know:\n- It's the mayor, its job is planning and dispatching\n- Where plan.md, state.json, and worker task files are\n- How to use `crys sling`, `crys status`, `crys done`\n- That it should ask you product questions, not guess\n\nThis could be a `mayor-prompt.md` that you paste or load on session start. Or `crys start` could auto-send it.\n\n**4. Worker area boundaries are honor-system only.**\n\nWe tell workers \"don't touch files outside your area.\" But nothing enforces it. Two workers editing the same file is the #1 source of merge conflicts. At 3-4 workers this is manageable. At 6 it'll happen.\n\nNot worth solving in code right now — but worth adding to principles.md as a strong rule, and having the merger flag violations in its merge reports.\n\nI think #1 (worktree file sharing) and #3 (mayor prompt) are worth adding to the design. The others are \"be aware\" items. Want me to add those two?\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Done. Four additions:\n\n1. **Worktree symlinks** — `.crysknife/` and `.kiro/specs/` symlinked from each worktree back to the main worktree so all agents share the same state, task files, and specs\n2. **Mayor template** (`mayor.md`) — defines the mayor's role, files it owns, CLI tools it uses, decision loop, and rules (never write code, always ask overseer for product decisions)\n3. **Principles template** (`principles.md`) — area boundary enforcement as a critical rule, git discipline (never push to main, never merge other branches), communication rules (use Feedback section), and explicit anti-patterns\n4. **Updated `crys init`** — now generates mayor template, principles.md, and an empty plan.md alongside the worker templates\n\nI think the design is solid now. The remaining unknowns are things you'll discover by using it — which is the whole point of building it yourself.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> any other issues come to mind ?",
    "\n\nHonestly, I think we've covered the major ones. The remaining things I can think of are minor and better discovered through use:\n\n- **Worktree symlink paths** — the relative paths in the symlinks might need tweaking depending on where you actually put the worktrees. You'll figure this out in 5 minutes when you first run `crys start`.\n- **How much context to give workers** — do they need to read the full design.md or just the relevant section? You'll tune this as you see what works.\n- **Merger ordering heuristics** — \"smallest diff first\" sounds good in theory but you might find a different strategy works better. Learn by doing.\n- **How often `crys watch` should nudge the mayor** — too often is annoying, too rarely and workers sit idle. Default to something, adjust.\n\nNone of these are design-level issues. They're tuning knobs you'll adjust in the first week.\n\nI think the design doc is ready to build from. Want to save this conversation and start a fresh one for implementation, or is there anything else you want to explore first?\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> we are going to use file comunication for now symlinks seem to be the best solutino for now is gonig to be like  alog of all theconvelsations. what other options do we have ? you mentiened sqlite at some point are there others you can think of. what are the pros and cons between them. im just coursios",
    "\n\nGood question. Here are the realistic options for agent communication/state sharing:\n\n```\nMETHOD              HOW IT WORKS\n────────────────    ──────────────────────────────────────────\n1. Shared files     Agents read/write files on disk (symlinks)\n   (what we chose)  JSON, markdown, plain text\n\n2. SQLite           Single-file database in the project\n                    Agents query/insert via CLI or library\n\n3. Unix sockets     Named pipe or socket file on disk\n                    Agents send/receive messages in real time\n\n4. Redis/similar    In-memory key-value store running locally\n                    Agents pub/sub or read/write keys\n\n5. tmux buffers     tmux's built-in paste buffers and\n                    send-keys for passing data between panes\n\n6. Git itself       Agents commit messages/notes as the\n                    communication channel (like Beads)\n\n7. HTTP server      Tiny local API server that agents POST to\n                    and GET from\n```\n\nPros and cons:\n\n```\n                    PROS                         CONS\n────────────────    ───────────────────────────  ──────────────────────────\nShared files        - Dead simple                - No concurrency protection\n(symlinks)          - Human readable             - No real-time notification\n                    - Git-trackable              - Polling required to detect\n                    - Any tool can read/write      changes\n                    - Survives crashes            - Gets messy with many files\n                    - Zero dependencies           - Agents can corrupt each\n                                                   other's writes\n\nSQLite              - Concurrent reads safe      - Agents can't easily write\n                    - Structured queries           (kiro-cli has no sqlite)\n                    - Single file, portable      - Not human readable\n                    - ACID transactions          - Only crys CLI can interact\n                    - Good for history/logs      - Extra dependency\n                    - Handles merge queue well   - Not Git-trackable\n\nUnix sockets        - Real-time messaging        - Complex to implement\n                    - No polling needed          - Requires a listener process\n                    - Low latency                  (daemon — we said no daemon)\n                    - Clean pub/sub model        - Doesn't survive crashes\n                                                 - Agents can't use them\n                                                   (kiro-cli has no socket API)\n\nRedis               - Real-time pub/sub          - External dependency\n                    - Concurrent safe            - Overkill for 6 agents\n                    - Fast                       - Another process to manage\n                    - Built-in expiry/TTL        - Agents can't talk to it\n                                                   directly\n\ntmux buffers        - Already have tmux          - Tiny buffer size\n                    - Zero extra dependencies    - No persistence\n                    - Real-time via send-keys    - Fragile, easy to lose data\n                                                 - Not structured\n                                                 - Basically what we use for\n                                                   nudging already\n\nGit itself          - Built-in versioning        - Slow (commit per message)\n(Beads approach)    - Full history               - Merge conflicts on the\n                    - Distributed by nature        communication channel itself\n                    - This is what Gas Town does - Noisy git log\n                                                 - Agents need to commit\n                                                   constantly\n\nHTTP server         - Clean API                  - Requires a running server\n                    - Language agnostic            (daemon again)\n                    - Real-time possible         - Extra complexity\n                    - Structured request/response- Agents would need curl\n                    - Easy to add a web UI later\n```\n\n**The real constraint is: kiro-cli agents can only interact with the filesystem and terminal commands.** They can't open sockets, connect to Redis, or make HTTP calls natively. They *can* run shell commands, so `curl`, `sqlite3`, and file operations all work. But the simpler the interface, the more reliably agents will use it.\n\nThat's why shared files win for now. The agent just writes to a markdown file. No API, no protocol, no client library. It's the lowest common denominator that any tool can interact with.\n\n**If you outgrow files, SQLite is the natural next step.** You'd keep agents writing to their task files (markdown), but `crys` CLI would sync that into SQLite for structured queries, history, and concurrent-safe state management. Best of both worlds — agents see simple files, the orchestrator sees a database.\n\n```\nEVOLUTION PATH:\n\nStage 6:   Shared files only (where we are)\n              ↓ pain: polling, no history, messy state\nStage 7:   Files for agents + SQLite for crys CLI\n              ↓ pain: want real-time, want web UI\nStage 8:   SQLite + HTTP server (or adopt Gas Town)\n```\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> not suer if you menteide i t or not what dose gastown use for communicatino it uses a library calle Beads right ?",
    "\n\nBeads isn't a library — it's a tool Yegge built. It's a lightweight issue tracker that stores everything as JSON lines in a file (`.beads/beads.jsonl`) committed to Git. So Gas Town's communication is fundamentally **shared files + Git**, same category as what we chose.\n\nBut Beads adds structure on top of raw files:\n\n```\nRaw shared files (what we do):\n  - Each agent has its own .md file\n  - Free-form text\n  - No schema, no IDs, no querying\n\nBeads (what Gas Town does):\n  - One JSONL file, one line per \"bead\" (issue/message/identity)\n  - Every bead has an ID, status, assignee, type\n  - CLI tool (bd) to create, query, update beads\n  - Git tracks all changes automatically\n  - Routing across repos via prefixes\n\n  .beads/beads.jsonl:\n  {\"id\":\"bd-x7k2\",\"type\":\"task\",\"title\":\"Fix navbar\",\"status\":\"open\",\"assignee\":\"polecat-3\"}\n  {\"id\":\"bd-a1b2\",\"type\":\"mail\",\"to\":\"mayor\",\"from\":\"worker-1\",\"body\":\"MR ready\"}\n  {\"id\":\"bd-m9n4\",\"type\":\"hook\",\"agent\":\"refinery-1\",\"molecule\":\"mol-abc\"}\n```\n\nSo the communication stack is:\n\n```\nGas Town communication:\n\n  Agent wants to send a message\n    → runs: bd create --type mail --to mayor --body \"MR ready\"\n    → Beads appends a JSON line to beads.jsonl\n    → Beads commits to Git\n\n  Agent wants to check its inbox\n    → runs: bd list --to me --type mail --status unread\n    → Beads reads beads.jsonl, filters, returns results\n\n  Agent wants to update task status\n    → runs: bd update bd-x7k2 --status done\n    → Beads modifies the line in beads.jsonl\n    → Beads commits to Git\n```\n\nIt's still files on disk at the bottom. But the `bd` CLI gives it structure, querying, and a consistent schema. The real-time messaging (`gt nudge`) is still tmux `send-keys` — same as what we do.\n\nFor Crysknife, our equivalent is:\n- `crys` CLI writes state.json (structured, like Beads)\n- Agents write markdown task files (unstructured, simpler than Beads)\n- Nudging is tmux send-keys (same as Gas Town)\n\nIf we ever wanted Beads-like querying, we'd move state.json to SQLite. But for 6 agents, JSON is fine.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> loking at the github link seems like beads is using dolt a databas right not sure if im correct can you check this is the repo. I just want to understand better how he does it https://github.com/steveyegge/beads",
    "\n[Tool uses: web_fetch]",
    "> t",
    "Good catch — you're right! Beads has evolved significantly since the Gas Town article was written. It's now **Dolt-powered**, not plain JSONL.\n\n[Dolt](https://github.com/dolthub/dolt) is a SQL database that works like Git — it has branching, merging, and version control built into the database itself. So Beads upgraded from \"JSON lines in a file committed to Git\" to \"a proper SQL database with Git-like versioning.\"\n\nHere's how it actually works now:\n\n```\nWHAT THE ARTICLE DESCRIBED (early Beads):\n\n  .beads/beads.jsonl          <- one JSON line per issue\n  committed to Git            <- versioning via git\n\nWHAT BEADS ACTUALLY USES NOW:\n\n  .beads/ (Dolt database)     <- SQL database (like MySQL)\n  with built-in versioning    <- branching, merging, diffing\n                                 all inside the database itself\n\n  bd create \"Fix bug\"         <- INSERT INTO beads ...\n  bd ready                    <- SELECT * FROM beads WHERE ...\n  bd update bd-x7k2 --claim   <- UPDATE beads SET ...\n```\n\nWhy this matters:\n\n```\nJSONL + Git (what the article said):\n  + Simple, human readable\n  + Any tool can read it\n  - Concurrent writes corrupt the file\n  - No querying (grep through JSON lines)\n  - Merge conflicts on the data file itself\n\nDolt (what Beads actually uses):\n  + Real SQL queries\n  + Concurrent-safe writes\n  + Cell-level merge (not line-level like Git)\n  + Native branching (each agent can have its own branch)\n  + Hash-based IDs prevent merge collisions\n  - Not human readable (it's a database)\n  - Extra dependency (Dolt binary)\n  - Heavier than a JSON file\n```\n\nThe cell-level merge is the killer feature for multi-agent. In a JSONL file, if two agents add a line at the same time, Git sees a merge conflict. In Dolt, if two agents create different beads, it merges them cleanly because it understands the schema — it knows they're different rows, not conflicting edits to the same line.\n\nFor Crysknife, we're fine with JSON + symlinks at 6 agents since only `crys` CLI writes state.json. But if you ever scale up and hit concurrent write issues, Dolt or SQLite would be the natural upgrade path — same progression Beads went through.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey so each git workspace reflects a branch in dolt how dose that work ?",
    "\n\nI don't want to speculate too much on the exact implementation since I haven't read Beads' source code. But based on what the README says and how Dolt works, here's my understanding:\n\nDolt is basically MySQL but with Git semantics built into the database. So you can do:\n\n```sql\n-- Dolt SQL (looks like MySQL but with version control)\nSELECT * FROM beads WHERE status = 'open';\n\n-- But also:\nCALL dolt_checkout('-b', 'worker-1-branch');\nINSERT INTO beads (id, title, status) VALUES ('bd-x1', 'Fix bug', 'open');\nCALL dolt_commit('-m', 'worker-1 created a task');\n\nCALL dolt_checkout('main');\nCALL dolt_merge('worker-1-branch');\n```\n\nSo the mapping would be something like:\n\n```\nGit worktree (code)          Dolt branch (data)\n─────────────────────        ─────────────────────────\nworker-1 worktree            worker-1 Dolt branch\n  edits src/auth/              creates/updates beads\n  on git branch feat/auth      on dolt branch worker-1\n\nworker-2 worktree            worker-2 Dolt branch\n  edits src/frontend/          creates/updates beads\n  on git branch feat/ui        on dolt branch worker-2\n\nmerger worktree              main Dolt branch\n  merges git branches          merges dolt branches\n  to main                      (cell-level, no conflicts\n                                if different rows)\n```\n\nThe key insight: Git manages the **code**, Dolt manages the **work tracking data**. They branch and merge independently but in parallel. When a worker creates a task or updates status, that happens in Dolt. When it writes code, that happens in Git. Both have their own branching.\n\nThe reason this is powerful: two workers can both create new beads on their own Dolt branches simultaneously. When merged, Dolt knows they're different rows (different hash IDs) and merges cleanly. With a JSON file, that's a guaranteed merge conflict.\n\nBut honestly, this is me reasoning from Dolt's docs and the Beads README. The actual implementation might be simpler or different. If you're curious about the specifics, the source is in `internal/` in that repo — might be worth a look when you get to the point of needing something beyond JSON files.\n\nFor Crysknife at 6 agents with only `crys` CLI writing state.json, we don't need any of this. It's good to know where the road leads though.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> we still need git worktrees",
    "\n\nYeah absolutely — Git worktrees are for code isolation, not data. Each worker needs its own working directory so they can be on different branches editing different files without stepping on each other. That's a hard requirement regardless of how we store state.\n\nThe Dolt/SQLite discussion is only about the state/communication layer (state.json vs database). Separate concern entirely.\n\n```\nGit worktrees  → isolate CODE (each worker on its own branch)\nState storage  → share DATA (state.json, task files, plan.md)\n\nWe need both. They solve different problems.\n```\n\nWorktrees are already in the design doc and ready to go.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> /can you add this whole dolt thing at the end of the document as a possible future upgrade to our current communication method",
    "> can you add this whole dolt thing at the end of the document as a possible future upgrade to our current communication method",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Done. Added a \"Future: State Storage Upgrade Path\" section at the end covering:\n\n- Current approach (JSON + symlinks)\n- Upgrade 1: SQLite (structured queries, keep markdown for agents)\n- Upgrade 2: Dolt (what Beads uses — branch-aware SQL with cell-level merge)\n- When to upgrade (JSON now → SQLite at Stage 7 → Dolt at Stage 8+)\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> /compact",
    "> good crysknife_design, gastown, and gas-town-user-manual so you endrstand better what we are talking about.",
    "\n\nLet me read all three.\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "\n[Tool uses: glob,glob]",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Got it. I've now read all three documents end to end. Here's what I've absorbed:\n\n**Gas Town article** — The full architecture: 7 worker roles (Mayor, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs, Boot, Crew), GUPP (hook-based propulsion), MEOW stack (Beads → Epics → Molecules → Protomolecules → Formulas → Wisps), Convoys as the work-order system, NDI (nondeterministic idempotence), patrols as looping ephemeral workflows, and the k8s comparison. Key insight: Gas Town optimizes for *completion* not *uptime*.\n\n**Gas Town User Manual** — The practical side: three dev loops (outer/middle/inner), crew cycling workflow (assign everyone → ignore → harvest results), PR Sheriff as standing orders, review sweeps + fix sweeps for code quality, heresies and how principles files prevent them, and the \"you technically only need the Mayor\" degradation path.\n\n**Crysknife Design** — Our simplified adaptation: 3 roles (Mayor, Workers, Merger), JSON state file, tmux runtime, symlinked worktrees, staging branch merge flow, adaptive planning with living plan.md, workflow tiers (full/standard/quick), and the future upgrade path to SQLite/Dolt we just added.\n\nThe mapping is solid. We took the parts that matter at 3-6 agents and left out what doesn't apply yet (Deacon, Dogs, Boot, Polecats, Wisps, Formulas, federation). The crew cycling pattern from the user manual maps directly to how you'd work with Crysknife workers. The PR Sheriff pattern is already captured as merger standing orders.\n\nReady to move forward whenever you are — next step from the roadmap is creating the Crysknife repo and starting Phase 1 implementation.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> compare the gastows user manual to our crysknife design witch do you think is better for starting out do you think is more polished ?",
    "\n\nHonestly? The Crysknife design doc is more polished and more useful as a starting point. Here's why:\n\n**Structure and clarity**\n\nThe Gas Town user manual is a blog post — it's conversational, jumps between tips, has asides about murder mysteries and panda bears, and assumes you already read the first article. It's entertaining but you'd have a hard time handing it to an agent and saying \"follow this.\"\n\nThe Crysknife doc reads like an actual engineering spec. It has:\n- Numbered design decisions with rationale\n- ASCII diagrams for every flow (agent roles, feedback loop, merge flow, data flow)\n- Concrete CLI commands with exact syntax and examples\n- Template files you can copy-paste\n- A state.json schema\n- Detection algorithms with thresholds\n- A phased implementation plan\n\nAn agent (or a human) could pick up the Crysknife doc and start building from it today. You can't do that with the Gas Town user manual.\n\n**Scope discipline**\n\nYegge's manual covers everything — 7 roles, patrols, wisps, formulas, federation, plugins, the Mol Mall — and most of it is aspirational or half-built (\"17 days old\", \"plugins didn't make the v1 launch\"). It's honest about that, but it means a lot of what you're reading doesn't exist yet or is unstable.\n\nCrysknife is deliberately scoped to what you actually need at 3-6 agents. Every section describes something that will be built and used. No vapor.\n\n**Where Gas Town wins**\n\nThe user manual has two things the Crysknife doc doesn't capture as well:\n\n1. The *operational rhythm* — the crew cycling cadence (assign → ignore → harvest) is described with real muscle memory detail. How you cycle through with `C-b n/p`, when to read vs when to leave them alone, the \"Jeff Bezos reviewing results\" mental model. Our doc has the mechanics but not the feel.\n\n2. The *failure modes* — heresies, the murder mystery, agents guessing wrong, work getting lost. Yegge is brutally honest about what goes wrong. Our doc is clean but hasn't been battle-tested yet, so it doesn't have that scar tissue.\n\n**Bottom line**\n\nFor actually building something and getting started: Crysknife doc, no contest. It's a blueprint you can execute against.\n\nFor understanding what it *feels like* to run multi-agent workflows and what will bite you: the Gas Town user manual is invaluable reference material. Keep it around — you'll want to re-read it after you've been running Crysknife for a week and start hitting the same problems Yegge describes.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> if I where to start a new folder to start this project would you recomend me to include gastown or gas-town-usermanual. or multi-agent-progression files as wel as crysknife or only crystnkif ?",
    "\n\nI'd bring over three files:\n\n```\ncrysknife/\n├── docs/\n│   ├── crysknife-design.md          # the blueprint — this drives everything\n│   ├── gas-town-user-manual.md      # operational wisdom reference\n│   └── multi-agent-progression.md   # your personal roadmap (Stage 5→8)\n```\n\n**Why these three:**\n\n- `crysknife-design.md` — obvious, it's the spec you're building from\n- `gas-town-user-manual.md` — the operational patterns (crew cycling, PR sheriff, heresies, review sweeps) are directly applicable. You'll reference this when writing the mayor template and principles. It's compact and practical.\n- `multi-agent-progression.md` — this is *your* progression map. It has your stage definitions, your MEOW mapping, your workflow tiers, your crew cycling cadence notes. It's the \"why\" behind the design decisions.\n\n**Leave behind:**\n\n- `gastown.md` (the first article) — it's 34 minutes of theory, architecture deep-dives, and Yegge storytelling. Everything useful from it is already distilled into either the Crysknife design doc or the progression doc. It's great background reading but not something you'd reference while building. If you ever need it, it's still in the chip8 repo.\n\nSo: the design doc to build from, the user manual for operational patterns, and the progression doc for your personal roadmap. That's a clean starting point.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> we came to a lot of new conculsiont while building the crysknife-desin document. check multi-agest-progression is not outdated or missind something improtant",
    "\n\nLet me read the progression doc and compare it against the design doc.\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Here's what I found comparing the two docs:\n\n**Outdated / needs updating:**\n\n1. The progression doc still uses `.orchestra/` as the directory name (Stage 7 section). Should be `.crysknife/`.\n\n2. Stage 7 describes generic \"Dispatcher / Monitor / Merger\" components. The Crysknife design doc has evolved these into specific roles: Mayor agent, Workers, Merger agent — with concrete templates, standing orders, and a feedback loop. The progression doc's Stage 7 is now a pale shadow of what we actually designed.\n\n3. The state.json example in Stage 7 uses `hook` field. Crysknife design uses `task_file` — same concept, different name. Should be consistent.\n\n4. Stage 7 has a bash `monitor.sh` script. Crysknife replaced this with `crys watch` using tmux pane content diffing. The bash script is obsolete.\n\n5. The build order in Stage 7 says \"Dispatcher first, Monitor second, Merger last.\" Crysknife design has 4 phases with different ordering (Foundation → Work Assignment → Monitoring → Planning/Tracking).\n\n**Missing from progression doc:**\n\n6. No mention of Git worktrees. The Crysknife design doc has a full worktree layout with symlinks. The progression doc still assumes all agents share one working directory.\n\n7. No mention of the staging branch merge flow (`merge/staging` → tests → fast-forward main). The progression doc just says \"you merge (lazygit).\"\n\n8. No mention of adaptive planning / living plan.md. The Crysknife design doc has a whole section on the mayor's decision loop and readiness-based task grouping (ready/blocked/discovered). The progression doc still has rigid phases.\n\n9. No mention of `crys done` and the done detection flow (worker signals completion → state update → nudge mayor + merger).\n\n10. No mention of the future state storage upgrade path (JSON → SQLite → Dolt).\n\n11. The CLI commands section doesn't exist — the progression doc doesn't reference any `crys` commands.\n\n12. The Gas Town concepts mapping table in the progression doc is less complete than the one in the Crysknife design doc (missing merger, merge_queue, convoy mapping).\n\n**Still good / keep as-is:**\n\n- Stages 5, 6, and 6.5 are solid — they describe where you've been and the manual workflow. No changes needed.\n- The MEOW stack mapping is still useful context.\n- The \"What to Adopt, In Order of Value\" list is still valid.\n- The 8 key principles at the bottom are all still correct.\n- The Gas Town Role Mapping table across stages is a nice overview.\n\n**Recommendation:**\n\nRewrite Stage 7 to reference the Crysknife design doc rather than duplicating (and contradicting) it. Something like: \"Stage 7 is Crysknife. See `crysknife-design.md` for the full spec.\" Then keep the progression doc focused on the journey (Stages 5 → 6 → 6.5 → 7) and the conceptual mapping, not the implementation details.\n\nWant me to update it?\n[Tool uses: none]",
