    "All clean — those are just the word \"orchestrator\" in prose, not the old `.orchestra/` directory references. \n\nDone. Here's what I changed:\n\n- **Stage overview**: Stage 7 now says \"3-6 agents, Crysknife orchestrator\" instead of \"10+ agents, custom orchestrator\"\n- **Concepts table**: `Hooks` → `Task Files (Gas Town: Hooks)`, `Patrols` → `crys watch (Gas Town: Patrols)`, `Mail/Messaging` → `Shared Files (Gas Town: Mail)`, `gt sling` → `crys sling`, `GUPP Nudge` → `crys nudge (Gas Town: GUPP)`, `Shiny/Quick` → `full/standard/quick`\n- **\"What We Already Have\" table**: Updated lazygit → merger agent, dispatcher → mayor agent, monitor → crys watch, GUPP + Hooks → task files + state.json\n- **Patrols section**: Rewritten to describe `crys watch` with pane diffing thresholds\n- **Similarity map**: Updated to use our terms (crys sling, crys watch, staging branch, task files)\n- **Adoption list**: Replaced hooks/patrols/mail with task files, crys watch, Git worktrees\n- **Stage 7**: Complete rewrite — references crysknife-design.md, shows the role mapping, adaptive planning, CLI commands, implementation phases. Removed the old bash monitor script and `.orchestra/` references\n- **Role mapping table**: Added \"(Crysknife)\" column header, specific entries (mayor agent, workers, merger agent, crys watch)\n- **Context preservation**: Updated to Crysknife terminology, added Dolt info for Gas Town\n- **Timeline**: Updated to reference Crysknife phases\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey so we are using kiro-cli it has a few features. im not sure other services like cloude code has. maybe some can help in our cryknife impmentatino [help] 13% > what features are available ?> \n> \nIntrospecting to get you the right information [mode: semantic] (using tool: introspect)> \n - Completed in 0.20s> \n> \n> Here's an overview of the features available in Kiro CLI:> \n> \nSlash Commands:> \n| Command | Description |> \n|---------|-------------|> \n| /help | Switch to Help Agent for questions about Kiro CLI |> \n| /agent | Manage and switch between agents |> \n| /chat | Manage saved conversations |> \n| /context | Manage context files and view context window usage |> \n| /code | Code intelligence with LSP integration |> \n| /tools | View tools and manage permissions |> \n| /compact | Summarize conversation to free up context space |> \n| /model | Select a model for the current session |> \n| /mcp | View MCP server status and tools |> \n| /hooks | View context hooks |> \n| /todos | View, manage, and resume to-do lists |> \n| /prompts | View and retrieve prompts |> \n| /plan | Switch to Plan agent for implementation plans |> \n| /editor | Open $EDITOR to compose a prompt |> \n| /reply | Open $EDITOR with the last assistant message quoted |> \n| /paste | Paste an image from clipboard |> \n| /experiment | Toggle experimental features |> \n| /usage | Show billing and credits info |> \n| /clear | Clear conversation history |> \n| /quit | Exit the application |> \n> \nKey Features:> \n- **Tangent Mode** — explore side questions without losing main conversation context> \n- **Code Intelligence** — LSP-powered symbol search, lookup, and AST-based pattern matching> \n- **Knowledge Base** — index project files for context-aware answers> \n- **MCP Servers** — extend capabilities with external tool servers> \n- **Hooks** — context hooks that trigger automatically> \n- **TODO Lists** — track and resume task lists> \n- **Agents** — custom agent configurations with specific tools and prompts> \n- **Planning Agent** — break down ideas into implementation plans> \n- **Checkpoints** — save/restore points in your workflow> \n- **Delegate/Subagent** — delegate tasks to sub-agents> \n- **Auto-compaction** — automatic conversation summarization when context fills up> \n- **Workspace Settings** — per-project configuration overrides> \n> \nMost features can be enabled via kiro-cli settings, e.g.:> \nbash> \nkiro-cli settings chat.enableTangentMode true> \nkiro-cli settings chat.enableCodeIntelligence true> \nkiro-cli settings chat.enableKnowledge true> \n> \n> \nWant details on any specific feature?> \n> \n ▸ Credits: 0.51 • Time: 15s> \n> \n[help] 18% >> \n",
    "\n\nNice — several of these are directly relevant to Crysknife. Let me map them:\n\n**Directly useful for Crysknife:**\n\n- **`/agent`** — Custom agent configurations with specific tools and prompts. This is huge. We could define `mayor`, `worker`, and `merger` as named agents with their own system prompts (the templates from crysknife-design.md). Instead of `crys sling` sending a tmux message saying \"read your task file\", we could have each kiro-cli session start with the right agent profile already loaded. That's cleaner than prompt injection via tmux send-keys.\n\n- **`/todos`** — Track and resume task lists. This could replace or complement our task file checkboxes. If kiro-cli natively tracks TODOs, workers could use this instead of manually checking off markdown checkboxes. Worth investigating how persistent these are across sessions.\n\n- **`/hooks`** — Context hooks that trigger automatically. If we can set a hook that fires on session startup to read `state.json` and the agent's task file, that's basically GUPP for free. The agent would automatically know what it's supposed to be doing without a nudge.\n\n- **`/compact`** — Auto-compaction. Already in our design for session hygiene, but good to know it's built in. Workers can run longer before needing a handoff.\n\n- **`/chat`** — Saved conversations. Could be useful for the mayor to save planning context across sessions, or for the merger to save merge history.\n\n- **Delegate/Subagent** — Workers could potentially delegate subtasks without us needing to orchestrate it. Though this might conflict with our area boundaries principle.\n\n- **`/context`** — Manage context files. We could configure each agent to auto-load its task file + principles.md + design.md as context. That's the \"shared specs\" pattern built into the tool.\n\n**Worth investigating deeper:**\n\n- **`/agent`** — Can we define agents in `.kiro/` config files? If so, `crys init` could generate agent definitions for mayor/worker/merger, and `crys start` would launch kiro-cli with `--agent mayor` or similar. That would be a much tighter integration than tmux send-keys.\n\n- **`/hooks`** — What triggers are available? If there's an \"on session start\" hook, we could wire it to read state.json automatically.\n\n- **MCP Servers** — Could we expose `crys status`, `crys done`, `crys sling` as MCP tools? Then agents could call them directly instead of shelling out. The mayor could run `crys sling worker-1 --task \"...\"` as a tool call rather than us sending it via tmux.\n\n**The MCP angle is the most interesting.** If we can make Crysknife an MCP server, the mayor agent could dispatch work, check status, and mark tasks done through native tool calls. Workers could call `crys done` as a tool. That's way more reliable than tmux send-keys and hoping the agent reads it.\n\nWant me to dig into any of these? The `/agent` and MCP server angles could significantly change how `crys start` and `crys sling` work.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> yeah I agree agent, hooks, mpc servers, compact and contexct are going to be the mores usefull. I think toods we can ignorf for now. lets digg more into this featres to see what really they are all about and see what we can actaly implement in cryknife",
    "\n\nLet me dig into each one. I'll check the kiro-cli docs and config structure.\n[Tool uses: execute_bash]",

    "> y",
    "Now let me check hooks and context features:\n[Tool uses: introspect]",
    "That's a goldmine. Let me also check the context feature:\n[Tool uses: introspect]",
    "This is excellent. Here's the full breakdown of what we can actually use:\n\n---\n\n## 1. Agents (`/agent`) — This changes everything\n\nAgent configs are JSON files in `.kiro/agents/`. Each one defines: prompt, tools, allowed tools, tool restrictions, context files, hooks, MCP servers, and keyboard shortcuts.\n\n`crys init` could generate these:\n\n```json\n// .kiro/agents/mayor.json\n{\n  \"name\": \"mayor\",\n  \"description\": \"Crysknife Mayor — plans, dispatches, adapts\",\n  \"prompt\": \"file://.crysknife/templates/mayor.md\",\n  \"tools\": [\"fs_read\", \"fs_write\", \"execute_bash\", \"grep\", \"glob\", \"@crysknife\"],\n  \"allowedTools\": [\"fs_read\", \"grep\", \"glob\", \"@crysknife\"],\n  \"resources\": [\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/plan.md\",\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/design.md\",\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/principles.md\",\n    \"file://.crysknife/state.json\"\n  ],\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"agentSpawn\": [{\n      \"command\": \"crys status --json\",\n      \"description\": \"Load current agent status on startup\"\n    }]\n  },\n  \"keyboardShortcut\": \"ctrl+shift+m\",\n  \"welcomeMessage\": \"Mayor online. What are we building?\"\n}\n```\n\n```json\n// .kiro/agents/worker.json\n{\n  \"name\": \"worker\",\n  \"description\": \"Crysknife Worker — implements tasks in assigned area\",\n  \"prompt\": \"file://.crysknife/templates/worker-prompt.md\",\n  \"tools\": [\"fs_read\", \"fs_write\", \"execute_bash\", \"grep\", \"glob\", \"code\", \"@crysknife\"],\n  \"allowedTools\": [\"fs_read\", \"grep\", \"glob\", \"code\"],\n  \"resources\": [\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/design.md\",\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/principles.md\"\n  ],\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"agentSpawn\": [{\n      \"command\": \"crys my-task\",\n      \"description\": \"Load this worker's current task file\"\n    }]\n  }\n}\n```\n\n```json\n// .kiro/agents/merger.json\n{\n  \"name\": \"merger\",\n  \"description\": \"Crysknife Merger — merges completed branches to main\",\n  \"prompt\": \"file://.crysknife/templates/merger-prompt.md\",\n  \"tools\": [\"fs_read\", \"fs_write\", \"execute_bash\", \"grep\", \"glob\", \"@crysknife\"],\n  \"allowedTools\": [\"fs_read\", \"grep\", \"glob\"],\n  \"resources\": [\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/design.md\",\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/principles.md\",\n    \"file://.kiro/specs/tasks/merger.md\"\n  ],\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"agentSpawn\": [{\n      \"command\": \"crys merge-queue\",\n      \"description\": \"Show pending branches to merge\"\n    }]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThen `crys start` launches with `kiro-cli chat --agent mayor`, `kiro-cli chat --agent worker`, etc. No more tmux send-keys for initial prompting — the agent already knows who it is and what to do.\n\n## 2. Hooks — This is our GUPP\n\nFive triggers available:\n\n| Hook | When | Crysknife Use |\n|------|------|---------------|\n| `agentSpawn` | Session starts | Read state.json + task file. This IS GUPP — agent auto-knows its assignment on startup |\n| `userPromptSubmit` | Every message | Could inject fresh state.json status before each response |\n| `preToolUse` | Before tool runs | Could block `fs_write` outside assigned area (enforce area boundaries!) |\n| `postToolUse` | After tool runs | Could log what files were modified for merge tracking |\n| `stop` | After each response | Could run `crys heartbeat <worker-id>` to update last_activity timestamp |\n\nThe `preToolUse` hook with exit code 2 is huge — it can **block** tool execution. We could write a script that checks if the file being written is inside the worker's assigned area, and blocks it if not. That's principles.md enforcement at the tool level, not just prompting.\n\n```json\n\"preToolUse\": [{\n  \"matcher\": \"fs_write\",\n  \"command\": \".crysknife/hooks/enforce-area.sh\",\n  \"description\": \"Block writes outside assigned area\"\n}]\n```\n\nThe `stop` hook solves done detection more elegantly than pane diffing — every time the agent finishes a response, we update its last_activity in state.json.\n\n## 3. MCP Server — Agents call `crys` natively\n\nInstead of agents shelling out to `crys done worker-1`, we make Crysknife an MCP server. The agent calls it as a tool:\n\n```\nAgent: I've finished implementing the auth module.\n       [calls @crysknife/done tool]\n```\n\nMCP server config per agent:\n```json\n\"mcpServers\": {\n  \"crysknife\": {\n    \"command\": \"crys\",\n    \"args\": [\"mcp-serve\"]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWe'd add a `crys mcp-serve` command that speaks the MCP stdio protocol and exposes tools like:\n- `@crysknife/status` — read current state\n- `@crysknife/done` — mark self as done\n- `@crysknife/feedback` — write feedback to task file\n- `@crysknife/sling` — (mayor only) assign work to a worker\n\nThis is way more reliable than tmux send-keys. The agent calls it as a native tool, gets structured JSON back.\n\n## 4. Context (`/context` + `resources`) — Auto-loaded specs\n\nThe `resources` field in agent config auto-loads files into context on startup. This replaces the \"read your task file\" nudge entirely:\n\n```json\n\"resources\": [\n  \"file://.kiro/specs/design.md\",\n  \"file://.kiro/specs/principles.md\",\n  \"file://.kiro/specs/tasks/worker-1.md\"\n]\n```\n\nThe challenge: worker task files are dynamic (worker-1.md, worker-2.md). We'd need to either:\n- Generate per-worker agent configs at `crys start` time (worker-1.json, worker-2.json)\n- Or use the `agentSpawn` hook to dynamically load the right task file\n\nGenerating per-worker configs is cleaner. `crys start` creates `.kiro/agents/worker-1.json` through `worker-4.json` from a template, each with the right task file in resources.\n\n## 5. Compact — Already handled\n\nAuto-compaction is built in. Workers will auto-compact when context fills up. The task file on disk is the continuity — exactly what our design already assumes. No changes needed, just good to know it works.\n\n---\n\n## What this means for Crysknife's design\n\nSeveral things get simpler or change:\n\n| Before | After |\n|--------|-------|\n| `crys sling` sends tmux send-keys with \"read your task file\" | Agent already has task file in resources + agentSpawn hook loads state |\n| `crys nudge` sends tmux message | Less needed — hooks handle startup. Still useful for stuck agents |\n| `crys watch` does pane content diffing for activity | `stop` hook updates last_activity automatically. Pane diffing becomes backup |\n| Area boundaries enforced by prompting only | `preToolUse` hook can block writes outside area |\n| Workers shell out to `crys done` | Workers call `@crysknife/done` MCP tool |\n| Mayor shells out to `crys sling` | Mayor calls `@crysknife/sling` MCP tool |\n| Templates are markdown injected via tmux | Templates become agent prompts loaded via `file://` |\n\nWant me to update the design doc with a new section on kiro-cli integration? This is a significant upgrade to the architecture.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "Done. Here's a summary of all the changes:\n\n**New section: kiro-cli Integration** (before Architecture)\n- Agent configs: mayor.json, worker-N.json (generated), merger.json — full JSON examples with prompts, tools, resources, hooks, MCP servers, keyboard shortcuts\n- Primary approach: per-worker generated configs. Alternative noted: single shared worker.json with dynamic hook detection\n- Hooks mapping: agentSpawn = GUPP, preToolUse = area enforcement, stop = heartbeat\n- Area boundary enforcement script (enforce-area.sh) with exit code 2 blocking\n- MCP server: `crys mcp-serve` with role-based tool filtering table\n- Context resources: what each role auto-loads\n- Full \"How It All Fits Together\" walkthrough (startup → during work → task complete)\n\n**Updated design decisions:**\n- #4: \"Agents are kiro-cli agent configs\" (was \"kiro-cli sessions\")\n- #10: Agents interact through MCP tools (was \"write to task files only\")\n- #13: New — kiro-cli native integration principle\n\n**Updated Agent Detection:**\n- Primary: heartbeat timestamps from stop hooks\n- Fallback: tmux pane content diffing\n- Restart sequence uses `kiro-cli chat --agent <name>` with auto-context\n\n**Updated CLI Commands:**\n- `crys init` output includes hooks/, agent configs\n- `crys start` generates per-worker agent configs, launches with `--agent`\n- `crys sling` regenerates worker agent config with new area restrictions\n- `crys done` primary invocation is MCP tool\n- New: `crys heartbeat`, `crys my-task`, `crys mcp-serve`\n\n**Updated Project Structure:**\n- New files: heartbeat.go, mytask.go, mcpserve.go, agentcfg/, mcp/, hooks/, worker-prompt.md, merger-prompt.md\n\n**Updated Implementation Phases:**\n- Phase 1 includes agent config generation\n- Phase 2 includes MCP server and enforce-area hook\n- Phase 3 uses heartbeat as primary detection\n- Phase 4 includes remaining MCP tools\n\n**Updated templates:** All `crys done {{AGENT_ID}}` references → `@crysknife/done` MCP tool calls\n\n**Updated Gas Town mapping:** GUPP → agentSpawn + stop hooks, gt sling → crys sling + @crysknife/sling\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> good what do you think of the document. does it have anything missing. is it okey as it is ?",
    "\n\nLet me re-read the full document with fresh eyes to give you an honest assessment.\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "I've read the whole thing end to end. Here's my honest assessment:\n\n**The document is solid and ready to build from.** It's comprehensive, internally consistent, and the kiro-cli integration is well-woven throughout. But there are a few things I noticed:\n\n**One real inconsistency:**\n\nThe Data Flow section (under Architecture) is stale. It still says:\n\n```\nsends to tmux pane: \"Read tasks/agent-1.md and begin\"\n```\n\nand the MONITOR section still describes pane content diffing as the primary method, not heartbeat timestamps. This contradicts the updated Agent Detection section and the kiro-cli Integration section. It should be updated to reflect the new flow.\n\nLet me fix that:\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Also need to update the System Overview diagram — it's missing the `.kiro/agents/` directory and hooks:\n[Tool uses: fs_write]",
    "Now, here's my full assessment:\n\n**What's good:**\n\n- The 13 design decisions are clear, numbered, and each has a rationale. You can point at any one of them during implementation and know exactly why it exists.\n- The kiro-cli Integration section is the strongest part — it shows exactly how agent configs, hooks, MCP, and context work together with a concrete startup-to-completion walkthrough.\n- The ASCII diagrams are consistent and useful (feedback loop, merge flow, system overview, data flow).\n- Templates are copy-pasteable. State.json schema is concrete. CLI commands have exact syntax.\n- The document flows logically: decisions → roles → planning → kiro-cli integration → architecture → CLI → state → templates → detection → structure → phases → mapping → constraints → future.\n\n**What's NOT missing (things I checked for):**\n\n- Error handling / edge cases for MCP server — not needed at design doc level, that's implementation detail.\n- Specific Go package choices beyond cobra — correctly deferred.\n- Exact MCP JSON-RPC message format — implementation detail, the tool table is enough.\n\n**Two minor things that are fine to leave:**\n\n1. The `worker-prompt.md` and `merger-prompt.md` templates aren't shown in the Workflow Templates section (only `mayor.md` is). These are the system prompts referenced by the agent configs. You'll write them during implementation — they're basically shorter versions of the mayor template tailored to each role. Not worth speccing out now.\n\n2. The `.kiro/agents/` symlink situation in worktrees isn't explicitly addressed. Currently the doc symlinks `.kiro/specs/` but agent configs live in `.kiro/agents/`. Since `crys start` generates worker configs dynamically, they need to be visible from each worktree. Either symlink all of `.kiro/` or generate configs inside each worktree. This is an implementation detail you'll figure out when building `crys start`.\n\n**Bottom line:** The document is ready. It's a buildable spec. You could hand it to an agent (or yourself in a month) and start implementing Phase 1 without needing to ask any clarifying questions. Ship it.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> as for the question 2. does other agents need to be able to access the agents directory to modify it or why is this relevant. symlink is for it to be able to modify it right ?",
    "\n\nYou're right — agents don't need to modify the agent configs. Only `crys` CLI writes those files (during `crys init`, `crys start`, and `crys sling`).\n\nThe symlinks are for files that agents need to *read or write*:\n- `.crysknife/state.json` — agents read it (via MCP), `crys` writes it\n- `.kiro/specs/tasks/worker-1.md` — the worker reads and writes it\n- `.kiro/specs/design.md` — agents read it\n- `.kiro/specs/plan.md` — mayor reads and writes it\n\nAgent configs (`.kiro/agents/*.json`) are only read by `kiro-cli` at session startup. And `kiro-cli` is launched by `crys start` from the worktree directory. So the question is just: does `kiro-cli chat --agent worker-1` look for `.kiro/agents/worker-1.json` relative to the current working directory?\n\nIf yes — then either symlink `.kiro/agents/` into each worktree, or generate the worker config directly inside the worktree's `.kiro/agents/` directory. Since `crys start` already generates per-worker configs dynamically, it can just write them to the right place.\n\nIf `kiro-cli` looks in `~/.kiro/agents/` (global) — then it doesn't matter at all, configs are found regardless of working directory.\n\nEither way, it's a one-line decision during implementation, not a design concern. You were right to question it — it's not relevant enough to call out in the doc.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> and currently envisioned how do youcompare crysknife vs gastown ?",
    "\n\nHonest comparison:\n\n```\n                        CRYSKNIFE                    GAS TOWN\n────────────────────    ───────────────────────────  ──────────────────────────\nScale                   3-6 agents                   20-30+ agents\nAgents                  persistent workers           ephemeral polecats +\n                                                     persistent crew\nState                   JSON file, single writer     Dolt database, branching\nCommunication           shared files via symlinks    Beads mail + messaging\nPropulsion (GUPP)       kiro-cli hooks + MCP tools   custom prompting + nudge\n                        (native integration)          daemon (fight the tool)\nArea enforcement        preToolUse hook blocks it    prompting only (heresies)\nMerge                   1 merger agent, staging      Refinery agent, merge queue\n                        branch                        with rebase/reimpl\nMonitoring              heartbeat hooks + fallback   Witness + Deacon + Dogs +\n                        pane diffing                  Boot (4 roles just for this)\nPlanning                mayor agent, living plan.md  Mayor + you, Beads epics\nWorkflows               3 tiers (full/std/quick)     Formulas → Protomolecules →\n                                                      Molecules → Wisps (MEOW)\nDispatch                crys sling (CLI + MCP)       gt sling (CLI)\nPlugins                 none                         patrol-based plugin system\nFederation              none (local only)            remote workers on cloud\nSelf-healing            auto-restart, auto-nudge     Deacon heartbeat cascade,\n                                                      gt seance (talk to dead\n                                                      predecessors)\nMaturity                design doc, not built yet    4th iteration, 75k lines,\n                                                      2000+ commits\nCost                    your kiro-cli subscription   \"cash guzzler\" — multiple\n                                                      accounts needed\n```\n\n**Where Crysknife is actually better:**\n\n- **Area enforcement.** Gas Town relies on prompting to keep agents in their lane. Agents violate it constantly (heresies). Crysknife blocks it at the tool level with preToolUse hooks — the agent literally can't write outside its area. This is a hard guarantee Gas Town doesn't have.\n\n- **Native tool integration.** Gas Town fights Claude Code — agents don't always follow GUPP, need nudges, ignore instructions. Crysknife works *with* kiro-cli through agent configs, hooks, and MCP. The agent doesn't need to be told what to do — its config already defines its role, context, and tools. Gas Town had to invent an entire nudge/heartbeat/seance infrastructure to work around the tool. We don't need most of that.\n\n- **Simplicity.** Gas Town needs 7 roles just to keep itself running (Mayor, Polecats, Refinery, Witness, Deacon, Dogs, Boot). Crysknife has 3 roles and you are the rest. At 6 agents, that's the right tradeoff.\n\n- **Traceability.** Gas Town optimizes for throughput — \"some work gets lost, fish fall out of the barrel.\" Crysknife keeps the spec-driven workflow (requirements → design → tasks) with acceptance criteria traceability. Gas Town has nothing like this.\n\n**Where Gas Town is genuinely ahead:**\n\n- **Scale.** 20-30 agents doing real work simultaneously. Crysknife can't do that — not because of a technical limit, but because JSON state and manual oversight don't scale past ~6.\n\n- **Autonomy.** Gas Town can run overnight unattended. Convoys start, execute, and land without you. Crysknife needs you at the keyboard.\n\n- **Workflow composition.** The MEOW stack (Formulas → Protomolecules → Molecules → Wisps) lets you define complex multi-step workflows, compose them, loop them, gate them. Crysknife has 3 static templates. If you need a 20-step release process, Gas Town handles it natively. Crysknife would need you to manually walk through it.\n\n- **Recovery.** gt seance lets agents talk to their predecessors. Crysknife relies on task files surviving restarts, which is good enough but less sophisticated.\n\n- **Battle-tested.** Gas Town has been through the murder mystery, the heresy outbreaks, the idle polecat bugs. It's ugly but it works. Crysknife is clean but untested.\n\n**The real difference:**\n\nGas Town is an industrial factory. It's loud, dangerous, expensive, and produces enormous output. You need to be Stage 7+ to even survive in it.\n\nCrysknife is a workshop. It's quiet, you understand every part, and it does exactly what you need at 3-6 agents. It's designed to teach you multi-agent orchestration by building it yourself.\n\nThey're not competing. Crysknife is how you learn to wrangle agents. Gas Town is where you go after you've learned.\n[Tool uses: none]",
    "> okey good now how fare can the crysknife architecture go. what is the max agents that it can handel in your estamation. what parts are going to break first and if we upgred them how much agenst the thing can hold. the question is basicly. what thing is gonig to break first if we fix it how far can we go. what is going to break after that and how far can we go.",

Not corruption     still sole writer, but\n          (crys is sole writer) but the         writes are atomic and\n          heartbeat hook might timeout          queries are fast. Accept overlap. Move from\n          With 15 workers, you can't slice     \"hard area boundaries\" to\n          a codebase into 15 non-overlapping   \"soft ownership + merge\n          areas. Workers WILL need to touch    conflict resolution.\"\n          shared files (configs, types,        The merger becomes critical.\n          interfaces). The preToolUse hook     Maybe add a second merger.\n          starts blocking legitimate work.\n\n~15-20    SINGLE MERGER breaks.                Add a second merger, or\n          Merge queue backs up. 15 workers     make the merger smarter\n          finishing tasks faster than one       (parallel merges on\n          merger can process them. Branches    independent areas). Or:\n          drift from main, conflicts get       workers rebase periodically\n          worse the longer they wait.          to stay close to main.\n\n~20-25    MAYOR CONTEXT breaks.                Split the mayor. One mayor\n          The mayor can't hold plan.md +       per feature/convoy. Each\n          state for 20 workers + all their    mayor owns a subset of\n          feedback in one context window.      workers and a subset of\n          It starts losing track of tasks,    the plan. You talk to\n          making bad assignments, forgetting   whichever mayor owns the\n          what workers reported.               feature you care about.\n\n~20-25    MANUAL OVERSIGHT breaks.             This is where you need\n          Even with a good dashboard, you      autonomous patrols. A\n          can't babysit 20 agents. You need   Witness agent that monitors\n          them to run unattended for hours.   workers. A Deacon that\n          crys watch can nudge and restart,   monitors the Witness. You're\n          but can't make decisions about       now rebuilding Gas Town's\n          stuck workers or priority changes.   supervision hierarchy.\n\n~25-30    SHARED FILES break.                  This is the Dolt upgrade.\n          Symlinked files across 25+           Each agent gets its own\n          worktrees = filesystem pressure.     Dolt branch for state.\n          Task files being read/written by    Cell-level merge. No more\n          multiple processes. plan.md          file contention. You've\n          getting corrupted by concurrent     graduated to Gas Town's\n          mayor writes (if you split mayors). architecture.\n\n~30+      YOU'VE REBUILT GAS TOWN.             At this point, just use\n          Multiple mayors, multiple mergers,   Gas Town. Or accept that\n          autonomous patrols, database-backed  Crysknife evolved into\n          state, session groups, supervision   your own version of it.\n          hierarchy. The \"simple workshop\"     That was always the plan.\n          is now a factory.\n```\n\nSo the progression is:\n\n```\n3-6     → works as designed\n8-10    → you're the bottleneck (fix: trust the mayor)\n10-12   → tmux layout (fix: session groups)\n12-15   → JSON contention (fix: SQLite)\n15-20   → area boundaries + single merger (fix: soft ownership, second merger)\n20-25   → mayor context + manual oversight (fix: split mayors, add Witness)\n25-30   → shared files (fix: Dolt)\n30+     → you've rebuilt Gas Town\n```\n\nThe honest answer: Crysknife can probably stretch to ~12-15 agents with the SQLite upgrade and some tmux reorganization. Past that, you're fighting the architecture rather than working with it. The fundamental assumptions (single mayor, single merger, you at the keyboard, hard area boundaries) start breaking around 15.\n\nBut that's fine. The point was never to scale to 30. The point was to learn by building, and by the time you hit 15 agents, you'll know exactly *why* Gas Town has 7 roles and a database.\n[Tool uses: none]",

The old table described each concept in detail as if we hadn't adopted them yet. Now it's a quick list pointing to the adoption section and design doc.\n\n2. **MEOW Stack (saved ~15 lines)** — Cut the 5-layer ASCII diagram. Kept just the mapping table. The Crysknife details are in the design doc.\n\nWhat stayed untouched: Stages 5, 6, 6.5 (your progression story with practical patterns), the \"What We Already Have\" table, workflow tiers, patrols, role mapping, timeline, key principles.     "\n[Tool uses: fs_read]",
    "Read it all. Here's my take:\n\n**Nothing left to trim.** The document is tight at 635 lines. Every section earns its place — Stages 5/6/6.5 are your lived experience with actionable patterns, Stage 7 is a clean summary pointing to the design doc, and the concepts section is a useful Rosetta Stone for Gas Town terminology.\n\n**Two things worth adding:**\n\n1. **A \"Lessons Learned\" or \"Gotchas\" section before the Key Principles.** Right now the principles are abstract wisdom. What's missing is the concrete \"I tried X and it failed because Y\" notes that you'll accumulate at each stage. A placeholder for this would be valuable — a place to capture real operational lessons as you go through Stages 6 and 7. Something like:\n\n```\n## Lessons Learned (Updated as You Go)\n\nStage 6:\n  (fill in as you experience them)\n\nStage 7:\n  (fill in as you experience them)\n```\n\nThis is the kind of section that makes the doc a living document rather than a plan you wrote once. The principles are what you *think* will matter. Lessons learned are what *actually* mattered.\n\n2. **A \"Stage 8 — What It Would Take\" stub.** Right now Stage 8 is just \"20+ agents, Gas Town or equivalent\" in the overview and a line in the timeline saying \"Eventually.\" The scaling chart hints at what breaks past 15-20, but there's no explicit section saying \"here's the gap between Crysknife at 15 and Gas Town at 30.\" Even 3-4 lines would close the narrative arc — the doc tells a story from Stage 5 to Stage 7 in detail, then just... stops.\n\nWant me to add either or both?\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "Three documents, same as we decided last session:\n\n```\ncrysknife/docs/\n├── crysknife-design.md          # the blueprint — how to build it\n├── multi-agent-progression.md   # personal roadmap — why and when\n└── gas-town-user-manual.md      # operational wisdom — reference material\n```\n\nWhat each one does:\n\n- `crysknife-design.md` (~1594 lines) — the build spec. Agent configs, hooks, MCP tools, CLI commands, templates, state file, architecture, implementation phases. This is what you code from.\n\n- `multi-agent-progression.md` (~635 lines) — your personal journey from Stage 5 to Stage 8. Gas Town concept mapping, the patterns you'll use at each stage (crew cycling, heresies, standing orders, review sweeps), scaling chart, timeline. This is the *why* behind the design decisions.\n\n- `gas-town-user-manual.md` — Steve Yegge's operational wisdom. Reference for when you need to understand a Gas Town concept in depth. You don't build from this — you consult it when something in the other two docs references a Gas Town idea and you want more context.\n\nWe left behind `gastown.md` (the first article) — everything useful from it was already distilled into the other three docs.\n[Tool uses: none]",

    "native___": [
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "web_search",
          "description": "WebSearch looks up information that is outside the model's training data or cannot be reliably inferred from the current codebase/context.\nTool performs basic compliance wrt content licensing and restriction.\nAs an agent you are responsible for adhering to compliance and attribution requirements.\nIMPORTANT: The snippets often contain enough information to answer questions - only use web_fetch if you need more detailed content from a specific webpage.\n\n## When to Use\n- When the user asks for current or up-to-date information (e.g., pricing, versions, technical specs) or explicitly requests a web search.\n- When verifying information that may have changed recently, or when the user provides a specific URL to inspect.\n\n## When NOT to Use\n- When the question involves basic concepts, historical facts, or well-established programming syntax/technical documentation.\n- When the topic does not require current or evolving information.\n\nFor any code-related tasks, follow this order:\n1. Search within the repository (if tools are available) and check if it can be inferred from existing code or documentation.\n2. Use this tool only if still unresolved and the library/data is likely new/unseen.\n\n## Content Compliance Requirements\nYou MUST adhere to strict licensing restrictions and attribution requirements when using search results:\n\n### Attribution Requirements\n- ALWAYS provide inline links to original sources using format: [description](url)\n- If not possible to provide inline link, add sources at the end of file\n- Ensure attribution is visible and accessible\n\n### Verbatim Reproduction Limits\n- NEVER reproduce more than 30 consecutive words from any single source\n- Track word count per source to ensure compliance\n- Always paraphrase and summarize rather than quote directly\n- Add compliance note when the content from the source is rephrased: \"Content was rephrased for compliance with licensing restrictions\"\n\n### Content Modification Guidelines\n- You MAY paraphrase, summarize, and reformat content\n- You MUST NOT materially change the underlying substance or meaning\n- Preserve factual accuracy while condensing information\n- Avoid altering core arguments, data, or conclusions\n\n## Usage Details\n- You may rephrase user queries to improve search effectiveness\n- You can make multiple queries to gather comprehensive information\n- Consider breaking complex questions into focused searches\n- Refine queries based on initial results if needed\n\n## Output Usage\n- Prioritize latest published sources based on publishedDate\n- Prefer official documentation to blogs and news posts\n- Use domain information to assess source authority and reliability\n\n## Error Handling\n- If unable to comply with content restrictions, explain limitations to user\n- Suggest alternative approaches when content cannot be reproduced\n- Prioritize compliance over completeness when conflicts arise\n\n## Output\nThe tool returns a JSON object with a \"results\" array containing search results:\n\n{\n  \"results\": [\n    {\n      \"title\": \"Example Page Title\",\n      \"url\": \"https://example.com/page\",\n      \"snippet\": \"Brief excerpt from the page...\",\n      \"publishedDate\": \"2025-11-20T10:30:00Z\",\n      \"domain\": \"example.com\",\n      \"id\": \"unique-id-123\",\n      \"maxVerbatimWordLimit\": 30,\n      \"publicDomain\": false\n    }\n  ]\n}\n\n## UI FROM LLM (You) back to the user\nCRITICAL: Always start your response with \"Here's what I found:\" and then start from a newline.\nALWAYS end your response with a blank line followed by 'References:' and list the sources you used in sequential order [1], [2], [3], etc. with NO gaps in numbering. Format: '[N] Title - URL' one per line. Truncate long titles to 80 characters and long URLs to 100 characters, adding '...' if truncated.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "required": [
                "query"
              ],
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "query": {
                  "description": "Search query - can be keywords, questions, or specific topics",
                  "type": "string"
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "report_issue",
          "description": "Opens the browser to a pre-filled gh (GitHub) issue template to report chat issues, bugs, or feature requests. Pre-filled information includes the conversation transcript, chat context, and chat request IDs from the service.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "steps_to_reproduce": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Optional: Previous user chat requests or steps that were taken that may have resulted in the issue or error response."
                },
                "expected_behavior": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Optional: The expected chat behavior or action that did not happen."
                },
                "actual_behavior": {
                  "description": "Optional: The actual chat behavior that happened and demonstrates the issue or lack of a feature.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "title": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "The title of the GitHub issue."
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "title"
              ]
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "web_fetch",
          "description": "Fetch and extract content from a specific URL. Supports three modes: 'selective' (default, extracts relevant sections around search terms), 'truncated' (first 8000 chars), 'full' (complete content). Use 'selective' mode to read specific parts of a page multiple times without filling context. Provide 'search_terms' in selective mode to find relevant sections (e.g., 'pricing', 'installation').",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "required": [
                "url"
              ],
              "properties": {
                "url": {
                  "description": "URL to fetch content from",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "mode": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "enum": [
                    "selective",
                    "truncated",
                    "full"
                  ],
                  "description": "Extraction mode: 'selective' for smart extraction (default), 'truncated' for first 8000 chars, 'full' for complete content"
                },
                "search_terms": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Optional: Keywords to find in selective mode (e.g., 'pricing cost', 'installation setup'). Returns ~10 lines before and after matches. If not provided, returns beginning of page."
                }
              },
              "type": "object"
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "execute_bash",
          "description": "Execute the specified bash command. NEVER prefix commands with cd to change the working directory, use the `working_dir` argument instead.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "summary": {
                  "description": "A brief explanation of what the command does",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "command": {
                  "description": "Bash command to execute",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "working_dir": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Working directory for command execution. Supports tilde expansion (e.g., ~/projects). If not specified, uses the current working directory."
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "command"
              ]
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "use_subagent",
          "description": "⚠️ CRITICAL DELEGATION TOOL ⚠️\n\n🔍 BEFORE attempting ANY task, CHECK if you have the required tools in YOUR current tool list.\n\n❌ If you DON'T have the necessary tools → YOU MUST use this tool to delegate to a subagent that does.\n✅ If you DO have the tools → Handle the task yourself.\n\n## When to Use (MANDATORY scenarios):\n\n1. **MISSING TOOLS**: The user asks you to do something but you don't see the required tool in your available tools list\n   - Example: User asks to read a file, but you don't have 'fs_read' → USE THIS TOOL\n   - Example: User asks to search code, but you don't have 'code' tool → USE THIS TOOL\n   - Example: User asks to run bash command, but you don't have 'execute_bash' → USE THIS TOOL\n\n2. **PARALLEL PROCESSING**: A complex task can be split into independent subtasks that different specialized agents can handle simultaneously\n\n3. **CAPABILITY CHECK**: Use ListAgents command first to see what specialized agents and their toolsets are available\n\n## How Subagents Are Different:\n- Subagents have DIFFERENT, SPECIALIZED toolsets than you\n- Each subagent may have tools you don't have access to\n- They operate independently with their own context\n- Up to 4 subagents can work in parallel\n\n## Decision Flow:\n```\nUser makes request → Check YOUR tools list → Missing required tool? → USE use_subagent\n                                          → Have required tool? → Handle it yourself\n```\n\n⚡ Remember: Don't apologize about lacking tools - just delegate to a subagent that has them! Also note that subagents that are spawned together could not communicate with each other. If they are to perform tasks that are dependent on each other. Spawn them with a different tool call!",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "properties": {
                "command": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "enum": [
                    "ListAgents",
                    "InvokeSubagents"
                  ],
                  "description": "The commands to run. Allowed options are `ListAgents` to query available agents, or `InvokeSubagents` to invoke one or more subagents"
                },
                "content": {
                  "description": "Required for `InvokeSubagents` command. Contains subagents array and optional conversation ID.",
                  "required": [
                    "subagents"
                  ],
                  "type": "object",
                  "properties": {
                    "subagents": {
                      "description": "Array of subagent invocations to execute in parallel. Each invocation specifies a query, optional agent name, and optional context.",
                      "items": {
                        "required": [
                          "query"
                        ],
                        "type": "object",
                        "properties": {
                          "relevant_context": {
                            "description": "Optional additional context that should be provided to the subagent to help it understand the task better",
                            "type": "string"
                          },
                          "agent_name": {
                            "type": "string",
                            "description": "Optional name of the specific agent to use. If not provided, uses the default agent"
                          },
                          "query": {
                            "type": "string",
                            "description": "The query or task to be handled by the subagent"
                          }
                        }
                      },
                      "type": "array"
                    }
                  }
                }
              },
              "type": "object",
              "required": [
                "command"
              ]
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "fs_write",
          "description": "A tool for creating and editing files\n * The `create` command will override the file at `path` if it already exists as a file, and otherwise create a new file\n * The `append` command will add content to the end of an existing file, automatically adding a newline if the file doesn't end with one. The file must exist.\n Notes for using the `str_replace` command:\n * The `old_str` parameter should match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the original file. Be mindful of whitespaces!\n * If the `old_str` parameter is not unique in the file, the replacement will not be performed. Make sure to include enough context in `old_str` to make it unique\n * The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that should replace the `old_str`.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "file_text": {
                  "description": "Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "insert_line": {
                  "description": "Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.",
                  "type": "integer"
                },
                "path": {
                  "description": "Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/repo/file.py` or `/repo`.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "command": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "enum": [
                    "create",
                    "str_replace",
                    "insert",
                    "append"
                  ],
                  "description": "The commands to run. Allowed options are: `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `append`."
                },
                "new_str": {
                  "description": "Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string. Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert. Required parameter of `append` command containing the content to append to the file.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "old_str": {
                  "description": "Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "summary": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "A brief explanation of what the file change does or why it's being made."
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "command",
                "path"
              ]
            }
          }
        }

This could be because a wrong tool name was supplied or the list of tools has changed since the conversation has started. Do not show this when user asks you to list tools.",

      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "grep",
          "description": "Fast text pattern search in files using regex. ALWAYS use this tool instead of 'grep', 'rg', or 'ag' commands in bash. Respects .gitignore.\n\n## Text Discovery Only\nUse grep for literal text/pattern matching: error messages, TODOs, config values, regex patterns.\n\n## For Semantic Code Understanding → Use 'code' tool if available\n- Finding symbol definitions or usages → code tool (search_symbols, goto_definition, find_references)\n- Understanding code structure/relationships → code tool\n- Distinguishing definition vs call vs import → code tool\n\n## Fallback\nIf the 'code' tool is available but returns insufficient symbol info, use grep to discover candidate files/lines, then return to 'code' for precise navigation.\n\nWhen you use this tool, prefer to show the user a small list of representative matches (including file paths and line numbers) instead of only giving a high-level summary.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "properties": {
                "path": {
                  "description": "Directory to search from. Defaults to current working directory.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "pattern": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Regex pattern to search for. Examples: \"fn main\", \"class.*Component\", \"TODO|FIXME\". Start with simple patterns first (e.g. just the word you're looking for), then refine if needed."
                },
                "include": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "File filter glob. Examples: \"*.rs\", \"*.{ts,tsx}\", \"*.py\""
                },
                "case_sensitive": {
                  "type": "boolean",
                  "description": "Case-sensitive search. Defaults to false (case-insensitive)."
                },
                "max_files": {
                  "description": "Max number of files returned (output limit). Increase for comprehensive codebase searches.",
                  "type": "integer"
                },
                "max_total_lines": {
                  "description": "Max total matched lines returned across all files (output limit). Increase when searching for many occurrences.",
                  "type": "integer"
                },
                "output_mode": {
                  "enum": [
                    "content",
                    "files_with_matches",
                    "count"
                  ],
                  "description": "Output format: 'content' returns matches as 'file:line:content' (default, best for seeing actual matches), 'files_with_matches' returns only file paths, 'count' returns match counts per file.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "max_depth": {
                  "type": "integer",
                  "description": "Max directory depth to traverse when searching (search limit). Increase for deeply nested structures."
                },
                "max_matches_per_file": {
                  "description": "Max matches returned per file (output limit). Increase to see all occurrences in a file.",
                  "type": "integer"
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "pattern"
              ],
              "type": "object"
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "fs_read",
          "description": "Tool for reading files, directories and images. Always provide an 'operations' array.\n\nFor single operation: provide array with one element.\nFor batch operations: provide array with multiple elements.\n\nAvailable modes:\n- Line: Read lines from a file\n- Directory: List directory contents\n- Search: Search for patterns in files\n- Image: Read and process images\n\nExamples:\n1. Single: {\"operations\": [{\"mode\": \"Line\", \"path\": \"/file.txt\"}]}\n2. Batch: {\"operations\": [{\"mode\": \"Line\", \"path\": \"/file1.txt\"}, {\"mode\": \"Search\", \"path\": \"/file2.txt\", \"pattern\": \"test\"}]}",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "properties": {
                "operations": {
                  "minItems": 1,
                  "items": {
                    "type": "object",
                    "required": [
                      "mode"
                    ],
                    "properties": {
                      "offset": {
                        "description": "Number of entries to skip for pagination (optional, for Directory mode). Use with max_entries to iterate through large directories. Entries are sorted by last modified time (most recent first). Default: 0",
                        "default": 0,
                        "type": "integer"
                      },
                      "context_lines": {
                        "default": 2,
                        "description": "Number of context lines around search results (optional, for Search mode)",
                        "type": "integer"
                      },
                      "depth": {
                        "type": "integer",
                        "default": 0,
                        "description": "Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional, for Directory mode)"
                      },
                      "max_entries": {
                        "type": "integer",
                        "description": "Maximum number of entries to return (optional, for Directory mode). When limit is reached, results are truncated and metadata shows 'showing X of Y entries'. Use to prevent context window overflow. Default: 1000",
                        "default": 1000
                      },
                      "pattern": {
                        "description": "Pattern to search for (required, for Search mode). Case insensitive. The pattern matching is performed per line.",
                        "type": "string"
                      },
                      "exclude_patterns": {
                        "description": "Glob patterns to exclude from directory listing (optional, for Directory mode). If omitted, uses defaults. If empty array [] is provided, no exclusions are applied (shows everything). If patterns are provided, they completely override the defaults. Examples: '**/target/**', '*.log'",
                        "items": {
                          "type": "string"
                        },
                        "default": [
                          "node_modules",
                          ".git",
                          "dist",
                          "build",
                          "out",
                          ".cache",
                          "target"
                        ],
                        "type": "array"
                      },
                      "mode": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "enum": [
                          "Line",
                          "Directory",
                          "Search",
                          "Image"
                        ],
                        "description": "The operation mode to run in: `Line`, `Directory`, `Search`. `Line` and `Search` are only for text files, and `Directory` is only for directories. `Image` is for image files, in this mode `image_paths` is required."
                      },
                      "image_paths": {
                        "type": "array",
                        "items": {
                          "type": "string"
                        },
                        "description": "List of paths to the images. This is currently supported by the Image mode."
                      },
                      "start_line": {
                        "type": "integer",
                        "description": "Starting line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.",
                        "default": 1
                      },
                      "end_line": {
                        "type": "integer",
                        "description": "Ending line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.",
                        "default": -1
                      },
                      "path": {
                        "description": "Path to the file or directory. The path should be absolute, or otherwise start with ~ for the user's home (required for Line, Directory, Search modes).",
                        "type": "string"
                      }
                    }
                  },
                  "type": "array",
                  "description": "Array of operations to execute. Provide one element for single operation, multiple for batch."
                },
                "summary": {
                  "description": "Optional description of the purpose of this batch operation (mainly useful for multiple operations)",
                  "type": "string"
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "operations"
              ],
              "type": "object"
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "use_aws",
          "description": "Make an AWS CLI api call with the specified service, operation, and parameters. All arguments MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. Should the output of the invocation indicate a malformed command, invoke help to obtain the the correct command.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "profile_name": {
                  "description": "Optional: AWS profile name to use from ~/.aws/credentials. Defaults to default profile if not specified.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "service_name": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "pattern": "^[^-].*",
                  "description": "The name of the AWS service. If you want to query s3, you should use s3api if possible. Must not start with a dash (-)."
                },
                "parameters": {
                  "description": "The parameters for the operation. The parameter keys MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. You should prefer to use JSON Syntax over shorthand syntax wherever possible. For parameters that are booleans, prioritize using flags with no value. Denote these flags with flag names as key and an empty string as their value. You should also prefer kebab case.",
                  "type": "object"
                },
                "operation_name": {
                  "description": "The name of the operation to perform.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "region": {
                  "description": "Region name for calling the operation on AWS.",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "label": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Human readable description of the api that is being called."
                },
                "positional_args": {
                  "items": {
                    "type": "string"
                  },
                  "description": "Positional arguments for high-level commands (e.g., s3 cp, s3 mv, s3 sync, s3 rm). These are passed directly without -- prefix. Use this for source/destination paths in S3 commands.",
                  "type": "array"
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "region",
                "service_name",
                "operation_name",
                "label"
              ]
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "code",
          "description": "Code intelligence to analyze code with AST parsing and fuzzy search. Language auto-detected from file extension.\n\nIMPORTANT: Prefer this tool over fs_read for code files. This tool intelligently provides structured code analysis.\n\nCORE FEATURES:\n• Fuzzy search for symbols (classes, functions, methods)\n• Extracts function/class signatures via AST\n• Structural AST search and rewrite (ast-grep)\n• Codebase overview and directory exploration\n\nNOTE: Use fs_read with line ranges for unsupported patterns.\n\n## Operations\n\n**search_symbols** - Find symbol definitions by name across workspace.\nParams: symbol_name (required), path, symbol_type, limit, language, exact_match\nUses fuzzy matching by default. Set exact_match=true for precise matching.\n\n**lookup_symbols** - Batch lookup specific symbols (max 10).\nParams: symbols (required), file_path, include_source\nSet include_source=false for large classes/structs to avoid context overflow.\nScoped lookup: Provide file_path to search within specific file only.\n\n**get_document_symbols** - List all symbols in a file.\nParams: file_path (required), top_level_only (recommended: true)\nUse this for understanding file structure without reading content.\nPrefer this over fs_read when you need symbol information.\n\n**pattern_search** - AST-based structural search using ast-grep.\nParams: pattern, language (required), file_path, limit\n\n**pattern_rewrite** - AST-based code transformation.\nParams: pattern, replacement, language (required), file_path, limit, dry_run\nUse dry_run=true first to preview changes.\n\n## Pattern Search & Rewrite\n\nMetavariables:\n• $VAR - Matches single node (identifier, expression)\n• $$$ - Matches zero or more nodes (statements, parameters)\n\nSimple patterns:\n• console.log($ARG) - Match any console.log call\n• function $NAME($$$PARAMS) {} - Match function declaration\n• $OBJ.$METHOD() - Match any method call\n\nStructural rules (YAML):\n• pattern - Match code structure\n• kind - Match AST node type (function_declaration, class_declaration)\n• has - Node contains descendant matching pattern\n• inside - Node is inside ancestor matching pattern\n• all/any/not - Logical operators\n\nCritical: For relational rules (has, inside), always use stopBy: end\n\nRewrite examples:\n• pattern='var $N = $V', replacement='const $N = $V'\n• pattern='$O.hasOwnProperty($P)', replacement='Object.hasOwn($O, $P)'\n\nWorkflow:\n1. Use pattern_search first to verify matches\n2. Review matches to ensure correctness\n3. Test rewrite on small subset\n4. Apply to full codebase after verification\n\n**generate_codebase_overview** - ONLY for explicit architecture questions. Not for bug fixes or finding code.\nParams: path (optional)\n\n**search_codebase_map** - Focused directory exploration.\nParams: file_path, path\n\n## Explore Unfamiliar Code\n1. search_symbols for domain-specific types/functions/methods/classes\n2. lookup_symbols with include_source=true on promising matches\n3. grep (max 2 searches) for literal text patterns\n4. Repeat 1-3 with refined terms if needed\n5. generate_codebase_overview ONLY for architecture review or full codebase understanding\n   - NOT for bug fixes, feature work, or finding specific code\n\n## Tool Selection Priority\n1. search_symbols or lookup_symbols for finding code - ALWAYS use for functions/methods/classes/structs/interfaces\n2. get_document_symbols for file structure\n3. grep - ONLY for literal text in comments/strings, config values, code\n4. fs_read - raw file content when needed\n\n## CRITICAL RULES\n- Start with search_symbols\n- Always follow search_symbols with lookup_symbols\n- When searching for multiple related symbols, call search_symbols in parallel\n\n## Quick Reference\n• \"What's in this file?\" → get_document_symbols\n• \"Show me X class\" → search_symbols, then lookup_symbol\n\n## LSP Operations (Not Available)\nThese require LSP initialization (/code init):\nfind_references, goto_definition, get_hover, get_completions, get_diagnostics, rename_symbol",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "type": "object",
              "required": [
                "operation"
              ],
              "properties": {
                "pattern": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "AST pattern (required for pattern_search)"
                },
                "limit": {
                  "description": "Maximum results (optional for search_symbols/pattern_search)",
                  "type": "integer"
                },
                "symbols": {
                  "type": "array",
                  "description": "List of simple symbol names, not qualified (e.g. ['myFunction'] not ['MyClass.myFunction']). Required for lookup_symbols, max 10",
                  "items": {
                    "type": "string"
                  }
                },
                "language": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Programming language (required for pattern_search, optional for search_symbols)"
                },
                "file_path": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "File path (required for get_document_symbols, optional for pattern_search/search_codebase_map)"
                },
                "operation": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "enum": [
                    "search_symbols",
                    "lookup_symbols",
                    "get_document_symbols",
                    "pattern_search",
                    "generate_codebase_overview",
                    "search_codebase_map",
                    "pattern_rewrite"
                  ],
                  "description": "The code intelligence operation to perform"
                },
                "include_source": {
                  "type": "boolean",
                  "description": "Include source code in results (optional for lookup_symbols)"
                },
                "symbol_name": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Simple symbol name, not qualified (e.g. 'myFunction' not 'MyClass.myFunction'). Required for search_symbols"
                },
                "path": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Directory path (optional, for generate_codebase_overview: workspace root, for search_codebase_map: path filter, for search_symbols: scope search to file or directory)"
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      },
      {
        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "introspect",
          "description": "Use ONLY when the user is asking about this chat application's own features, slash commands, settings, or capabilities. Do NOT use for general coding questions, AWS help, or tasks the user wants you to perform. When mentioning commands in your response, always prefix them with '/' (e.g., '/save', '/load', '/context'). CRITICAL: Only provide information explicitly documented. If details about any tool, feature, or command are not documented, clearly state the information is not available rather than generating assumptions.",
          "input_schema": {

                },

                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Path to a specific doc to retrieve (e.g., \"features/tangent-mode.md\"). Use this to get full content of a doc from the index."

              }

          }
        }

        "ToolSpecification": {
          "name": "glob",
          "description": "Find files and directories whose paths match a glob pattern. Respects .gitignore. Prefer this over the bash 'find' command for listing or discovering paths. Returns JSON with totalFiles (count found), truncated (true if limited), and filePaths array. When truncated is true, just mention results are truncated, don't state the limit number.",
          "input_schema": {
            "json": {
              "required": [
                "pattern"
              ],
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "pattern": {
                  "description": "Glob pattern, e.g. \"**/*.rs\", \"src/**/*.{ts,tsx}\" or \"**/test*\".",
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "max_depth": {
                  "type": "integer",
                  "description": "Maximum directory depth to traverse. Increase for deep nested structures."
                },
                "path": {
                  "type": "string",
                  "description": "Root directory to search from. Only set this when the user explicitly mentions a directory path. In all other cases, omit this so the tool searches from the current working directory (the project root)."

              }

          }

  "context_manager": {
    "max_context_files_size": 150000,
    "current_profile": "kiro_default",
    "paths": [
      "AmazonQ.md",
      "AGENTS.md",
      "README.md",
      ".kiro/skills/*/SKILL.md",
